entries.ocr_text:
FOREWORD
The 1954 General Assembly, through the enactment of Senate Bill
No. 140, imposed upon the Legislative Research Commission the duty
of furnishing the printer correct copies of bills and resolutions for in
clusion in the Session Acts. As this law did not become effective until
July 1, 1954, statutes in effect prior to that date placed the respon
sibility of furnishing copy to the printer upon the office of the Secretary
of State and the function of distribution in the Department of Library
and Archives. Section 57.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes stipu
lates that bound copies of Session Acts be delivered for distribution
within sixty days after the end of a regular session. Had this law been
complied with, the Legislative Research Commission would have had
no responsibility for the 1954 Session Acts.
This date limit is based on sound reasons. The vast majority of
legislation enacted by any session of the General Assembly becomes
effective as law ninety days following adjournment. Legislators, judges,
prosecuting attorneys and other interested citizens need to have copies
of the Session Acts in their possession prior to the date laws become
effective. Records in the Department of Library and Archives, how
ever, do not indicate that this deadline has ever been met. The most
prompt delivery in recent years has been some on hundred and eighty
days following the close of the session. In some instances the Session
Acts have not been available for distribution until approximately a
year after the close of a session.
In the preparation of this volume of the 1954 Session Ads the
office of the Secretary of State furnished the printer with a copy of the
enrolled bills and resolutions as filed in his office. The staff of the
Legislative Research Commission read and compared page proofs with
the enrolled bills. Every effort has been made by our staff to incor
porate in this volume a correct record of the laws passed by the 1954
General Assembly.
In the past no standard procedure has been followed in numbering
chapters of the Kentucky Acts. In some instances chapters have been
arranged by subject matter. Other Session Acts have had chapters in
the sequence of Senate and House bill numbers.
The staff of the Legislative Research Commission examined all
available session laws of other states and found that the numbering of
chapters according to date of approval was customary. The Commision,
meeting on March 2, 1954, recommended that this arrangement be used
for the 1954 Session Acts to expedite the work of the printer. The Sec
retary of State, however, declined to follow this recommendation in
furnishing copy to the printer.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 7

classification.title: FOREWORD to the 1954 Session Acts
classification.summary: This foreword explains the legislative process for publishing the 1954 Session Acts. It details the shift in responsibility for furnishing copies to the printer from the Secretary of State to the Legislative Research Commission, as mandated by Senate Bill No. 140, effective July 1, 1954. It also discusses the timeliness of publication, the historical lack of a standard chapter numbering procedure, and the Commission's recommendation to number chapters by date of approval, which was not followed by the Secretary of State.
classification.reasoning: The provided text is a foreword to the 1954 Session Acts, detailing administrative and procedural aspects of legislative publication. It discusses the roles of different state offices, the timeline for publishing laws, and changes in numbering conventions. There is no mention of race, nor any indication of discriminatory practices or laws targeting specific racial groups. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
Accordingly, acts in this volume are numbered beginning with the
lowest numbered Senate bill which passed and going on through the
remainder of Senate Bills, House Bills, Senate Resolutions, and House
Resolutions in that order. This method precluded furnishing any copy
to the printer prior to final executive action on all bills and resolutions.
The 48 resolutions follow the 250 bills.
The staff of the Legislative Research Commission recommends
that the system of assigning chapter numbers in the order of date of
approval by the Governor or their becoming law without signature be
followed in the future in order to expedite furnishing copy and type
setting and to comply with the time limit established by Section 57.180
of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Final editorial work on this volume has been performed under the
general supervision of Charles Wheeler, Research Editor. James
Fleming, Research Analyst, has contributed materially to this volume
by supervising the preparation of the comprehensive index, without
which the usefulness of any volume of the Session Acts would be
seriously impaired.
Though responsibility for the preparation of this volume has been
divided, a major goal of the Commission in future years will be to
publish the Acts and make them available to the public without the
unfortunate delay which has characterized this publication in previous
years.
ARTHUR Y. LLOYD
Frankfort, Ky. Director
July 26, 1954 Legislative Research Commission

ACTS OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY
OF THE
COMMONWEALTH OF KENTUCKY
PASSED AT THE REGULAR SESSION OF THE GENERAL ASSEM
BLY, WHICH WAS BEGUN IN THE CITY OF FRANKFORT, KEN
TUCKY, ON TUESDAY, JANUARY THE FIFTH, 1954, AND ENDED
FRIDAY, MARCH THE NINETEENTH, 1954.
CHAPTER 1
(S. B. 3)
AN ACT relating to annexation of territory by sanitation districts in
counties not containing a city of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. A new section of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
hereby created to read as follows:
When the board of directors of a district in a county not
containing a city of the first class desire to annex territory to the
district, it shall cause to be filed with the commissioner a petition
setting forth a reasonably accurate description of the territory
proposed to be annexed, together with a statement of the reasons
constituting the necessity for annexation of the territory. No
such petition shall be declared void on account of alleged defects,
but the commissioner may at any time permit amendments in
form and substance to conform to the facts, by correcting any
errors in the description of the territory or in any other particular.
Section 2. A new section of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
hereby created to read as follows:
(1) When a petition for annexation of territory to a dis
trict is filed with the commissioner, he shall investigate at once
the boundary of the territory proposed to be annexed, and may,
at the cost of the district, cause to be made surveys necessary to
establish with reasonable accuracy a boundary that will, in his
judgment, accomplish the purpose sought by annexation of the
territory in a practicable and workable manner, and that will
be sufficiently comprehensive to avoid confusion or interference
with any other similar district then existing or that may be
created. The boundary established by the commissioner need
not follow the boundary proposed by the district.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 22

classification.title: AN ACT relating to annexation of territory by sanitation districts in counties not containing a city of the first class.
classification.summary: This act creates a new section of Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning the annexation of territory by sanitation districts in counties that do not contain a city of the first class. It outlines the process for filing a petition for annexation, including the description of the territory and the reasons for annexation. The commissioner is empowered to investigate the proposed boundary, conduct surveys, and establish a boundary that serves the purpose of annexation in a practical and workable manner, potentially differing from the district's proposed boundary.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with the administrative process of annexation of territory by sanitation districts in specific types of counties. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. The focus is purely on the procedural aspects of district expansion. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:

( 2 ) Should it be found desirable to include in the territory
to be annexed by a district all or a portion of a city of the second,
third, fourth, fifth or sixth class, the governing body of such city
shall determine by ordinance whether the city or portion thereof
shall be included in the district, or whether the city shall bind
itself to pay the charges for the services of the district furnished
in such area.
(3) When the boundaries of the territory proposed to be
annexed have been fixed by the commissioner as prescribed in
subsection (1) of this section, he shall give notice of the applica
tion for annexation of the territory by posting a notice in at least
ten places in the territory proposed to be annexed, or by publish
ing it in at least two consecutive, regular editions of a newspaper
of general circulation in the territory proposed to be annexed.
Section 3. A new section of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
created to read as follows:
Any owner of real property in the territory proposed to
be annexed who wishes to object to the annexation of the territory
to the district shall, within sixty days after the giving of notice
by the commissioner, file his petition in the circuit court of the
county in which the larger part of the territory proposed to be
annexed is located, naming the commissioner defendant and
setting out in his petition his objections to the annexation of
the territory. The commissioner shall be represented in the
circuit court by the county attorney and in the Court of Appeals
by the Attorney-General, and he also may require his defense
to be made by the district. The issues may be made up and the
case may be docketed for hearing as in an action for a declaration
of rights. An appeal to the Court of Appeals may be taken in
the manner provided in the Rules of Civil Procedure, but such
appeals shall be docketed within sixty days from the entry of
judgment, unless the time be extended by the lower court, but
in no event beyond one hundred and twenty days from the entry
of judgment. The appeal may be advanced for immediate hear
ing and submission. The burden of proof shall be upon the
plaintiff to show cause why the territory, or any portion of
it,
should not be annexed to the district.
Ifthe court renders judg

the changes required for annexation of the territory, which,

this Act, but such new proceedings shall not be instituted for a

suspend the judgment until the case has been passed upon by the

CHAPTER 2
(S. B. 13)
AN ACT to amend and re-enact Section 145 of the Constitution of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky.
That upon the concurrence of three-fifths of all the members
elected to each House of the General Assembly, the ayes and
nays being taken thereon and entered in full in their respective
journals, Section 145 of the Constitution of the Commonwealth
of Kentucky, be amended, extended and reenacted so that as so
amended, extended and reenacted it will read as follows :
"145. QUALIFICATIONS OF VOTER. PERSONS DIS
FRANCHISED. Every citizen of the United States of the age
of eighteen years who has resided in the state one year, and in
the county six months, and the precinct in which he offers to
vote sixty days next preceding the election, shall be a voter
in said precinct and not elsewhere but the following persons
are excepted and shall not have the right to vote.
"1. Persons convicted in any court of competent jurisdic
tion of treason, or felony, or bribery in an election, or of such
high misdemeanor as the general assembly may declare shall
operate as an exclusion from the right of suffrage, but persons
hereby excluded may be restored to their civil rights by execu
tive pardon.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 24

classification.title: AN ACT to amend and re-enact Section 145 of the Constitution of the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 145 of the Kentucky Constitution regarding voter qualifications and disenfranchisement. It outlines the general requirements for voting (age, residency) and then lists exceptions, including persons convicted of treason, felony, bribery in an election, or "such high misdemeanor as the general assembly may declare shall operate as an exclusion from the right of suffrage." It also notes that such excluded persons may be restored to civil rights by executive pardon.
classification.reasoning: The statute amends Section 145 of the Kentucky Constitution, which deals with voter qualifications and disenfranchisement. While it does not explicitly mention race, the category of disenfranchisement, particularly for 'high misdemeanor' as declared by the General Assembly, was a common mechanism used in Jim Crow laws to disproportionately affect Black citizens' right to vote. The historical context of Jim Crow laws in Kentucky (1865-1966) strongly suggests that such broad discretionary powers granted to the General Assembly for disenfranchisement would have been applied in a racially discriminatory manner. Therefore, it is classified as a Jim Crow law with an implicit racial indicator. The confidence is high due to the direct amendment of a constitutional section related to voting rights and the historical context of such provisions. The document type is 'amendments' as it explicitly states it is amending a section of the Constitution. The category is 'voting'.

"3. Idiots and insane persons."
3. That this amendment shall be submitted to the voters of
the State for their ratification or rejection at the time and in the
manner provided for under Section 256 of the Constitution and
under the Kentucky Statutes.
Approved March 22, 1954
CHAPTER 3
(S. B. 16)
AN ACT relating to taxation and sewer service charges of sanitation
districts and amending sections 220.360 and 220.510 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 220.360 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The board of directors, as soon as duly appointed and
qualified, may levy one, two, or three annual taxes, which taxes
need not be in successive years, of not more than fifteen cents
upon each one hundred dollars of assessed valuation of property
within the district, to be used for the purpose of paying the
expenses of organization, surveys and plans, and for other in
cidental expenses that may be necessary up to the time money is
received from the sale of bonds. This tax shall be certified to
the auditors of the various counties and by them to the respective
treasurers of their counties. The tax shall be based upon the last
preceding assessment for state and county purposes, its collec
tion shall conform to the collection of taxes for counties, and the
same provisions concerning the nonpayment of taxes shall apply.
The tax shall be added by the county clerk to the next state and
county tax bill following the levy of the tax by the board of
directors, and shall be collected concurrently with the state and
countv taxes. Neither the county tax commissioner nor the
county court clerk shall be entitled to any additional compensa
tion for the services rendered in connection with the listing of
the property for taxation nor shall the sheriff receive any addi
tional compensation for the collection of the tax.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 25

classification.title: AN ACT relating to taxation and sewer service charges of sanitation districts and amending sections 220.360 and 220.510 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.

It also mentions that the tax collection will conform to the collection of taxes for counties and that the same provisions concerning nonpayment of taxes shall apply.

Chapter 3 5
Section 2. Section 220.510 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The board of directors shall, by resolution, determine the
rates and compensation or rentals to be charged for the use of
the sanitary works. The board of directors may provide for a
sewer service charge to be imposed and collected, beginning at
the time the plan for the improvement has been approved by the
State Board of Health, and work is begun on plans and specifi
cations for the improvement. The rates shall at all times be rea
sonable, taking into account the cost of the works, the cost of
operation and maintenance, and the amount necessary for the
amortization of the bonds issued to finance the works. The
same schedule of rates and charges shall apply to all users of the
same class. The rates shall be binding upon all users of the
system. The board may alter and revise the rates in its discre
tion. In case of failure of any user to pay for services rendered,
the board may compel payment and may enjoin further use until
the payment is made, or it may institute an action in any court
having jurisdiction for the recovery of charges for services ren
dered, or the board may, by a notice in writing, signed by its

or the person, firm, or corporation which furnishes water to the
user's premises, to shut off the water service to said user's prem
ises until such time as all delinquent charges, plus a reasonable
charge for turning off and on the water service, against said user,
are paid in full. Upon receipt of such notice in writing, the
municipality, or the person, firm, or corporation, which fur
nishes water to the said user's premises, shall immediately shut
off and discontinue the water service to the said user's premises.
Upon full payment of such account, plus a reasonable charge for
turning off and on the water service, the chairman, or any mem
ber of said board, shall notify the said municipality, person, firm,
or corporation, which furnishes water to said user, that the ac
count is paid in full, including such reasonable charge for turn
ing off and on the water service, and that the said water service
can again be provided to said user's premises. The board of
directors shall promptly pay to such municipality, person, firm,
or corporation, such fee or charge collected for turning off and
on such water service. The board may enter into contracts with
public corporations or other large users of sewer services. The

AN ACT changing the classification of the City of Fort Wright, in

WHEREAS, satisfactory evidence has been presented to the General
Assembly that the population of the City of Fort Wright, in Kenton
County, is such as to justify its being assigned to the fifth class of
cities;
NOW THEREFORE
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
The City of Fort Wright, in Kenton County, is changed from

on any grand or petit jury.

stander has served on a jury in the circuit court as a bystander

(2) No person shall be eligible to serve as a juror in any
court inferior to the circuit court who has served as a juror in
any inferior court within four months prior thereto, and such
prior service shall be cause for challenge.

ticular nature or notoriety of a pending case, or for other

on the date when summoned, excuse any number of them in its
discretion until such day as they may be ordered to report for
jury service.

one branch or division of the court may be used by the judge

month and the term, or date, for which the jurors are selected,

and his deputies the oath prescribed in Section 9 of this Act.
Section 16. In circuit courts having continuous sessions
and more than one judge, if the judge of any division cannot
attend upon the day provided for the drawing of names for
juries, the judge of any division of the court may attend and
draw names for the juries. In circuit courts having continuous
sessions and only one judge, if the judge cannot attend upon
the day provided for the drawing of names for juries, he shall
draw the names for the juries upon the first day thereafter he
does attend. Any special judge presiding at a term of court
at which the regular judge is absent, or over any division of a
circuit court having continuous sessions, may draw the names
for the juries in the same manner as the regular judge.
Section 17. Grand or petit juries may be summoned for
any special term of a circuit court by direction of the judge.

It also details the administration of oaths to the clerk and deputies.

16 Acts of the General Assembly
direct the clerk to immediately open the list made and delivered
to him at the last regular term of the court. The clerk shall
immediately open the list, make a copy thereof and deliver it
to the sheriff, who shall summon the jurors. If the list of jurors
selected for a regular term is used at a special term, the judge
at the special term shall draw and make up from the drum, in
the manner provided in Section 14 hereof, other lists, instead
of the ones used, for the next regular term of the court.
Section 18. ( 1 ) The clerk of each circuit court shall open
the sealed envelope containing the jury list for a term, or month,
not more than twenty days before the date on which the grand
and petit jurors are required to attend and shall make fair copies
of the jury lists and deliver them to the sheriff in sufficient time
to enable him to summon the jurors by the time hereinafter
specified. The sheriff shall, at least seven days prior to the date
on which they are required to attend, summon the persons to
attend. In courts having terms, the judge shall, by order, specify
to the sheriff the number of jurors to be summoned to attend
on the first day of the term, and, if less than the full number
drawn for jury service are directed to attend on the first day of
the term, then the subsequent day in the term on which the
remainder shall be summoned to attend. In courts of continuous
session, the jurors shall be summoned to attend on the first
Monday in the month for which they were drawn, or on such
day as is ordered by the judge; provided, that the judge, by order,
may direct the sheriff to summon less than the full number
drawn to attend the first Monday, and the remainder at a sub
sequent day. The officer shall summon such persons by giving
to each one found personal notice, and to each one not found
by leaving a written notice at the juror's place of residence with
some member of his family over sixteen years of age. The list
shall be returned by the sheriff seven days in advance of the
day upon which the jurors are required to attend, with a certifi
cate thereon of the date and manner in which each juror was
summoned. From this list the regular panels of grand and petit
juries shall be selected. The court may, in selecting the respec
tive panels, interrogate such persons relative to any matter which
it deems pertinent and material to determine their qualifications
as jurors.

for jury service is deceased, or was otherwise unable to be served,

(3) No sheriff or other officer shall, corruptly or through
favor or ill will, summon a juror with intent that the juror shall
find a verdict for or against any party, or, with like intent that
a person shall or shall not find an indictment against any par
ticular individual, and he shall not through favor or ill will fail
to summon a person to act as a juror.
Section 19. At the commencement of each term of court
at which jury cases may be tried, the judge shall administer to
the sheriff and deputies the following oath:
"You do swear, without favor or affection, or without a
desire to favor or injure the rights of any litigant, you will sum
mon, when required, jurors for this county; that, to the best of
your skill and judgment, you will select discreet, sensible, im
partial and sober men; that you will not, directly or indirectly,
communicate or converse with any juryman, unless under the
direction of the court, touching the subject matter of any suit
pending for trial at that time; that you will not, by any means
attempt to influence, advise, or control a juryman in his opinion
in any case under trial."
Section 20. The court may adjourn the whole or a part
of the petit jury to any day of the term, but they shall not be
paid for the time they stand adjourned. The grand jury may be
adjourned in like manner without pay.
Section 21. A grand jury shall be summoned at three reg
ular terms of circuit court, unless otherwise ordered by the judge
by an order entered of record. In courts of continuous session the
judge of the court, or of the criminal division if the court has
divisions, shall empanel a grand jury on the first Monday of
each month that the court is in session, unless otherwise ordered
by the judge.
Section 22. If, during the session of the grand jury, one
or more of said jurors becomes ill, or it is necessary to excuse
him for any reason within the discretion of the court, the court

Section 23. The court shall appoint one of the number
of every grand jury foreman thereof, and the following oath,
in substance, shall be administered to the grand jury: "Saving
yourselves, you do swear that you will diligently inquire of and
present all felonies, misdemeanors, and breaches of the penal
laws, that have been committed or done within the limits of
the jurisdiction of this county, of which you have knowledge or
may receive information."
Section 24. The judge of each circuit court shall charge
each grand jurv empaneled in his court with reference to their
duties in regard to law enforcement.
Section 25. (1) In courts in counties not containing a
city of the first class, grand juries may remain in session six days
at any term of court, and the court may, if the business before
the grand jury requires it, by an order duly entered on the order
book, extend the time up to nine additional days.
(2) In courts in counties containing a city of the first class,
the grand jury may remain in session from the first Monday of
the month for which it is empaneled to, but not including, the
first Monday in the following month, in the discretion of the
court.
Section 26. On the day the persons selected by the judge
to serve as petit jurors are summoned to attend, the panel shall
be called, and the names of such as attend and are not excused
by the court shall be entered of record. The clerk shall write the
names of jurors entered of record on identical separate slips of
paper. When a jury is wanted for the trial of a case, it shall be
drawn from a box after the slips have been deposited therein
and well mixed. The clerk shall provide and keep for that purpose
a suitable box with a sliding lid.
Section 27. No officer, party to an action pending, or his
attorney or attorneys shall, without leave of court, converse with
the jury or any member thereof upon any subject after they
have been sworn.

CHAPTER 8
(S. B. 45)
AN ACT relating to strip mining and the conservation and reclamation
of natural resources affected thereby.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires:
(1) "Strip mining" means all or any part of the process
followed in the production of commercial coal from a natural
coal deposit by the open pit or open cut method;
(2) "Overburden" means all the earth and other materials
which are removed to gain access to the commercial coal in the
process of strip mining;
(3) "Spoil bank" means a deposit of removed overburden
of more than twelve inches in depth;
(4) "Area of land affected" means the area of land from
which overburden is to be or has been removed and upon which
a spoil bank is to be or has been deposited;
(5) "Operation" means all of the premises, facilities, and
equipment used in the process of producing coal from a desig
nated strip mine area;
(6) "Operator" means any person, partnership, or corpora
tion engaged in strip mining who removes or intends to remove
more than two hundred and fifty tons of coal from the earth by
strip mining within twelve successive calendar months;

20 Acts of the General Assembly
(7) "Person" means person, partnership, or corporation;
(8) "Director" means the Director of Strip Mining and Rec
lamation;
(9) "Commission" means the Strip Mining and Reclama
tion Commission created by Section 3 of this Act;
(10) "Reclamation" means the reconditioning of the area
of land affected by a strip mining operation under a plan ap
proved by the commission.
Section 2. The General Assembly finds that the unregu
lated strip mining of coal causes soil erosion, stream pollution,
the accumulation of stagnant water and the seepage of contami
nated water, increases the likelihood of floods, destroys the value
of land for agricultural purposes, counteracts efforts for the con
servation of soil, water and other natural resources, destroys or
impairs the property rights of citizens, creates fire hazards, and
in general creates hazards dangerous to life and property, so as
to constitute an imminent and inordinate peril to the welfare of
the Commonwealth. The General Assembly further finds that
there are wide variations in the circumstances and conditions
surrounding and arising out of the strip mining of coal due pri
marily to differences in topographical and geological conditions,
and by reason thereof it is necessary, in order to provide the
most effective, beneficial and equitable solution to the problem,
that a broad discretion be vested in the authority designated to
administer and enforce the regulatory provisions enacted by the
General Assembly. The General Assembly further finds that
other commercial activities involving the removal of minerals
and other natural substances other than coal from the earth by
strip mining occasionally cause conditions or create results of
the character enumerated above, but that no imminent or inordi
nate peril presently exists by reason thereof. Therefore, it is
the purpose of this Act to provide such regulation and control
of the strip mining of coal as to minimize its injurious effects as
much as may be practicable.
Section 3. There hereby is created a Strip Mining and Rec
lamation Commission, which shall constitute an independent
agency of the state government within the meaning of Chapter
12 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, and which shall be com
posed of the Commissioner of Conservation, serving as chairman,

Chapter 8 21
the Chief of the Department of Mines and Minerals, and the
Director of Strip Mining and Reclamation provided for in Section
4 of this Act. The members of the commission, other than the
Director of Strip Mining and Reclamation, shall receive no com
pensation for their services on the commission, but shall be re
imbursed for their expenses incurred in performing their func
tions. The commission shall meet from time to time on the call
of any member, but shall meet at least four times each year.
Section 4. The Director of Strip Mining and Reclamation
shall be appointed by the Governor for a term not to exceed
four years. The director shall receive a salary to be fixed by the
Governor, and shall be reimbursed for expenses incurred in the
performance of his duties. He shall be the executive officer of
the commission and shall have supervision of the commission
staff. The commission shall have power to employ such personnel
as may be required to carry out the purposes of this Act. How
ever, the services and facilities of other state agencies, within
the scope of their regular functions, shall be utilized as far as
possible to minimize the personnel requirements of this Act.
Section 5. The commission shall have and be entitled to
exercise the following authority and powers:
(1) To exercise general supervision and administration
and enforcement of this Act and all rules and regulations and
orders promulgated thereunder;
(2) To encourage and conduct investigations, research, ex
periments and demonstrations and to collect and disseminate
information relating to strip mining and the reclamation of lands
and waters affected by strip mining;
(3) To adopt, after giving notice to all operators actively
engaged in strip mining and affording them an opportunity to
appear and offer evidence at a public hearing, general rules and
regulations pertaining to strip mining to accomplish the purposes
of this Act;
(4) To adopt, without hearing, rules and regulations with
respect to procedural aspects of hearings, the filing of reports
and orders, the issuance of permits and other matters;
(5) To issue, after hearing, orders requiring the adoption
by an operator of such remedial measures necessary for the recla
mation of the area of land affected by his operation;

Section 9. Under regulations adopted by the commission,
an operator shall prepare and carry out a reclamation plan for
the area of land affected by his operation which shall, after
approval by the commission, require him to:
( 1 ) Cover the face of the coal and, where practicable, all
toxic materials, roof coal, pyritic coal or shale determined by the
commission to be acid-producing or creating a fire hazard;
(2) Seal off any break-through creating a hazard;
(3) Impound, drain or treat all runoff water so as to re
duce soil erosion, damage to agricultural lands and pollution
of streams and other waters;
(4) Remove or bury all metal, lumber and other refuse
resulting from the operation;
(5) Grade spoil banks where practicable and provide suit
able vegetative cover.
Section 10. (1) It shall be the duty of an operator to
commence the reclamation of the area of land affected by his
operation as soon as possible after the beginning of strip mining
of that area, and to complete such reclamation as soon as may
be feasible. Before an operator commences to reclaim an area
of land affected, he shall file with the commission a plan of rec
lamation. The commission may require modification of the
plan to meet the requirements of this Act or of its regulations.
The commission shall allow the operator a reasonable period
of time to complete the reclamation of that area of land affected

dress shown on the application for a permit. The notice shall
specify in what respects the operator has failed to comply with
this Act or the regulations or orders of the commission. If the
operator has not reached an agreement with the commission or
has not complied with the requirements set forth by it within
forty days after mailing of the notice, the permit may be revoked
by the commission and the performance bond shall then be
forfeited to the commission.
(2) When a bond is forfeited pursuant to the provisions
of this Act, the director shall give notice to the Attorney General,
who shall collect the forfeiture.
Section 14. All sums received through the payment of fees
or the forfeiture of bonds shall be placed in the State Treasury
and credited to a special agencv account to be designated as the
Strip Mining and Reclamation Fund. This fund shall be available
to the commission and may be expended for the administration
and enforcement of this Act and for the reclamation of land
affected by strip mining operations. Any unencumbered and any
unexpended balance of this fund remaining at the end of any
fiscal year shall not lapse but shall be carried forward for the
purposes of this Act until expended or until appropriated by sub
sequent legislative action.

Section 15. In the reclamation of land affected by strip
mining for which it has funds available, the commission may
avail itself of any services which may be provided by other state
agencies or by agencies of the Federal government, and may
compensate them for such services. The commission may cause
the reclamation work to be done by its own employes or by em
ploves of other governmental agencies or through contracts with
qualified persons. Such contracts shall be awarded to the lowest
or best bidder upon competitive bids after reasonable advertise
ment. The commission and any other agency and any contractor
under a contract with the commission shall have the right of
access to the land affected to carry out such reclamation.
Section 16. Any person aggrieved by any order, determina
tion, ruling or regulation of the commission or of the director
may apply to the commission for a review thereof within thirty
days after issuance thereof. The commission shall review the
matter as soon as practicable, hear the parties, their representa
tives and witnesses, and make a final determination of the issue,
a copy of which shall be mailed to the complainant. Within
twenty days after the mailing of a copy of the final determination,
any person aggrieved may appeal therefrom on any question of
law, including adequacy of evidence, to the Franklin Circuit
Court. The complaint shall state fully the grounds upon which a
review is sought and assign all errors relied on. Two copies of the
complaint shall be filed with the commission on the day it is filed
with the clerk of the Franklin Circuit Court. The appellant shall
file as a part of his complaint the record before the commission,
including all pleadings, orders and such parts of the evidence
as he believes necessary to develop the points involved. The
commission may certify other portions of the evidence. To facili
tate final adjudication of the matter, the commission shall furnish
to the appellant, on payment of a reasonable fee to be fixed by
the commission, a certified copy of such parts of the record as
he may require. The case shall then be heard by the court on the
record, except that either party may introduce evidence of fraud.
Except as otherwise expressly provided, the Rules of Civil Pro
cedure shall govern procedure involving appeals under this
section.

ber of the commission.

versity or college or practical experience in governmental ad
ministration, and who shall hold office at the pleasure of the

released within five days from the receipt of such request. The
superintendent shall provide reasonable means and arrange
ments for informing patients hospitalized under the provisions
of this section of their right to release and for assisting them in
making and presenting requests for release.
(5) If the superintendent believes that the patient is dan
gerous to himself or other persons, or would be benefited by
further care and treatment in a hospital, he may within five
days after the receipt of the request for release file a petition
in proper court for an inquest concerning the sanity of the
patient as provided in KRS Chapter 202. The court, instead of
appointing examiners as provided in KRS 202.100 and 202.110
may admit as evidence the affidavit of the superintendent and
a staff physician. After such petition is filed, the superintendent
may continue to hold the patient even beyond the expiration
of the five-day period referred to in this section until officially
notified by the court that an inquest has been held and the
person has been found to be of sound mind. No fee shall be
charged for the affidavit of the superintendent and a staff
physician, it being considered a part of the regular state service.

Section 3. Section 203.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The superintendent of a state mental hospital may
admit for diagnosis, care and treatment any person who is men
tally ill or has symptoms of mental illness, and who, being
twenty-one years of age or over, applies therefor, and any person
under twenty-one years of age who is mentally ill or has symp
toms of mental illness, if his parent or guardian applies therefor

(2) The superintendent of a state mental hospital may
discharge any voluntary patient who has recovered or whose
hospitalization he determines to be no longer advisable. He may
also discharge any voluntary patient if to do so would, in the
judgment of the superintendent, contribute to the most effective
use of the hospital in the care and treatment of the mentally ill.

release is requested, in writing, by his guardian, parent, spouse,
or adult relative shall be released within five days from the
receipt of such request, except as hereinafter provided in this
section. If the patient was admitted on his own application and
the request for release is made by a person other than the patient,
release shall be conditioned upon the agreement of the patient
thereto. If the patient, by reason of his age, was admitted on
application of his parent or guardian, his release, prior to at
taining the age of twenty-one years, shall be conditioned upon
the consent of such parent or guardian.

confinement is recommended by the examining physician, such
person shall be confined in the appropriate county, municipal
or state hospital or mental institution to await further legal action
as prescribed in this chapter. In counties where hospital facili
ties are not available, said person may be confined in the county
jail for a period not to exceed forty-eight hours.

evidence say in your verdict whether the person whom you have
in charge is of unsound mind and, if of unsound mind, whether

AN ACT relating to school property.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 162.010 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The title to all property owned by a school district is vested
in the Commonwealth for the benefit of the district board of
education. In the acquisition of land for school purposes whether
by purchase or condemnation, or otherwise, the title obtained
shall be in fee simple. Any reversionary interest in any land held
by boards of education on June 14, 1934 shall not deprive such
boards of the ownership of the buildings or other improvements
thereon.
Section 2. Section 162.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Each board of education mav, when unable to make a con-
tract satisfactory to the board with the owner for the purchase
of real estate to be used for school purposes, initiate condemna
tion proceedings under any of the methods of condemnation
authorized by KRS 416.010 to 416.080; KRS 416.120; and KRS
416.230 to 416.310; and the title to land so obtained shall be
vested in fee simple.

CHAPTER 22
(S. B. 79)
AN ACT concerning service of process on nonresident motorists.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 188.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Any nonresident operator or owner of any motor vehicle
who accepts the privilege extended by the laws of this state to
nonresidents to operate motor vehicles or have them operated
within state shall, by such acceptance and by the operation of
such motor vehicle within this state, make the Secretary of
State the agent of himself or his personal representative for the
service of process in any civil action instituted in the courts of
this state against the operator or owner, or the personal repre
sentative of the operator or owner, arising out of or by reason of
any accident or collision or damage occurring within this state
in which the motor vehicle is involved.
Section 2. The provisions of KRS 188.010 to 188.060 shall
apply to any operator or owner of any motor vehicle who is a
resident of this state at the time any accident or collision or
damage occurs and who thereafter becomes a nonresident.
Approved March 22, 1954

AN ACT, relating to public printing, amending Subsection (1) of Sec
tion 57.020, Subsection (7) of Section 57.140 and Sections 57.180,
57.220, 57.250 and 118.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Subsection (1) of Section 57.020 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
"The Superintendent of Public Printing shall superintend all
printing done by the contractors for the state printing, and shall
specify the kind and style of paper, the type to be used, and
the style of printing and binding, including the multiple printing
of forms in large quantities according to standard size of sheets
available, subject to specifications furnished by the Department
of Finance."
Section 2. Subsection (7) of Section 57.140 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
"The acts, the journals of each house of the General As
sembly, and the report of the Commissioner of Agriculture, La
bor and Statistics shall be bound. All other printed matter shall
be only stitched, unless otherwise specially ordered."
Section 3. Section 57.180 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
"All contractors under this chapter shall promptly execute
all orders issued to them by the Department of Finance. The acts
and the journals of the two houses of the General Assembly
shall be printed and bound, and delivered respectively within
sixty days and one hundred eighty days after the end of the ses
sion to the State Librarian for distribution, under the penalty
of forfeiture of the bond of the contractor. The Department of
Finance may, on good cause shown by the contractor, extend the
time for not more than thirty days for executing any work."
Section 4. Section 57.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:

CHAPTER 27
(S. B. 104)
AN ACT providing for the regulation of the practice of ophthalmic dis
pensing; authorizing the issuance of licenses to qualified ophthalmic
dispensers; creating a Board to be known as the "Kentucky Board of
Ophthalmic Dispensers", and conferring duties upon such Board;
and providing for penalties for the violation of this Act.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
1. Definitions.
"Board" as used in this Act shall mean the "Kentucky Board
of Ophthalmic Dispensers."
"Ophthalmic Dispensing". A person practices ophthalmic
dispensing within the meaning of the provisions of this act re
lating to ophthalmic dispensers, who prepares and dispenses
lenses, spectacles, eyeglasses and/or appurtenances thereto to
the intended wearers thereof on written prescriptions from
physicians, osteopaths or optometrists duly licensed to practice
their profession, and in accordance with such prescriptions,
interprets, measures, adapts, fits and adjusts such lenses, spec
tacles, eyeglasses and/or appurtenances thereto to the human
face for the aid or correction of visual or ocular anomalies of the
human eyes. The services and appliances relating to ophthalmic
dispensing shall be dispensed, furnished or supplied to the in
tended wearer or user thereof only upon written prescription
issued by a physician, osteopath or optometrist; but duplications,
replacements, reproductions or repetitions may be done without
prescriptions, in which event any such act shall be construed
to be ophthalmic dispensing the same as if performed on the
basis of an original written prescription.
2. On and after January 1, 1955, no person shall engage
in the practice of ophthalmic dispensing as defined in this Act
unless he is duly licensed to practice medicine, osteopathy or
optometry in this Commonwealth or unless he is the holder of
a license as an ophthalmic dispenser; Provided, this section
shall not be so construed as to prohibit a firm or corporation
from operating a business which comes within the meaning of
ophthalmic dispensing, as defined in Section 1 of this Act, if
each of its employees either (1) coming in contact with the
public and performing for the public any service or services

It defines ophthalmic dispensing and sets requirements for its practice, including the necessity of a prescription from a licensed physician, osteopath, or optometrist for dispensing lenses, spectacles, eyeglasses, and related appurtenances. The act also allows for firms or corporations to operate such businesses if their employees meet licensing requirements.

included within the meaning of ophthalmic dispensing, or (2)
otherwise performing any work or service required by this Act
to be performed by an ophthalmic dispenser, is the holder of a
license as an ophthalmic dispenser.
3. A license as an ophthalmic dispenser shall be issued
by the Board to any person who pays a fee of Twenty-five
($25.00) Dollars and submits evidence under oath satisfactory
to the Board—
(a) That he is more than twenty-one years of age and of
good moral character;
(b) That he has at least two years of satisfactory train
ing and experience in ophthalmic dispensing under the super
vision of an ophthalmic dispenser, physician, osteopath or
optometrist, or is a graduate of an accepted school of ophthalmic
dispensing;
(c) That he has passed a satisfactory examination in
ophthalmic dispensing given by the Board.
4. The Board shall issue an "ophthalmic dispenser" license
to any person who makes application therefor prior to January 1,
1955, and submits evidence under oath satisfactory to the Board
that he is more than twenty-one years of age and of good moral
character; that he was actually engaged as an ophthalmic dis
penser in this state on December 31, 1953, and so engaged for
at least one year prior thereto.
If any person who so desires to secure a license without
examination as provided above shall be in the Armed Forces on
January 1, 1955, and who, at the time of his induction into the
Armed Forces, was actually principally engaged as an ophthalmic
dispenser, his time to make application shall be extended until
six months after the date of his discharge or release from active
duty from such Armed Forces.
5. Nothing in this Act shall be construed to authorize or
permit any ophthalmic dispenser to hold himself out as being
able to, or to either offer, undertake or attempt, by any means
or method, to examine or exercise eyes, to fit contact lenses, or
to diagnose, treat, correct, relieve, operate or prescribe for any
human ailment, deficiency, deformity, disease, injury, pain or
physical condition except that an ophthalmic dispenser holding
a license as such issued hereunder may fit contact lenses under
the supervision of a physician, osteopath or optometrist.

classification.title: An Act relating to ophthalmic dispensers
classification.summary: This act outlines the requirements for obtaining a license as an ophthalmic dispenser in Kentucky. It specifies age, moral character, training, experience, and examination requirements. It also includes provisions for a grandfather clause for those engaged in dispensing prior to December 31, 1953, and extensions for those in the Armed Forces. The act clarifies that ophthalmic dispensers cannot perform eye examinations or diagnose/treat eye conditions, but can fit contact lenses under supervision.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the professional licensing requirements for ophthalmic dispensers. It specifies qualifications such as age, moral character, training, experience, and passing an examination. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that would implicitly create racial segregation or discrimination. The act focuses solely on professional standards and licensing procedures within the field of ophthalmic dispensing. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Chapter 27 69
6. Nothing in the provisions of this Act relating to
ophthalmic dispensing shall be construed to limit or restrict, in
any respect, the practice of medicine by duly licensed physicians
or the practice of osteopathy by duly licensed osteopaths or the
practice of optometry by duly licensed optometrists, and such
licensed physician, osteopath or optometrist shall have all the
rights and privileges which may accrue under the provisions
of this Act to ophthalmic dispensers licensed hereunder.
7. This Act shall not be construed to prohibit an un
licensed person from performing mechanical work upon inert
matter in an optical office, laboratory or shop.
8. Persons, firms and corporations that sell completely
assembled eyeglasses or spectacles without advice or aid as to
the selection thereof, as merchandise, from permanently located
or established places of business shall not be subject to this Act.
9. Physicians, osteopaths and optometrists and their em
ployees while working under their regulation and supervision shall
have the right to practice ophthalmic dispensing without quali
fying under this Act and shall not be subject to any of the pro
visions herein.
10. A license to practice ophthalmic dispensing shall be
renewed each year by the payment of a fee of Ten ($10.00)
Dollars, unless such license has been suspended or revoked by
the Board.
11. There is hereby created a Board of Ophthalmic Dis
pensers to be known as the "Kentucky Board of Ophthalmic
Dispensers". It shall consist of five members to be appointed by
the Governor, one member of which shall be a licensed medical
physician or osteopath experienced in the treatment and ex
amination of eyes and one member of which shall be a licensed
optometrist. The other three members shall be licensed
ophthalmic dispensers except that on the original Board the
three ophthalmic dispensers need not be licensed ophthalmic
dispensers, but must be persons experienced in ophthalmic dis
pensing and must be licensed as ophthalmic dispensers by the
Board to hold their positions. They shall not pass upon their
own qualifications. The Board shall elect one of its members
chairman and one member secretary-treasurer. These officers
shall serve at the pleasure of the Board.
Two members of the Board shall serve for two years from

the date of their appointment and qualification and the other

The Board shall have the right to make reasonable rules and

It may provide for the licensing of apprentice ophthalmic

12. The Board may suspend or revoke any license to
practice ophthalmic dispensing for any of the following causes :
(a) The conviction of a felony involving moral turpitude;
(b) Chronic or persistent inebriety or addiction to a
drug habit, to an extent that disqualifies from practicing with
safety to the public;
(c) Fraud or deception in the application or in the ex
amination for the license;
(d) Violation of this Act.
13. No license shall be suspended or revoked until after
a hearing before the Board upon notice to the license holder
of at least ten days. The notice shall be served either personally
or by registered mail and shall state the date and place of the
hearing and set forth the ground or grounds constituting the
charges against the license holder. The license holder shall be
heard in his defense either in person or by counsel and may
produce witnesses and testify in his own behalf.
14. A licensee, whose license has been suspended or revoked
by the Board, may appeal from the suspension or revocation order
within thirty days of such order to the Circuit Court in the county
in which the licensee is practicing. The procedure shall be the
same as in a civil appeal from a county court to a circuit court.
The trial in the circuit court shall be de novo, and an appeal
may be taken by either side to the Court of Appeals as in a
civil action. The judgment or order of the Board, suspending
or revoking the license, shall be stayed, pending a final ad
judication by the courts.
15. All fees imposed and collected under the provisions
of this Act shall be paid to the Secretary-Treasurer of the Board

16. Penalties. Any person who shall directly or indirectly en
gage in the practice of ophthalmic dispensing or hold himself out
to the public as being able so to do, or who shall violate any
of the provisions of this Act relating to ophthalmic dispensing,
or having had his license suspended or revoked shall continue
to engage in the practice of ophthalmic dispensing, or who
directly or indirectly employs, permits or authorizes an un
licensed person to engage in the practice of ophthalmic dispensing
shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, and, upon conviction, shall be
punished by imprisonment for not more than thirty days, or by
a fine of not more than one hundred dollars or by both such
fine and imprisonment, and each such violation shall be deemed
a separate offense.
Became law without Governor's signature March 31, 1954

CHAPTER 32
(S. B. 121)
AN ACT relating to the purposes for which proceeds of bonds issued to
meet the needs of city universities and colleges may be used.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 165.100 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
The money derived from the bonds issued under authority
of KRS 165.080 shall be used to meet the needs of the uni
versity. The authorized use includes the payment for real
estate and buildings purchased or to be purchased, the erection
and alteration of improvements, and the securing of proper
equipment, but does not include the payment of salaries or
operating expenses or the payment of any existing indebted
ness incurred as an operating expense.
Approved March 22, 1954

CHAPTER 34
(S. B. 125)
AN ACT permitting the fiscal courts of counties and the governing
bodies of cities to contract with each other for the performance of
governmental services.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Notwithstanding any existing means provided
by law for joint city-county functions, the fiscal court of any
county and the governing body of any city within that county
may enter into contractual arrangements with each other for
the performance of governmental services within the county,
both within and without the boundary of the city.
Section 2. Any contractual arrangement entered into
under Section 1 of this Act shall:
(1) Name the service to be performed and clearly define
same;
(2) State which subdivision of government shall render
the service;
(3) Define the area within which the service contracted
for shall be performed;
(4) Provide the means of payment of the cost of the
service;

CHAPTER 37
(S. B. 135)
AN ACT relating to the terms of fiscal courts, enabling fiscal courts to
hold monthly special terms, declaring an emergency, and amending
Section 67.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 67.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
Section 1. (1) The fiscal court of each county shall hold
its sessions at the county seat.
(2) The fiscal court shall hold two regular terms each year,
which shall continue until the business of the court is completed.
The county judge may, by an order of record, fix the dates for
the commencement of the regular terms, but one of the terms
must be held in October; if he does not fix the terms, the first
regular term shall commence on the first Tuesday of April and
the second regular term on the first Tuesday of October.
(3) The county judge may call a special term of the fiscal
court for the transaction of any business of which that court
has jurisdiction. Whenever a special term is necessary and the
county judge is unable, or refuses to act, a majority of the mem
bers of the fiscal court may call the special term.
Approved March 10, 1954

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the terms of fiscal courts, enabling fiscal courts to hold monthly special terms, declaring an emergency, and amending Section 67.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 67.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to allow fiscal courts to hold special terms in addition to their two regular annual terms. It specifies that regular terms continue until business is completed, and the county judge can set dates, with one term required in October. If the judge doesn't set dates, terms begin the first Tuesday of April and October. Special terms can be called by the county judge, or by a majority of fiscal court members if the judge is unable or refuses to act.

80 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 38
(S. B. 136)
AN ACT relating to Partnerships.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
PART I
PRELIMINARY PROVISIONS
Section 1. NAME OF ACT.-This Act may be cited as
Uniform Partnership Act.
Section 2. DEFINITION OF TERMS.-In this Act,
"Court" includes every court and judge having jurisdiction in
in the case.
"Business" includes every trade, occupation, or profession.
"Person" includes individuals, partnerships, corporations, and
other associations.
"Bankrupt" includes bankrupt under the Federal Rankruptcy
Act or insolvent under any state insolvent act.
"Conveyance" includes every assignment, lease, mortgages,
or encumbrance.
"Real Property" includes land and any interest or estate
in land.
Section 3. INTERPRETATION OF KNOWLEDGE AND
NOTICE— (1) A person has "knowledge" of a fact within the
meaning of this Act not only when he has actual knowledge
thereof, but also when he has knowledge of such other facts
as in the circumstances shows bad faith.
(2) A person has "notice" of a fact within the meaning
of this Act when the person who claims the benefit of the
notice:
(a) States the fact to such person, or
(b) Delivers through the mail, or by other means of
communication, a written statement of the fact to such person
or to a proper person at his place of business or residence.
Section 4. RULES OF CONSTRUCTION.- ( 1 ) The rule
that statutes in derogation of the common law are to be strictly
construed shall have no application to this Act.
(2) The law of estoppel shall apply under this Act.
(3) The law of agency shall apply under this Act.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 101

Section 5. RULES FOR CASES NOT PROVIDED FOR

PART II
NATURE OF PARTNERSHIP
Section 6. PARTNERSHIP DEFINED.-(l) A partner
ship is an association of two or more persons to carry on as
co-owners a business for profit.
(2) But any association formed under any other statute
of this state, or any statute adopted by authority, other than
the authority of this state, is not a partnership under this Act,
unless such association would have been a partnership in this
state prior to the adoption of this Act; but this Act shall apply
to limited partnerships except in so far as the statutes relating
to such partnerships are inconsistent herewith.
Section 7. RULES FOR DETERMINING THE EXIST
ENCE OF A PARTNERSHIP.-In determining whether a
partnership exists, these rules shall apply:
(1) Except as provided by Section 16 persons who are
not partners as to each other are not partners as to third persons.
(2) Joint tenancy, tenancy in common, tenancy by the
entireties, joint property, common property, or part ownership
does not of itself establish a partnership, whether such co-owners
do or do not share any profits made by the use of the property.
(3) The sharing of gross returns does not of itself establish
a partnership, whether or not the persons sharing them have a
joint or common right or interest in any property from which the
returns are derived.
(4) The receipt by a person of a share of the profits of
a business is prima facie evidence that he is a partner in the
business, but no such inference shall be drawn if such profits
were received in payment:

partnership or with the authority of his co-partners, loss or injury
is caused to any person, not being a partner in the partnership, or
any penalty is incurred, the partnership is liable therefor to

and misapplies it; and
(b) Where the partnership in the course of its business
receives money or property of a third person and the money or
property so received is misapplied by any partner while it is in
the custody of the partnership.

nership; but any partner may enter into a separate obligation

Section 16. PARTNER BY ESTOPPEL.-( 1) When a
person, by words spoken or written or by conduct, represents
himself, or consents to another representing him to any one, as
a partner in an existing partnership or with one or more persons
not actual partners, he is liable to any such person to whom
such representation has been made, who has, on the faith of
such representation given credit to the actual or apparent part
nership, and if he has made such representation or consented
to its being made in a public manner he is liable to such person,
whether the representation has or has not been made or com
municated to such person so giving credit by or with the knowl
edge of the apparent partner making the representation or
consenting to its being made:
(a) When a partnership liability results, he is liable as
though he were an actual member of the partnership.
(b) When no partnership liability results, he is liable
jointly with the other persons, if any, so consenting to the con
tract or representation as to incur liability, otherwise separately.
(2) When a person has been thus represented to be a
partner in an existing partnership, or with one or more persons
not actual partners, he is an agent of the persons consenting to
such representation to bind them to the same extent and in the
same manner as though he were a partner in fact, with respect
to persons who rely upon the representation. Where all the
members of the existing partnership consent to the representation,
a partnership act or obligation results; but in all other cases it
is the joint act or obligation of the person acting and the persons
consenting to the representation.
Section 17. LIABILITY OF INCOMING PARTNER.-A
person admitted as a partner into an existing partnership is
liable for all the obligations of the partnership arising before his
admission as though he had been a partner when such obligations
were incurred, except that the liability shall be satisfied only
out of partnership property.

86 Acts of the General Assembly
PART IV
RELATIONS OF PARTNERS TO ONE ANOTHER
Section 18. RULES DETERMINING RIGHTS AND
DUTIES OF PARTNERS.-The rights and duties of the partners
in relation to the partnership shall be determined, subject to any
agreement between them, by the following rules :
( a ) Each partner shall be repaid his contributions, whether
by way of capital or advances to the partnership property and
share equally in the profits and surplus remaining after all
liabilities, including those to partners, are satisfied; and must
contribute toward the losses, whether of capital or otherwise,
sustained by the partnership according to his share in the profits.
(b) The partnership must indemnify every partner in re
spect of payments made and personal liabilities reasonably in
curred by him in the ordinary and proper conduct of its business,
or for the preservation of its business or property.
(c) A partner, who in aid of the partnership makes any
payment or advance beyond the amount of capital which he
agreed to contribute, shall be paid interest from the date of
the payment or advance.
(d) A partner shall receive interest on the capital con
tributed by him only from the date when repayment should
be made.
( e ) All partners have equal rights in the management and
conduct of the partnership business.
(f) No partner is entitled to remuneration for acting in
the partnership business, except that a surviving partner is en
titled to reasonable compensation for his services in winding up
the partnership affairs.
(g) No person can become a member of a partnership
without the consent of all the partners.
(h) Any difference arising as to ordinary matters con
nected with the partnership business may be decided by a ma
jority of the partners; but no act in contravention of any
agreement between the partners may be done rightfully without
the consent of all the partners.
Section 19. PARTNERSHIP BOOKS.-The partnership
books shall be kept, subject to any agreement between the
partners, at the principal place of business of the partnership, and

Section 20. DUTY OF PARTNERS TO RENDER IN
FORMATION.— Partners shall render on demand true and full
information of all things affecting the partnership to any partner

Section 21. PARTNER ACCOUNTABLE AS A FIDUCI
ARY.— (1) Every partner must account to the partnership for

partnership or from any use by him of its property.

partner.
Section 22. RIGHT TO AN ACCOUNT.-Any partner
shall have the right to a formal account as to partner
ship affairs:
(a) If he is wrongfully excluded from the partnership
business or possession of its property by his co-partners,
( b ) If the right exists under the terms of any agreement,

(d) Whenever other circumstances render it just and
reasonable.
Section 23. CONTINUATION OF PARTNERSHIP BE
YOND FIXED TERM.-(l) When a partnership for a fixed
term or particular undertaking is continued after the termination
of such term or particular undertaking without any express
agreement, the rights and duties of the partners remain the same
as they were at such termination, so far as is consistent with a
partnership at will.
( 2 ) A continuation of the business by the partners or such
of them as habitually acted therein during the term, without any
settlement or liquidation of the partnership affairs, is prima facie
evidence of a continuation of the partnership.

88 Acts of the General Assembly
PART V
PROPERTY RIGHTS OF A PARTNER
Section 24. EXTENT OF PROPERTY RIGHTS OF A
PARTNER.— The property rights of a partner are ( 1 ) his rights
in specific partnership property, (2) his interest in the partner
ship, and (3) his right to participate in the management.
Section 25. NATURE OF A PARTNER'S RIGHT IN
SPECIFIC PARTNERSHIP PROPERTY.-(l) A partner is
co-owner with his partners of specific partnership property hold
ing as a tenant in partnership.
(2) The incidents of this tenancy are such that:
( a ) A partner, subject to the provisions of this Act and to
any agreement between the partners, has an equal right with
his partners to possess specific partnership property for partner
ship purposes; but he has no right to possess such property for any
other purpose without the consent of his partners.
(b) A partner's right in specific partnership property is
not assignable except in connection with the assignment of rights
of all the partners in the same property.
(c) A partner's right in specific partnership property is
not subject to attachment or execution, except on a claim against
the partnership. When partnership property is attached for a
partnership debt the partners, or any of them, or the repre
sentatives of a deceased partner, cannot claim any right under
the homestead or exemption laws.
( d ) On the death of a partner his right in specific partner
ship property vests in the surviving partner or partners, except
where the deceased was the last surviving partner, when his
right in such property vests in his legal representative. Such
surviving partner or partners, or the legal representative of the
last surviving partner, has no right to possess the partnership
property for any but a partnership purpose.
(e) A partner's right in specific partnership property is
not subject to dower, curtesy, or allowances to widows, heirs, or
next of kin.
Section 26. NATURE OF PARTNER'S INTEREST IN
THE PARTNERSHIP.-A partner's interest in the partnership is
his share of the profits and surplus, and the same is personal
property.

Chapter 38 89
Section 27. ASSIGNMENT OF PARTNER'S INTEREST.
— (1) A conveyance by a partner of his interest in the partnership
does not of itself dissolve the partnership, nor, as against the
other partners in the absence of agreement, entitle the assignee,
during the continuance of the partnership, to interfere in the
management or administration of the partnership business or
affairs, or to require any information or account of partnership
transactions, or to inspect the partnership books; but it merely
entitles the assignee to receive in accordance with his contract
the profits to which the assignee partner would otherwise be
entitled.
(2) In case of a dissolution of the partnership, the assignee
is entitled to receive his assignor's interest and may require an
account from the date only of the last account agreed to by
all the partners.
Section 28. PARTNER'S INTEREST SUBJECT TO
CHARGING ORDER.- ( 1 ) On due application to a competent
court by any judgment creditor of a partner, the court which
entered the judgment, order, or decree, or any other court, may
charge the interest of the debtor partner with payment of the
unsatisfied amount of such judgment debt with interest thereon;
and may then or later appoint a receiver of his share of the
profits, and of any other money due or to fall due to him in
respect of the partnership, and make all other orders, directions,
accounts and inquiries which the debtor partner might have
made, or which the circumstances of the case may require.
(2) The interest charged may be redeemed at any time
before foreclosure, or in case of a sale being directed by the
court may be purchased without thereby causing a dissolution:
(a) With separate property, by any one or more of the
partners, or
(b) With partnership property, by any one or more of the
partners with the consent of all the partners whose interests
are not so charged or sold.
(3) Nothing in this Act shall be held to deprive a partner
of his right, if any, under the exemption laws, as regards his
interest in the partnership.

90 Acts of the General Assembly
PART VI
DISSOLUTION AND WINDING UP
Section 29. DISSOLUTION DEFINED.-The dissolution
of a partnership is the change in the relation of the partners
caused by any partner ceasing to be associated in the carry
ing on as distinguished from the winding up of the business.
Section 30. PARTNERSHIP NOT TERMINATED BY
DISSOLUTION.— On dissolution the partnership is not termi
nated, but continues until the winding up of partnership affairs
is completed.
Section 31. CAUSES OF DISSOLUTION.-Dissolution is
caused:
(1) Without violation of the agreement between the part
ners:
(a) By the termination of the definite term or particular
undertaking specified in the agreement,
(b) By the express will of any partner when no definite
term or particular undertaking is specified,
(c) By the express will of all the partners who have not
assigned their interests or suffered them to be charged for
their separate debts, either before or after the termination of
any specified term or particular undertaking,
(d) By the expulsion of any partner from the business
bona fide in accordance with such a power conferred by the
agreement between the partners;
(2) In contravention of the agreement between the part
ners, where the circumstances do not permit a dissolution under
any other provision of this section, by the express will of any
partner at any time;
(3) By any event which makes it unlawful for the business
of the partnership to be carried on or for the members to cam
it on in partnership;
(4) By the death of any partner;
(5) By the bankruptcy of any partner or the partnership;

AN ACT relating to highways and the financing thereof.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. It is the declared policy and purpose of the
General Assembly, in enacting this Act, to foster, promote, and
expedite the construction, maintenance and improvement of a
modern and adequate system of roads throughout the Common
wealth, in the interests of the public safety and general welfare;
and, in the furtherance of such policy and purpose, to authorize
the maximum financial commitments permissible under the
provisions of the Constitution.

wise requires:
( 1 ) "Authority" means The Kentucky Highway Authority;

such department, agency, officer or officers of the Common
wealth as may succeed to the principal powers, functions and
duties thereof;
(3) "Project" means any property, structure, facility, or
undertaking which the Authority is authorized to acquire, con
struct, improve, maintain, equip, furnish, lease or operate under
the provisions of this Act;
(4) "Person" means natural persons, firms, associations,
corporations, business trusts, partnerships and public bodies;

ica, the President of the United States of America, and any

of all land, rights-of-way, property, rights, easements, and in

shall be entitled to no compensation for their services as mem
bers; but the appointed members shall be entitled to compen
sation on the basis of twenty-five dollars per diem, in addition
to reimbursement for their expenses. Of the members appointed
by the Governor, two shall serve for a term which shall expire
with the term of the Governor appointing them; two shall serve
for terms expiring one year after such expiration; two shall
serve for terms expiring two years after such expiration; and
two shall serve terms expiring three years after such expiration.
In making the initial appointments, the Governor shall designate
the expiration of the term of each appointee. Subsequent ap
pointments shall each be for a term of four years, and until a
successor is appointed and qualified, except that in the event
a vacancy occurring by reason of death or resignation, the ap
pointment shall be only for the unexpired term. Appointments

102 Acts of the General Assembly
shall be made in such manner that of the eight appointed mem

Section 4. (1) Within ninety days after the effective
date of this Act, the Governor shall appoint the citizen mem
bers of the Authority, and the Authority shall then meet and
organize. The Governor shall, by virtue of his office, be chairman
of the Authority, and the Lieutenant Governor shall in like
manner be the vice-chairman.
(2) Eight members of the Authority shall constitute a
quorum for the transaction of business, and a lesser number
may adjourn from time to time until a quorum is convened.
The Authority shall convene at least three times in each calendar
year at such times and places within the Commonwealth as
may be stated in a notice of the meeting, mailed to each mem
ber not less than ten days in advance, or upon written waiver
of notice by each member.
(3) The Authority may by resolution entered upon its
records, upon the affirmative vote of not less than eight mem
bers, create an executive committee of five of its members. The
executive committee may exercise such powers of the Authority
as may be set forth in such resolution. At least two members of
the executive committee shall be appointive members of the
Authority, who shall be of different political affiliations. The ex
ecutive secretary shall keep full and correct minutes of the
meetings and final actions of the Authority, which shall be
open to the inspection of the public at all reasonable times.
Copies of any records or proceedings of the Authority, authenti
cated under the seal of the Authority by the executive secretary,
shall be received as prima facie evidence of such records or
proceedings in any court, commission, board, department or
agency of the Commonwealth.
Section 5. The Authority shall elect, by the votes of at
least eight members, an executive secretary who shall not be
a member of the Authority but who shall be its chief executive
officer. The executive secretary shall serve on a full-time basis
at the pleasure of the Authority. He shall receive an annual
salary not to exceed the salary of the State Highway Engineer,
less one hundred dollars.
