classification.title: Chapter 39 107
classification.summary: This statute outlines the terms and conditions for leases between an Authority and a department, focusing on rental payments, lease duration, and the department's financial obligations. It specifies that rentals are compensation for services and facilities, binding the department to pay from current resources during each legislative biennium. Unpaid rentals can be carried over to future bienniums if current resources are insufficient. The department can pledge highway revenues as security for rental payments. Failure to pay rentals in a biennium does not affect future rental obligations or create a cause of action for those future rentals.

Section 4. The State Law Library shall occupy the quarters
on the second floor of the State Capitol formerly occupied by the
Legislative and Law Library, and the State Property and Build
ings Commission shall allocate sufficient other space in the State
Capitol to provide stack room for 30,000 books.
Section 5. The law librarian shall keep the State Law Li
brary open at such hours, maintain the property of the library in
such manner and order, and make and keep such catalogues, in
ventories, records and reports, as the board of trustees may di
rect. He shall be responsible for all books lost from the library
during his period of service, and may be required by the board of
trustees to give bond to secure such responsibility.
Section 6. Such books in the State Law Library as from
time to time may become no longer useful for law library pur
poses may be sold, exchanged or otherwise disposed of by the
board of trustees.
Section 7. KRS 171.070 is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any person may use the books of the State Law Li
brary but the law librarian shall not permit anyone to take the
books out of the library, with the following exceptions:
( a ) Members of the legislature.
(b) Judges and commissioners of the Court of Appeals,
the Attorney-General, the Governor, and other state officials.
(c) Attorneys may take the books to the rooms of the
Court of Appeals for use in the argument of cases there pending.
(d) Books may be placed on temporary loan to other
libraries.
( 2 ) The law librarian shall keep a record in which he shall
charge each book to the person taking it from the library and
he shall see that all books are returned at the proper time.
Section 8. KRS 171.080 is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Any person who fails to return a book or other prop
erty taken from the State Law Library by him, or who damages
property of the library, shall be liable for double the value of
the thing taken or damage to the property.
(2) The law librarian shall, in the name of the Common
wealth of Kentucky, sue any such person in any court of Frank
lin County having jurisdiction of the amount involved.

tucky.
Section 24. KRS 171.360 is amended to read as follows:

ians of each institution. Thirty copies shall be available to the

comprehensive collection of state documents. Such duty shall
be exclusive of and in addition to any duties imposed by other
provisions of law as to furnishing or delivering any such mater
ials. It shall be the duty of the central depository promptly to

received and to replace any loss or miscarriage in the transporta
tion of such material to the extent extra copies shall be available
for such purpose. Such duty shall be exclusive of and in addition
to the duty of any other agency to distribute certain books or
documents under other provisions of laws; provided, that the
central depository need not make distribution, to a particular
depository library, of such material as the latter shall be entitled
to receive under other provisions of law.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 28.435 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
( 1 ) Any circuit judge may, if he deems it necessary to aid
the court and the stenographic reporter, require that a device

a circuit court; provided that such a device may not be used

graphic notes of the proceeding, except that the court with the
approval of the parties, acting through their respective at
torneys, may require that a device be used solely for recording
such testimony without the presence of a stenographic reporter.
( 2 ) The fiscal court of any county may purchase a record
ing device for the use of the circuit court of that county if it

CHAPTER 44
(S. B. 147)
AN ACT relating to abandoned refrigerators, iceboxes, and ice chests.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Any person, firm or corporation which abandons or discards,
in any place accessible to children, any refrigerator, icebox or
ice chest, of a capacity of one and one-half cubic feet or more,
which has an attached lid or door which may be opened or
closed by means of an attached latch, or any owner, lessee or
manager of such place who knowingly permits such abandoned
or discarded refrigerator, icebox or ice chest to remain in such
condition, shall be guilty of a misdemeanor, and upon convic
tion shall be fined not more than fifty dollars, or imprisoned not
more than thirty days, or both.
Approved March 22, 1954

Section 2. (1) On and after the effective date of this
Act all offices, departments, institutions and agencies of this
state which are supported in whole or in part by this state
shall purchase, and all political subdivisions of this state may
purchase, when economically feasible from the Department
of Welfare all articles or products required by such offices,
departments, institutions, agencies, or political subdivisions of
this state, produced or manufactured by prison labor, as pro
vided by this Act, and no such article or product shall be pur
chased by any such office, department, institution, or agency,
from other source, except as hereinafter provided:
(a) Exceptions may be made in any case where, in the
opinion of the Department of Finance, the articles or products
so produced or manufactured do not meet the reasonable re
quirements of such offices, departments, institutions, agencies,
or, in any case, where the requisition cannot be reasonably
complied with because of an insufficient supply of the articles
or products required. However, no office, department, institution
or agency, shall be allowed to evade the intent and meaning
of this Act by slight variations from standards adopted by the
Division of Purchases, when the articles or products produced
or manufactured by the Department of Welfare in accordance
with said standards, are reasonably adapted to the actual needs
of such office, department, institution, or agency.
(2) All purchases, under this Act shall be made through
the Department of Finance upon requisition by the proper au
thority of the office, department, institution, agency or political
subdivision of this state.
Section 3. The articles or products manufactured or pro
duced by prison labor in accordance with the provisions of this
Act shall be devoted, first, to fulfilling the requirements of the
offices, departments, institutions, and agencies of this state which
are supported in whole or in part by this state, and, secondly,
to supply the political subdivisions of this state with such ar
ticles and products.

CHAPTER 48

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. (1) It shall be the duty of the Legislative
Research Commission to formulate, supervise and execute plans
and methods for the future revision, clarification, classification,
codification, arrangement, annotating, indexing, printing, bind
ing, publication, copyrighting, sale and distribution of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes, including annotations and supplements,
and all editions thereof. The commission shall determine when
editions shall be published and fix the prices thereof. Such print
ing shall not be considered printing of the first class within the
meaning of the statutes relating to printing of the first class.
The commission shall prepare and submit to the General As
sembly such consolidation, revision and other matters relating
to the statutes as can be completed from time to time.
(2) The commission shall control and supervise the use
of any type acquired by the state in connection with the print
ing of editions of the statutes, and may permit the use of the
type for the printing of pamphlets of particular portions of the
statute laws requested by any state department or agency, under
such terms and conditions as the commission considers rea
sonable.
(3) The commission, in preparing editions of the stat
utes for publication or distribution, shall not alter the sense,

When any edition of the statutes is published by the commis
sion, the director shall prepare a certificate certifying that each
section printed in such edition has been compared with the
original section in the enrolled Kentucky Revised Statutes, or,
in the case of sections of Acts enacted after the adoption of
the Kentucky Revised Statutes, with the original section in the
enrolled bill, and that, with the exception of the changes in
form permitted by this section, the sections in the printed
edition are correctly printed. One copy of the edition, with
the original certificate affixed thereto, shall be filed in the
office of the Secretary of State. All other copies of the
edition shall contain a printed copy of the certificate. Any
edition so certified shall constitute prima facie evidence of
the law in all courts and proceedings, and any section in such
edition may be amended or repealed by reference to the sec
tion number, without reference to the legislative Act from
which it was compiled. No compilation of the statute laws of
Kentucky not bearing such a certificate shall be admissable as
evidence of the law in any court or proceeding.
(4) The commission shall keep in its office an accurate
and complete record of all legislation enacted by the General
Assembly and shall study same for the purpose of determining
the intention and the effect thereof. The commission shall pre
pare and keep accurate and complete annotations of court
decisions construing the statutes. The commission shall advise
and assist the members of the General Assembly in the prepa
ration and revision of legislation and matters connected there
with. The commission shall, when requested by the General
Assembly or either house thereof, report to the General As
sembly on bills introduced, calling attention to their effect on
existing legislation and to any deficiencies in their form.

wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. City legislative bodies of cities of the second
class may, by proper legislation, provide for pensions for police
court judges, who after the effective date of this Act, have be
come sixty years of age and have served as a regular police
judge, including service prior to the effective date of this Act,
for a period of twenty years.

for police court judges shall establish a separate fund to be known
as the Police Judge Pension Fund. The regular police court
judge shall contribute into this fund an amount equal to not

contributions to be deducted by the city treasurer from the
monthly pay of such police judge and credited to the Police
Judge Pension Fund. The Police Judge Pension Fund shall be

CHAPTER 54
(S. B. 187)
AN ACT relating to the preservation of bank records.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Preservation of bank records.
A. Every state bank shall retain its business records for
such periods as are or may be prescribed by or in accordance
with the terms of this Section.
B. Each state bank shall retain permanently the minute
book of meetings of its stockholders and directors, its capital
stock ledger and capital stock certificate ledger or stubs, its
general ledger, its daily statements of condition, its general
journal, its investment ledger, its copies of bank examination
reports, and all records which the Commissioner of Banking
shall in accordance with the terms of this Section require to be
retained permanently.
C. All other bank records shall be retained for such periods
as the Commissioner of Banking shall in accordance with the
terms of this Section prescribe.
D. The Commissioner of Banking shall from time to time
issue regulations classifying all records kept by state banks and
prescribing the period for which records of each class shall be
retained. Such periods may be permanent or for a lesser term
of years. Such regulations may from time to time be amended
or repealed. Prior to issuing any such regulation the Commis
sioner of Banking shall consider
(1) Actions at law and administration proceedings in
which the production of bank records might be necessary or de
sirable.

class, and shall thereafter be under no duty to produce such
record in any action or proceeding.
F. Any state bank, including the Department of Bank
ing, may cause any or all records at any time in its custody to
be reproduced by the microphotographic process and any re
production so made shall have the same force and effect as the
original thereof and be admitted in evidence equally with the
original.
G. To the extent that they are not in contravention of any
law of the United States, the provisions of this Section shall
apply to all banks doing business in this State.
Approved March 22, 1954

classification.title: AN ACT relating to child desertion.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 435.240 (3), paragraph (a) of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to define child desertion as a felony. It specifies that a parent who fails to comply with court orders for child maintenance or support after a divorce, or who abandons or deserts a child under sixteen years of age, leaving the child in indigent or destitute circumstances, or who takes the child from the custodial parent without consent, shall be guilty of a felony and subject to imprisonment for one to five years. This provision applies only to judgments, decrees, or orders granted subsequently.

Chapter 57 145
CHAPTER 57
(S. B. 198)
AN ACT relating to zoning and planning in cities of the first class and
counties containing such cities.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Subsection (1) of Section 100.067 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(1) For the aforesaid purposes, the legislative body of such
city and the fiscal court of such county, respectively, are hereby
empowered to regulate, within their respective jurisdictions, the
location, height, bulk, number of stories, and size of buildings
and other structures, the percentage of lot area which may be
occupied by buildings or structures, the size of yards, courts
and other open spaces, building setback lines on streets and
highways, and the future location and width of streets, whether
public or private, and highways, the density of population, the
erection, use and occupancy of buildings or structures, and the
use of land, for trade, industry or residence, or other purposes.
Except as to said setback lines and the future location and width
of streets and highways, the foregoing enumeration of powers
shall not apply to land used solely for agricultural, farming,
dairying, stockraising or similar purposes, nor buildings nor
structures thereon, but if any portion of any such land or any
building or structure on such land is used for commercial or
business purposes then that portion of the land and any such
building or structure shall be subject to all of the above regula
tions. %
Section 2. Subsection (1) of section 100.093 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(1) No person owning land composing a subdivision, or his
agent, shall transfer or sell or agree to sell any lot or parcel of
land located within such subdivision by reference to, or exhibition
of, or by any other use of a plat of such subdivision, before such
plat has been approved, filed and recorded as provided in KRS
100.088 to 100.092. Any such instrument of transfer, sale or
contract shall be void and shall not be subject to be recorded,
but any and all rights of such purchaser to damages, if any, are
hereby preserved.
Approved March 22, 1954

The act also addresses the transfer and sale of subdivision lots, requiring plat approval before sale.

CHAPTER 58
(S. B. 203)
AN ACT relating to public securities and authorizing the execution
thereof by facsimile signatures and seals, providing at least one
signature thereon shall be manually subscribed.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Public securities heretofore or hereafter au
thorized to be issued and delivered at any one time in a principal
amount of Five Million Dollars ($5,000,000) or more may be
executed with an engraved, imprinted, stamped or otherwise
reproduced facsimile of any signature, seal or other means of
authentication, certification or endorsement required or per
mitted to be recorded thereon, if so authorized by the board,
body or officer empowered by law to authorize the issuance of
such securities, provided that at least one signature required or
permitted to be placed thereon shall be manually subscribed.
Section 2. The words "public securities" as used herein
shall mean bonds, notes or other obligations for the payment
of monev issued by this State, by its political subdivisions, or
by any department, agency or other instrumentality of this State
or of anv of its political subdivisions.
Section 3. This Act shall not repeal anv other law author
izing the execution of public securities with facsimile signatures
or seals.
Approved March 22, 1954
CHAPTER 59
(S. B. 204)
AN ACT relating to purchase by fiscal courts, of equipment for sheriffs
and deputies.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
The fiscal court of any county may purchase mobile radio
communication equipment for the use of the sheriff and deputies
of that county if it deems the purchase of such equipment to
be to the best interest of the county, considering the county
finances and other permissible expenditures.
Approved March 24, 1954

Chapter 60 147
CHAPTER 60
(S. B. 219)
AN ACT relating to teachers' tenure.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 161.740 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows:
(1) Teachers eligible for continuing service status in any
school district shall be those teachers who meet qualifications
listed in this section:
(a) Hold a standard or college certificate as defined in
KRS 161.720;
(b) When a teacher is recommended for reemployment
after teaching four years in a district, the superintendent shall
recommend said teacher for a continuing contract, and, unless the
board of education by a four-fifth vote of its full membership
rejects the continuing contract of the teacher, a written con
tinuing contract shall be issued. If the board of education re
jects the continuing contract of the teacher recommended, the
superintendent may recommend employment of such teacher,
under a limited contract for not to exceed two years, but upon
subsequent reemployment only a written continuing contract
shall be entered into.
( c ) When a teacher has attained continuing contract status
in one district and is employed in and transfers to another dis
trict, upon the recommendation of the superintendent the board
of education may at the time of employment, or at any time with
in a two year period, declare any transfer teacher eligible for a
written continuing contract and extend to such teacher such
contract. If during the two year period the teacher has not re
ceived a written continuing contract and the teacher is recom
mended for reemployment at the end of the two year period, the
superintendent shall recommend said teacher for a continuing
contract and if reemployed a written continuing contract shall be
issued.
(d) Any teacher who has taught six years or more and is
eligible for a continuing contract but has not received such
contract shall, if employed for the school year 1955-56, receive
a written continuing contract.

CHAPTER 61
(S. B. 226)
AN ACT relating to the number of retail package liquor licenses and
retail drink licenses permitted in cities of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The number of retail package liquor licenses
issued by the Alcoholic Beverage Control Board to licensees in
counties containing cities of the first class, and including such
cities, shall not exceed a number equal to one for every one
thousand five hundred persons resident in such county.
Section 2. The number of retail drink licenses issued by
the Alcoholic Beverage Control Board to licensees in counties
containing cities of the first class, and including such cities shall
not exceed a number equal to one for every one thousand five
hundred persons resident in such countv.
Section 3. In order that a fixed and approved standard of
population as prescribed in Sections 1 and 2 of this Act may be
adopted the annual estimates of population as determined by
chambers of commerce of cities of the first class shall be used in
every year except a census year, and during a census year the
United States Government census figures of population shall be
controlling.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 62 149
CHAPTER 62
(S. B. 227)
AN ACT relating to location of retail package liquor and retail drink
licenses in cities of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The State Alcoholic Beverage Control Board
shall, for the purpose of regulating the location of retail package
liquor and retail drink licenses in cities of the first class, divide
such cities into "down-town business areas" and "combination
business and residential areas."
Section 2. No retail package liquor or retail drink license
shall be granted or issued to any licensee who proposes to sell
retail package liquor or liquor by the drink at a location with
in seven hundred feet of the location of any similar establish
ment in any combination business and residential area, nor
shall such license be granted or issued to any licensee who pro
poses to operate at a location in a combination business and
residential area within seven hundred feet of a similar estab
lishment located in a down-town business area. This Act shall
not affect location of such establishments in down-town busi
ness areas of such cities.
Section 3. The distance between location of similar
establishments as prescribed by this Act shall be measured by
following the shortest route of ordinary pedestrian travel along
public thoroughfares from the nearest point of any present lo
cation of any such similar place of business to the nearest point
of any proposed location of any such place of business. The
measurement shall be taken from the entrance of the existing
licensed premises to the entrance of any proposed location.
Section 4. The location of all establishments licensed to
sell at retail distilled spirits by the package or bv the drink, or
both, on the effective date of this Act shall not be affected by
the terms of this Act and this Act shall not apply to existing
licensed locations or to the renewal of licenses therefor, or to
transfers thereof. The distance limitation prescribed by this
Act shall not affect any existing licensed location, nor the right
of the owner thereof to renew or transfer the license for such
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 170

AN ACT relating to prior service credit for Teachers Retirement pur
poses for veterans of World War L
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Any honorably discharged veteran of World War I who is
a member of the Teachers Retirement System in good standing,
may receive prior service credit for such military service, not
to exceed an enlistment of four years, upon presenting his dis
charge papers or a certified photostatic copy of the same to
the Board of Trustees of the Teachers Retirement System and
their acceptance of this or other sufficient evidence as to the
validity of the military service presented for credit. In no case
shall any member receive credit for more than thirty years of
prior service including any military service accredited.
Became law without Governor's signature March 31, 1954
CHAPTER 65
(S. B. 231)
AN ACT relating to eggs and the marketing thereof.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The functions assigned by the provisions of
KRS 217.220, 217.240 and 217.250 to the Department of Health
relating to egg marketing shall be transferred to and performed
by the Department of Agriculture, Labor and Statistics. Any
unexpended funds to the credit of the Department of Health
for use in carrying out the provisions of KRS 217.220 to KRS
217.250 shall be transferred to the credit of the Department
of Agriculture, Labor and Statistics for the same use.
Section 2. This Act shall become effective April 1, 1955.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 67 155
CHAPTER 66
(S. B. 242)
AN ACT relating to probation and parole.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 439. 150 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
Prisoners paroled from a state penal institution shall at all
times during the continuance of the parole be under the control
and supervision of and subject to the orders, rules and directions
of and in the legal custody of the Department.
The Department shall have the authority to give the paroled
prisoners a final release from parole and shall issue to the parolee
who has satisfactorily completed his period of parole, a certifi
cate of discharge. After final discharge has been issued by the
Department the parolee may not be held again for violation
of that parole.
Approved March 22, 1954
CHAPTER 67
(S. B. 243)
AN ACT relating to probation and parole.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 439.030 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
When directed by the court, a probation officer shall fully
investigate and report to the court in writing the circumstances
of the offenses, criminal record, social history and present
condition of the defendant. No defendant charged with a felony
shall be placed on probation or released under the post-ponement
of rendition of judgment until the report of the investigation
has been presented to and considered by the court. Whenever
practicable the investigation shall include physical and mental
examinations of the defendant. The court shall direct that de
fendants placed on probation shall be under the supervision
of the Division of Probation and Parole.
Approved March 22, 1954

CHAPTER 73
(H. B. 15)
AN ACT authorizing and adding one additional judge to the circuit court
for the Seventeenth Circuit Court District; providing for the ap
pointment and election of the additional judge to said circuit court;
and providing for the manner in which said court shall be held
and the business therein conducted.
WHEREAS, the population of the Seventeenth Circuit Court District,
embracing the County of Campbell, now exceeds seventy-five
thousand inhabitants; and
WHEREAS, said District and County has a city of more than twenty
thousand inhabitants; and
WHEREAS, said District is entitled to an additional circuit judge;
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That one additional judge of the circuit court
for the Seventeenth Circuit Court District is hereby authorized
and added to said court.
Section 2. (1) Within ninety days after this Act shall have
become operative, the Governor shall appoint a person having the
qualifications of a circuit judge to preside over the Common Law
and Equity Division of the Seventeenth Circuit Court District,
who, when appointed, shall be commissioned by the Governor
as judge of the Common Law and Equity Division of the Seven
teenth Circuit Court District, and shall enter upon the duties
of the office as soon as he shall have qualified, and shall con
tinue in office until the first Monday in January, 1956, and until
his successor is elected, and shall have qualified.
(2) At the regular election to be held on the first Tuesdav
after the first Monday in November, 1955, and every six years
thereafter, such additional judge shall be elected, as other circuit
court judges are required to be elected, and shall enter upon the
duties of the office on the first Monday in January next succeed
ing his election.
Section 3. The court in the Seventeenth Circuit Court
District shall be held in continuous session and the business
therein shall be conducted in the manner now provided by law
for other circuit courts of continuous session having two judges.
Approved March 1, 1954

Chapter 74 163
CHAPTER 74
(H. B. 16)
AN ACT relating to operation of motor vehicles while under the influ
ence of intoxicating liquor or narcotic drugs and amending section
189.520 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 189.520 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is hereby
amended to read as follows:
(1) No person under the influence of intoxicating liquors
or narcotic drugs shall operate a vehicle that is not a motor
vehicle upon a highway.
(2) No person shall operate a motor vehicle on a highway
while under the influence of intoxicating liquors or narcotic
drugs.
(3) No peace officer or Highway Patrolman shall fail to
enforce rigidly this section.
(4) In any criminal prosecution for a violation of sub
sections (1) or (2) of this section, wherein the defendant is
charged with having operated a vehicle while under the in
fluence of intoxicating liquor, the amount of alcohol in the
defendant's blood, as determined at the time of making a
chemical analysis of his blood, urine, breath or other bodily
substance, shall give rise to the following presumptions:
(a) If there was 0.05 percent or less by weight of alcohol
in such blood, it shall be presumed that the defendant was not
under the influence of intoxicating liquor;
(b) If there was more than 0.05 percent, but less than
0.15 percent by weight of alcohol in such blood, such fact
shall not constitute a presumption that the defendant either
was or was not under the influence of intoxicating liquor, but
such fact may be considered, together with other competent
evidence, in determining the guilt or innocence of the defendant;
(c) If there was 0.15 percent or more by weight of alcohol
in such blood, it shall be presumed that the defendant was
under the influence of intoxicating liquor.
(5) The provisions of subsection (4) of this section shall
not be construed as limiting the introduction of any other com

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 138.510 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows :
"An excise tax is imposed on every person, corporation or
association which operates a running or trotting horse race track
at which betting is conducted through a pari-mutuel or other
similar system. The tax shall be an amount equal to four per
cent of the first eighteen million dollars wagered during the year,
and six per cent of all money in excess of eighteen million dollars
wagered during the year. A year as used herein shall begin at
12:01 A. M. July 1 and end at 12 midnight June 30."

classification.title: AN ACT relating to pari-mutuel and other similar systems of betting, including the taxation thereof.
classification.summary: This act amends Section 138.510 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes to impose an excise tax on operators of horse race tracks with pari-mutuel betting. The tax is 4% on the first eighteen million dollars wagered annually and 6% on amounts exceeding eighteen million dollars. The act also imposes a floor stocks tax on malt beverages and outlines penalties for non-payment and violations. It specifies an effective date of July 1, 1954.

Section 2. A new section of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is created to read as follows :
"The commission, including the tax levied in Section 1, de

or association which operates a running horse race track at
which betting is conducted through a pari-mutuel or other similar

handled, and the breaks, which breaks shall be made and cal

CHAPTER 77
(H. B. 19)
AN ACT relating to excise tobacco taxes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Sec. 1. Subsection (1) of Section 138.140 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(1) A tax shall be paid on the sale of cigarettes made of
tobacco, or any substitute for tobacco, at the rate of three cents
on each twenty cigarettes or fraction thereof. This tax shall
be paid only once, regardless of the number of times the
cigarettes may be sold in this state.
Sec. 2. Section 138.150 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Cigarette tax stamps shall be purchased only from the
Department of Revenue. The department may prescribe rules
and regulations governing their sale and distribution.
(2) The department shall prescribe the forms of the
cigarette tax stamps, and shall have the stamps prepared for
sale in such a manner as it deems proper.
(3) The stamps shall be sold at their face value but the
department shall allow each wholesaler required or authorized
by the department to purchase and affix stamps, for each three
dollars' worth of stamps purchased at their face value and

affixed by the wholesaler, an amount of stamps equal to

quire. This allowance shall be made only when the department

Sec. 3. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
(1) Upon all cigarettes upon which the tax imposed by
subsection (1) of KRS 138.140 as such statute existed on
January 1, 1954, has been paid, and which on July 1, 1954, are
held and intended for sale, there shall be levied, assessed, col
lected and paid a "floor stocks" tax at rates equal to three cents
on each twenty cigarettes or fraction thereof, less the credits
allowed under subsection (2) of this section.
(2) The tax levied under subsection (1) of this section shall
be credited with the amount of taxes paid pursuant to sub
section (1) of KRS 138.140 as such statute existed on January 1,
1954, on cigarettes subject to the tax levied by subsection ( 1 ) of
this section.
(3) Under such regulations as the Department of Revenue
shall prescribe, every person required by subsection (1) of this
section to pay any floor stocks tax shall on or before July 15, 1954,
make a return and pay such tax to the Department of Revenue.
Payment of the tax shown to be due may be extended to a date
not later than September 1, 1954, under such regulations as the
Department of Revenue shall prescribe.
(4) Any person who fails to pay the tax levied by this
section at the time prescribed in subsection (3) of this section
for payment shall pay a penalty of twenty percent of the amount
of tax due. Such penalty shall be assessed by the Department of
Revenue and may be reviewed only as provided in KRS 131.110
and KRS 131.120.
(5) Any person who violates any of the provisions of this
section shall be fined not less than fifty dollars nor more than
one thousand dollars or be imprisoned for not less than thirty
days nor more than one year, or both.
Sec. 4. This Act shall become effective on July 1, 1954.
Approved January 26, 1954

Chapter 79 203
CHAPTER 79
(H. B. 21)
AN ACT Relating to Income Taxes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 141.010 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
As used in this chapter, unless the context requires other
wise:
( 1 ) "Commissioner" means the Commissioner of Revenue.
(2) "Department" means the Department of Revenue.
(3) "Internal Revenue Code" means the Internal Revenue
Code in effect on January 1, 1954.
(4) "Dependent" means those persons defined as depend
ents under Section 25 (b) (3) of the Internal Revenue Code.
(5) "Fiduciary" means a guardian, trustee, executor, ad
ministrator, receiver, conservator, or any person acting in any
fiduciary capacity for any person, estate or trust.
(6) "Fiscal year" means an accounting period of twelve
months ending on the last day of any month other than De
cember.
(7) "Individual" means a natural person.
(8) "Federal income tax" means income tax paid or ac
crued to the United States Government on income subject to
assessment and taxation under the provisions of this chapter.
(9) "Net income," in the case of taxpayers other than
corporations, means the same as net income defined under Sec
tion 21 of the Internal Revenue Code adjusted as follows:
(a) Exclude income that is exempt from state taxation by the
Constitution and statutory laws of the United States.
( b ) Exclude dividend income received during the taxable
year upon stock of banks and trust companies organized
under the laws of this state.
(c) Deduct "Federal income tax."
( d ) Ignore the "net operating loss deduction."
(e) Include interest income derived from obligations of
sister states and political subdivisions thereof.

220 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 15. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is created to be numbered KRS 141.205 and to read as
follows:
(1) The department shall permit or require any parent
corporation or subsidiary corporation doing business within
this state to file a consolidated return covering the entire
operations of the parent corporation and its subsidiaries, when
ever it finds that the inter-corporate transactions of the related
group tend to reduce the net income of the corporation, or
corporations, doing business within this state below the amount
that would probably result if such corporation, or corporations,
was not a member of the related group.
(2) The department is authorized and empowered, in
such manner as it may determine, to assess the tax against any
of the corporations whose net income is involved in the con
solidated return upon the basis of the combined net income
and such other information as it may possess, or it may adjust
the tax in such other manner as it shall determine necessary
to prevent avoidance of taxes or to clearly reflect the net income
earned by such corporation or corporations from property located
and business done within this state.
(3) Any corporation owning or controlling either directly
or indirectly ninety percent or more of the voting stock of
another corporation shall be deemed a parent corporation for
purposes of this section. Any corporation having a parent corpora
tion shall be deemed a subsidiary corporation for purposes of
this section. Corporations having a common parent shall be con
sidered members of a related group.
(4) If the consolidated return required under this section
is not filed within sixty days after demand by the department,
said parent corporation or subsidiary corporation or both, shall
be subject to the penalty provided in this chapter for failure to
file returns.
(5) In the case of a corporation doing business in this
state and not permitted or required to file a consolidated return
under subsection ( 1 ) of this section that carries on transactions
with stockholders, directly or indirectly, the department shall
adjust the net income of such corporation to an amount that
would result if such transactions were carried on at arm's length.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 241

classification.title: KRS 141.205 - Consolidated Tax Returns for Corporations

It grants the department authority to assess taxes based on combined net income and to adjust taxes to prevent tax avoidance or accurately reflect income earned within Kentucky. It defines parent and subsidiary corporations based on stock ownership and outlines penalties for failure to file consolidated returns upon demand. It also addresses transactions between corporations and their stockholders.

Section 16. Section 141.210 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) As soon as practicable after each return is received,
the department shall examine and audit it. If the amount of
tax computed by the department is greater than the amount
returned by the taxpayer, the excess shall be assessed by the
department within four years from the date the return was filed,
except as provided in subsection (2) of this section, and except
that in the case of a failure to file a return or of a fraudulent
return the excess may be assessed at any time and in the case
of a return wherein the taxpayer omits from net income an
amount properly includable therein which is in excess of twenty-
five percent of the amount of net income stated in the return
the excess may be assessed at any time within six years after
the return was filed; provided, however, that if any excess is
assessed on account of any income which has been returned
for taxation by any other taxpayer, the department, with the
consent of such other taxpayer, his personal representatives or
heirs, shall reduce the amount of the excess assessed for each
year by the amount of the income tax paid for that year by such
other taxpayer on account of the income in question. A notice
of such assessment shall be mailed to the taxpayer. The time
herein provided may be extended by agreement between the
taxpayer and the department.
(2) For the purpose of subsection (1) of this section a
return filed before the last day prescribed by law for the filing
thereof shall be considered as filed on such last day.
Section 17. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is created to be numbered KRS 141.206 and to read as
follows :
(1) Every partnership owning property or engaging in
business in Kentucky, shall, on or before the fifteenth day of the
fourth month following the close of its annual accounting period,
file a report or return on such form as may be prescribed and
furnished by the department.
( 2 ) Individuals carrying on a business in partnership shall
be liable for income tax only in their individual capacities, and
no income tax shall be assessed upon the net income of any
partnership.

shall be paid as follows:
(a) If the declaration is filed on or before April 15 of the

(c) If the declaration is filed after July 15 and not after
November 15 of the taxable year and is not required by
subsection (3) of KRS 141.300 to be filed on or before July 15
of the taxable year, the estimated tax shall be paid at the
time of the filing of the declaration.
(d) If the declaration is filed after November 15 of the
taxable year, and is not required by subsection (3) of KRS
141.300 to be filed on or before November 15 of the taxable
year, the estimated tax shall be paid in full at the time of

be that applicable in the case of a miscellaneous payroll period
containing a number of days, including Sundays and holidays,
equal to the number of days in the period with respect to which
such wages are paid.

case of a miscellaneous payroll period containing a number of
days equal to the number of days, including Sundays and holi

performed during more than one-half of any such payroll period

deemed to be wages.

Section 29. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to be numbered KRS 141.345 and to read as follows:
(1) Where there has been an overpayment of tax under
KRS 141.310 or KRS 141.315, refund or credit shall be made
to the employer only to the extent that the amount of such over
payment was not deducted and withheld under KRS 141.310 or
KRS 141.315 by the employer.
(2) Unless written application for refund or credit is re
ceived by the department from the employer within two years
from the date the overpayment was made, no refund or credit
shall be allowed.
Section 30. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to be numbered KRS 141.350 and to read as follows:
The amount deducted and withheld as tax under KRS
141.310 and KRS 141.315 during any calendar year upon the
wages of any individual shall be allowed as a credit to the
recipient of the income against the tax imposed by KRS 141.020,
for taxable years beginning in such calendar year. If more than
one taxable year begins in such calendar year such amount shall
be allowed as a credit against the tax for the last taxable year
so beginning.
Section 31. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to be numbered KRS 141.355 and to read as follows:
(1) Where there has been an overpayment of any tax im
posed under KRS 141.020, KRS 141.310 or KRS 141.315, the
amount of such overpayment shall be credited against any income
tax or installment thereof then due from the taxpayer, and any
balance shall be refunded in the manner provided in KRS
141.235.
(2) Where the amount of the tax withheld at the source
under KRS 141.310 or KRS 141.315 exceeds the taxes imposed
by KRS 141.020 against which the tax so withheld may be
credited under KRS 141.350, the amount of such excess shall be
considered an overpayment.
Section 32. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes

Unless the tax levied under KRS 141.310 is determined

Section 33. Section 141.990 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) If any taxpayer fails or refuses to file a return or
furnish any information requested in writing by the department,
the department may make an estimate of the net income or
adjusted net income from any information in its possession,
assess the tax at not more than twice the amount estimated to
be due and add a penalty of twenty-five percent of the amount
of any deficiency tax assessed concerning such failure or refusal
to file a return or furnish any information. This penalty shall
be in addition to all other applicable penalties in this section.
(2) If any taxpayer fails to make and file a return re
quired by this chapter on or before the due date of the return
or the due date as extended by the department, then, unless it
is shown to the satisfaction of the department that the failure
is due to reasonable cause, five percent of the tax found to be
due by the department shall be added to the tax for each thirty
days or fraction thereof elapsing between the due date of the
return and the date on which filed, but the total penalty shall
not exceed twenty-five percent of the tax.
(3) If any part of any deficiency is due to negligence,
or intentional disregard of rules and regulations but without
intent to defraud, five percent of the total amount of the de
ficiency, in addition to the deficiency and other penalties pro
vided in this section, shall apply.
(4) If any part of any deficiency is due to fraud with
intent to evade tax, fifty percent of the total amount of the
deficiency, in addition to the deficiency and other penalties

Section 2. WHEREAS, at the 1948 General Assembly,
enactment of subsection (4) of KRS 92.281 made said section
subject to the provisions of KRS 91.200; and
WHEREAS, the provisions of KRS 91.200 are only ap
plicable to cities of the first class and are not applicable to cities

ply to any city other than a city of the first class; and
WHEREAS, some confusion has arisen concerning the ap
plicability of said subsection (4) of KRS 92.281, as enacted in
1948, as a result of which the right and power of cities, other
than cities of the first class, to levy taxes under this section and
to apportion, allocate and expend such taxes, without the neces
sity and requirement that same be paid into a sinking fund, has
been questioned to the extent that the status and financial
structures of all cities other than cities of the first class are
vitally affected; and
WHEREAS, it is imperative that the aforementioned con
fusion be eliminated in order that such cities may properly plan,
establish and make provision for the imposition and collection
of taxes necessary to properly administer the affairs of the cities;
which, under the law, must now be planned and provided for;
An emergency is hereby declared to exist and this Act shall
become effective immediately upon its passage and approval by
the Governor.

Chapter 82 247
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 95.710 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
To be eligible for appointment as a member of the police
force or fire department of a city of the fourth or fifth class, a
person must be a citizen of the state, a resident of the county for
at least six months, at least twenty-one years of age, able to read
and write the English language intelligibly, and sober, moral and
sagacious. No person who has been convicted of a felony shall
be eligible for appointment. After appointment no member shall
interfere in any election. If it is possible to secure such a person,
the appointee shall have been a resident of the city for at least six
months in addition to the other required qualifications; but should
the city legislative body determine a person meeting this added
qualification cannot be secured, a residence of six months in the
county shall be sufficient.
Approved February 24, 1954

park to be known as The Breaks Interstate Park, which shall

of such area and of such character as may be determined by the
commission created by this Compact.
Article II.
There is hereby created The Breaks Interstate Park Com
mission, which shall be a body corporate with the powers and
duties set forth herein and such additional powers as may be
conferred upon it by subsequent action of the appropriate au
thorities of Kentucky and Virginia. The commission shall con
sist of three commissioners from each of the two states, each
of whom shall be a citizen of the state he shall represent. Mem
bers of the commission shall be appointed by the Governor.
Vacancies shall be filled by the Governor for the unexpired
term. The term of one of the first commissioners appointed shall
be for two years, the term of another for three years, and the
term of the third for four years. Their successors shall be ap
pointed for terms of four years each. Each commissioner shall
hold office until his successor is appointed and qualified. An
officer or employe of the state, a political subdivision or the
United States government mav be appointed a commissioner
under this act.

instrumentality of both the Commonwealth of Kentucky and
the Commnowealth of Virginia for the purpose of effecting the
objects of this Compact, and shall be deemed to be performing
governmental functions of the two states in the performance
of its duties hereunder. The commission shall have power to

CHAPTER 83
(H. B. 28)
AN ACT relating to special circuit judges of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. There is hereby created the office of special
circuit judge of the Commonwealth of Kentucky.
Section 2. Each person who, after the effective date of
this Act, has served as a regular circuit judge in this Common
wealth and whose total service as a regular circuit judge, in
cluding service prior to the effective date of this Act, is as much
as ten years, and who has contributed for a minimum of two
years to the special circuit judge fund herein provided for, and
who has reached his or her sixtieth birthday shall be eligible
for appointment as a special circuit judge of the Commonwealth.
Section 3. Application for appointment as a special circuit
judge shall be made to the Court of Appeals upon such forms and
in such manner as may be prescribed by the Court of Appeals.
The Court of Appeals shall appoint as a special circuit judge each
person eligible therefor.
Section 4. Special circuit judges shall serve as special
judges and perform such other duties as may be assigned them
by the Court of Appeals or by the Chief Justice, and shall not
hold any other public office or otherwise engage in the practice
of law during their tenure as special circuit judges. Should any
special circuit judge fail or neglect, without cause, to perform
any duty assigned to him, the Court of Appeals shall forthwith
remove him from his office by entering an order on its record.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 272

ury a separate fund to be known as the special circuit judge
fund. Each regular circuit judge shall contribute into this fund
an amount equal to two percent of his annual salary as a circuit
judge paid by the state, such contribution to be deducted by the
State Treasurer from the monthly pay of each circuit judge
and credited in the special circuit judge fund to such circuit
judge.
Section 7. The special circuit judge fund shall be under
the exclusive control and management of a committee composed
of a Justice or Commissioner of the Court of Appeals to be
designated by the Chief Justice, three circuit judges to be selected
by the circuit judges, and a member of the Kentucky Bar Asso
ciation designated by the president of that organization. The
terms of all members shall be for four years, beginning on July 1,
1954, and until their successors are appointed and qualify. Va
cancies shall be filled by the original appointive or elective au

(2) Any person entitled to reemployment under the pro
visions of this section who, upon application, is not reemployed
as required by this section shall have a civil action for damages
against the officer having the power of appointment to such
position, and said action shall be brought in the courts of the
county where the office of the person violating this section is
located. In addition to the civil liability, any officer having the
power of appointment to a position in which any person is en
titled to reemployment under this section, who fails, upon due
application, to make such reemployment, shall be fined not less
than one hundred dollars nor more than five hundred dollars, in
the courts of the county where the offense is committed.
(3) This section is not intended to require the reemploy
ment of any person unless such person makes application for
reemployment within three months after his discharge from
service in the Armed Forces nor unless such person is physically
able to perform the duties of the position in which he seeks re
employment.
( 4 ) "Person" as used in this section shall mean and include
all employes other than heads of departments and those persons
employed in an emergency, provisional temporary or probational
status at the time of entering the military service.

It clarifies the city's liability for unpaid bonds and interest, stating the city is only liable to the extent of collected funds unless a balance remains, in which case it becomes personally liable and must levy general taxes or issue funding bonds. It also establishes a 30-day statute of limitations for challenging the validity of bond ordinances and states that any municipality exercising powers under KRS 94.290 through 94.325 and any person accepting benefits are estopped from questioning the validity of these provisions. The act is declared severable.

CHAPTER 87
(H. B. 35)
AN ACT relating to members of city council and city officers in cities
of the fourth class, and amending subsection (1) of Section 86.050 of
the Kentucky Revised Statutes and Section 86.230 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Subsection (1) of Section 86.050 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is hereby amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Members of the city council shall be elected for terms
of two years.
Section 2. Section 86.230 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
All city officers, including members of the city council,
shall take office on the first Monday in January following their
election or appointment. Any officer appointed by the city coun
cil shall be appointed by the council elected at the November
election preceding the commencement of his term.
Approved February 15, 1954
CHAPTER 88
(H. B. 45)
AN ACT relating to supervisors of taxes in cities of the fourth class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 92.260 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is hereby amended to read as follows:
The city council of each city of the fourth class shall each
year appoint three discreet freeholders as supervisors of taxes,
and fix their compensation. The supervisors shall qualify by tak
ing oath faithfully to perform the duties of their office without
favor to rich or poor. They shall select a chairman by vote or lot,
and he or either of the supervisors may administer an oath to tell
the whole truth concerning his taxable property to any person
appearing to have an assessment corrected.

WHEREAS, on January 1, 1954, there were pending in the Pike circuit
court, 440 criminal cases, 587 ordinary actions filed prior to July 1,
1953, 640 equity actions filed prior to July 1, 1953, and 274 civil
actions filed between July 1, 1953, and December 31, 1953, under
the new rules of civil procedure, making a total of 1,941 cases
pending on the docket of the Pike circuit court as of January 1,
1954; and
WHEREAS, the General Assembly, giving due regard to the elements
of territory, business, population and to the right of all persons to
a speedy trial, as provided by the Constitution of this Common
wealth, finds from the aforesaid facts that an additional judge is
necessary in said county and judicial district.
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. One additional judge of the circuit court for
the 35th Circuit Court District is hereby authorized and added to
said court.
Section 2. The circuit court of said 35th Circuit Court
District shall be divided into two divisions. One of the divisions
shall be known and designated as the criminal division, and the
other shall be known and designated as the civil division which
shall be divided into jury and non-jury cases under the rules of
civil procedure adopted by the Kentucky Court of Appeals and
effective July 1, 1953. The judge of the court at the time the
additional judge is provided shall be judge of the civil division,
divided into jury and non-jury cases, and the additional judge
shall be judge of the criminal division and in addition thereto
shall be, together with the present judge of said judicial district,
judge of the civil division, divided into jury and non-jury cases.
Section 3. Within thirty days after this Act shall have be
come effective, the Governor shall appoint a person having the
qualifications of a circuit judge to preside over the criminal di
vision and the civil division, divided into jury and non-jury cases,
of the 35th Circuit Court District, who, when appointed, shall
be commissioned by the Governor as judge of the criminal
division and civil division of the 35th Circuit Court District, and
shall enter upon the duties of the office as soon as he shall have
qualified and shall continue in office until his successor is elected
and qualified as herein provided. At the regular election to be

CHAPTER 90

AN ACT relating to the establishment of a penalty for violation of
Section 277.245 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Any owner or operator of a railroad running through or
within this state as a common carrier of persons or property or
both, for compensation, who either operates for its employes,
or who furnishes to its employes for their transportation to or
from the place or places where they are required to labor, a rail
track motor car that has not been fully equipped as required by
KRS 277.245, shall be fined not less than one hundred dollars,
nor more than five hundred dollars for each offense and each
day or part of a day it operates or furnishes each of such rail
track motor cars not so equipped as provided in KRS 277.245
to its employes for operation to or from the place or places
where they are required to work shall constitute a separate
offense.

CHAPTER 91
(H. B. 61)
AN ACT relating to compensation for court reporters in circuit courts of
continuous session in counties having a population of less than
150,000, and in circuit courts not of continuous session in counties
containing a city of the second class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Subsection (2) of Section 28.450 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(2) For making original transcripts reporters shall receive
thirty cents per hundred words. In civil actions involving less
than twenty-five hundred dollars they shall receive ten dollars
per day for taking stenographic notes of said actions and thirty
cents per hundred words for the original transcript thereof, which
shall be paid forthwith by the party in whose behalf the record
or transcript is ordered, and same shall be taxed as part of the
costs of the action. In criminal cases the fee for making tran
scripts shall be paid by the county in which the court is held
when the transcript is made upon the motion of the Common
wealth. If the transcript is made upon motion of the defendant
in a criminal case, he shall pay the fee therefor, unless the court
directs the reporter to file a transcript upon the motion of the
party suing in forma pauperis, in which event the fee shall be
paid by the county. In cases where the offense is a misdemeanor
the party asking for the reporter's service shall pay for the same
at the rate of ten dollars per day.
Approved March 9, 1954

Chapter 92 265
ing control sufficient to cover the taxable property on the rolls.
These tax bill forms shall be furnished not later than ten days
after the Kentucky Tax Commission has rendered it certifica
tion. The county annually shall furnish the county clerk with
sufficient tax bill forms for his use in the preparation of tax
bills for all taxpayers who are liable for a poll tax and who own
no property assessed for taxation.
( 2 ) After receiving the forms the county clerk shall make
out for the use of the sheriff or collector a correct tax bill for
each taxpayer in the county whose property has been assessed
and whose valuation is included in the certification provided in
KRS 133.180. If the bills are bound the cost of binding shall
be paid out of the county levy. Each tax bill shall show the rate
of tax upon each one hundred dollars worth of property for state,
county and school purposes; the name of the taxpayer and his
mailing address; the number of acres of farm land and its value;
the number of lots and their value; the amount and value of
notes and money; the value of mixed personal property; the
county poll; the school poll; the school tax; and the total amount
of taxes due the state, county, school fund and other levies. The
county clerk shall attest each bill by signing it officially. Pro
vision shall be made for the sheriff to have a stub, duplicate
or other proper evidence of receipt of payment of each tax bill.
( 3 ) Tax bills prepared in accordance with the certification
of the Kentucky Tax Commission shall be delivered to the sheriff
or collector by the county clerk before September 15 of each
year. The clerk shall take a receipt showing the number of tax
bills and the total amount of tax due each taxing district as
shown upon the tax bills. The receipt shall be signed and ac
knowledged by the sheriff or collector before the county clerk,
filed in the county court, and recorded in the order book of the
county court in the manner required by law for recording the
official bond of the sheriff.
(4) Upon delivery to him of the tax bills, the sheriff or
collector shall mail a notice to each taxpayer, showing the total
amount of taxes due the state, county, school fund and other
levies, the date on which the taxes are due, and any discount
to which the taxpayer may be entitled upon payment of the taxes
prior to a designated date.
Approved February 26, 1954

ing Section 23.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.

wealth of Kentucky:

inal and penal cases shall be heard at each of the terms, unless

1. Every motor carrier shall file with the Department of

maximum amount of not more than $10,000 on a form and in

Section 9. The tax imposed by subsection (1) of sec
tion 3 of this Act, except as otherwise provided herein, shall be
determined as follows:
1. The total number of gallons of gasoline used during the
taxable period in the motor carrier's operations on the public
highways shall be multiplied by the proportion that its miles
operated in Kentucky during such period bears to its total miles
operated both within and without Kentucky during such period.
The resulting figure shall be deemed the number of gallons of
gasoline used during such period in its Kentucky operations;
2. The number of gallons used within Kentucky, de
termined as provided in subsection (1) of this section, shall be
multiplied by seven cents;
3. Tax shall be paid on the amount determined under
subsection (2) of this section, after allowance of the credits set
forth in section 10 of this Act.
Section 10.
1. Every motor carrier subject to the tax imposed by sub
section ( 1 ) of section 3 of this Act shall be entitled to a credit
for each period equivalent to seven cents per gallon on gasoline
purchased by such carrier during such period for use in its
operations on public highways within and without this state,
provided such gasoline was purchased in Kentucky during the
same period and the tax imposed by KRS 138.220 has been paid.
Evidence of the payment of such tax in such form as may be
required by or satisfactory to the department shall be furnished
by each such motor carrier claiming the credit herein allowed.
2. The department shall at the close of each quarterly
period, ending September 30, December 31, March 31, and
June 30, compute all credits granted by the department during
such quarter, which credits shall be applied only to taxes due
on any report filed within the succeeding quarter.

AN ACT relating to the National Guard of Kentucky.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 38.010 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Definitions as used in this chapter, unless the context re
quires otherwise, are as follows:
(1) "Kentucky National Guard" includes the Army Na
tional Guard and Air National Guard;
(2) "Active Army National Guard and Active Air National
Guard" includes those organizations, officers and enlisted men
of the Federally recognized Kentucky National Guard author
ized to receive Federal pay, arms and equipment;
(3) "Inactive Army National Guard" and "Inactive Air
National Guard" includes those organizations, officers and en
listed men of the Kentucky National Guard not entitled to re
ceive Federal pay, arms and equipment;
(4) "Active State Service" is:
(a) The ordering by the Governor of any unit or units of
the Kentucky National Guard to enforce the laws of the Com
monwealth; resist an actual or threatened invasion or insurrec
tion; quell a riot or other domestic disturbance; or preserve and
protect the rights, lives, or property of citizens of the Common
wealth;
(b) Officers, warrant officers and enlisted men employed
under orders of the Governor in making tours of inspection,
mustering in or mustering out troops, making surveys of military
property, sitting on courts-martial, summary courts, efficiency
boards, courts of inquiry or boards of officers;
(c) The participation of any unit or units of the Kentucky
National Guard in gunnery competition anywhere within or with
out the United States.
Section 2. Section 38.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The Kentucky National Guard shall consist of the
officers, warrant officers, enlisted men and units as prescribed

Section 7. Section 38.110 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
An officer of the Kentucky National Guard shall hold his
commission so long as he is entitled to receive Federal pay and
allowances unless retired prior to that time by reason of resigna
tion, disability or for cause to be determined by a court-martial
or efficiency board legally convened for that purpose.
Section 8. Section 38.120 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
A commissioned officer tendering his resignation, if the Gov
ernor accepts it, shall receive an honorable discharge if he is not
under arrest or to be returned to a military court for deficiency
or delinquency. The Governor may not accept the resignation
of an officer of the Kentucky National Guard who has been
charged with an offense under the Kentucky Military Code of
Justice which is tantamount to a felony under Kentucky criminal
law.
Section 9. Section 38.130 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
The Governor may order any commissioned officer before
an efficiency board consisting of three officers senior in rank to
the officer whose fitness for service is under examination. The
board shall have the powers of courts of inquiry and courts-mar
tial and shall inquire into the moral character, capacity and gen
eral fitness for the service of the commissioned officer, and record
and return the testimony taken and a record of the proceedings.
If the findings of the board are unfavorable to the officer and are
approved by the Governor, he shall be discharged from the serv
ice. No officer shall participate in the examination or decision
of the board in any case in which the decision would affect his

Section 14. Section 38.220 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
For service and attendance upon boards appointed by the
Governor, general courts-martial and courts of inquiry as a
member, judge advocate, recorder or witness, or upon inspection
or other duty ordered by the Governor, officers and enlisted men,
when not engaged on duty for which pay is provided, shall re
ceive the same pay and allowances as is provided for active state
service.
-Section 15. Section 38.240 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Any member of the National Guard who is incapacitated
from pursuing his usual business or occupation by reason of any
injury received or disease contracted when on duty or assembled
therefore under lawful authority, which injury or disease directly
results from such service, shall be entitled to receive the pay
provided by KRS 38.200 and actual necessary expenses for care,
hospitalization and medical attendance during the period of such
incapacity. Where a claim is made under this section, the Adju
tant General shall, from time to time, designate medical officers
to examine the claimant, and may direct the claimant to be re
moved to and treated in a designated hospital. If the claimant
refuses to permit the examination, or to go to the hospital or to
follow the advice given or treatment prescribed for him therein,
he shall forfeit his right to any claim, pay or allowance under this
section.
(2) If the death of any member of the National Guard results
from an injury received or disease contracted when on duty or
assembled therefor under lawful authority, the state shall pay his
funeral expenses, but not to exceed two hundred and fifty dollars.
(3) The body of an officer or enlisted man who dies while in
active state service, shall, under the direction of his commanding
officer and at the expense of the state, be promptly prepared for
burial, enclosed in a suitable casket, and returned to his late resi
dence under a proper escort; but the cost to the state of the casket
and preparing the body for burial shall not exceed the sum of two
hundred and fifty dollars.

semble for drill and instructions not less than forty-eight times

Section 18. Section 38.290 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) Premises occupied by the Kentucky National Guard for
military purposes shall be under the control of the commanding
officer using them.
(2) All military property furnished for the use of the several
organizations of the Kentucky National Guard shall be kept in
the armory or storerooms of the command, except when in use for
authorized military purposes.
Section 19. Section 38.310 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
No military property furnished by the United States, through
the agency of the state, to any officer or soldier of the Kentucky
National Guard shall be sold, bartered, exchanged, pledged,
loaned or given away. No person, not a soldier or duly author
ized officer of the state, who has possession of any military prop
erty so furnished, and which has been the subject of any such
barter, exchange, pledge, loan or gift, shall have any right, title or
interest therein, but the property may be seized and taken wher
ever found by any officer of the state, civil or military, and shall
thereupon be delivered to the Adjutant General or other officer
authorized to receive it. The possession of such military prop
erty by any person not a soldier or officer of the state shall be
presumptive evidence of such a sale, barter, exchange, pledge,
loan or gift.
Section 20. Section 38.420 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
Officers, warrant officers and enlisted men of the Kentucky
National Guard shall, while on active state service have the same
right to carry arms, and in the same manner as sheriffs and other

Chapter 99 295
(1) "National Guard" means the Kentucky Army National
Guard and the Kentucky Air National Guard;
(2) "Active Militia" means a volunteer defense unit other
than the National Guard;
( 3 ) "Officer" means a commissioned officer including a war
rant officer;
(4) "Superior officer" means an officer superior in rank or
command;
(5) "Enlisted person" means any person who is serving in
an enlisted grade in any force of the National Guard or Active
Militia;
(6) "Active state service" means full-time military duty in
the active service of the state under an order of the Governor as
defined in KRS 38.010 (4);
(7) "Military court" means a court-martial, a court of in
quiry, a provost court, or a military commission;
( 8 ) "Law officer" means an official of a general court-martial
detailed in accordance with Section 26 of this Act;
(9) "Subject person" means person subject to this Act;
( 10 ) "Code" means this Act.
Section 2. All persons belonging to the National Guard or
Active Militia shall be subject to the provisions of this code.
Section 3. ( 1 ) Any person charged with having committed,
while in a status in which he was subject to this code, an offense
against this code for which the person cannot be tried in the
courts of the state shall not be relieved from amenability to trial
by courts-martial by reason of the termination of said status.
(2) All persons discharged from the National Guard or
Active Militia subsequently charged with having fraudulently ob
tained said discharge shall be subject to trial by court-martial on
said charge and, after apprehension, shall be subject to this code.
Upon conviction of said charge they shall be subject to trial by
court-martial for any offense under this code committed prior to
the fraudulent discharge.
( 3 ) Any person who has deserted from the National Guard
or Active Militia shall not be relieved from amenability to the
jurisdiction of this code by virtue of a separation from any sub
sequent period of service.

296 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 4. ( 1 ) When any officer, dismissed by order of the
Governor for absence without leave for a period of six months or
more, makes a written application for trial by court-martial, set
ting forth under oath that he has been wrongfully dismissed, the
Governor, as soon as practicable, shall convene a general court-
martial to try such officer on the charges on which he was dis
missed. A court-martial so convened shall have jurisdiction to
try the dismissed officer on such charges, and he shall be deemed
to have waived the right to plead any statute of limitations ap
plicable to the offense with which he is charged. The court-
martial may, as part of its sentence, adjudge the affirmance of the
dismissal, but if the court-martial acquits the accused or if the
sentence adjudged, as finally approved or affirmed, does not in
clude dismissal, the Adjutant General shall substitute for the
dismissal ordered by the Governor a form of discharge author
ized for administrative issuance.
( 2 ) If the Governor fails to convene a general court-martial
within six months from the presentation of an application for
trial under this section, the Adjutant General shall substitute for
the dismissal ordered by the Governor a form of discharge au
thorized for administrative issuance.
(3) Where a discharge is substituted for a dismissal under
the authority of this section, the Governor alone may reappoint
the officer to such commissioned rank and precedence as in
the opinion of the Governor such former officer would have at
tained had he not been dismissed. The reappointment of such
a former officer may be provided a position vacancy is avail
able under applicable tables of organization. All time between
the dismissal and such reappointment shall be considered as
service for all purposes.
(4) When an officer is discharged from the National
Guard or Active Militia by administrative action or is dropped
from the rolls by order of the Governor, there shall not be a
right to trial under this section.
Section 5. ( 1 ) This code shall be applicable in all places
within the state. It shall also apply to all subject persons while
serving without the state and while going to and returning from
such service without the state in like manner and to the same
extent as while such persons are serving within the state.

Section 6. (1) Apprehension is the taking into custody
of a person.
(2) Any person authorized under regulations issued pur
suant to this Act to apprehend subject persons, any marshal of
a court-martial appointed pursuant to the provisions of this
Act and any peace officer as defined in KRS 61.310 and 446.010
may apprehend subject persons upon reasonable belief that an
offense has been committed and that the person apprehended
committed it.
(3) All officers, petty officers, and noncommissioned of
ficers shall have authority to quell all quarrels, frays and dis
orders among subject persons and to apprehend such persons
who take part in the same.
(4) Except as otherwise provided in this Act, no peace
officer or marshal of a court-martial shall demand or require
payment of any fee or charge of any nature for apprehending
or placing in confinement any subject person.
Section 7. It shall be lawful for any civil officer having
authority to apprehend offenders under the laws of the United
States or of any state, district, territory or possession of the
United States, summarily to apprehend a deserter from the Na
tional Guard or Active Militia and deliver him into the custody
of the National Guard or Active Militia.
Section 8. (1) Arrest is the restraint of a person by an
order not imposed as a punishment for an offense, directing him
to remain within certain specified limits. Confinement is the
physical restraint of a person.
(2) An enlisted person may be ordered apprehended or
into arrest or confinement by any officer by an order, oral or
written, delivered in person, or through other subject persons,
or through any person authorized by this code to apprehend
persons.

Section 12. ( 1 ) Under such regulations as may be issued

(2) When delivery under this Act is made to any civil
