be held to interrupt the execution of the sentence of the court-
martial, and the offender after having answered to the civil
authorities for his offense shall, upon the request of the Adjutant
General, be returned to military custody for the completion of
the said court-martial sentence.
Section 13. ( 1 ) Under such regulations as may be issued
pursuant to this Act, any commanding officer may, in addition
to or in lieu of admonition or reprimand, impose one of the fol
lowing disciplinary punishments for minor offenses without the
intervention of a court-martial:
(a) Upon officers of his command:
1. Withholding of privileges for a period not to exceed two
consecutive weeks;
2. Restriction to certain specified limits, with or without
suspension from duty, for a period not to exceed two consecutive
weeks; or
3. If imposed by the Governor or Adjutant General, for
feiture of pay and allowances not exceeding seventy-five dollars.
(b) Upon other military personnel:
1. Withholding of privileges for a period not to exceed two
consecutive weeks;
2. Restriction to certain specified limits, with or without
suspension from duty, for a period not to exceed two consecu
tive weeks;

Section 14. There shall be three kinds of courts-martial in
each of the forces of the National Guard and Active Militia,
namely:
(1) General courts-martial, which shall consist of a law
officer and any number of members not less than five;
(2) Special courts-martial, which shall consist of any num
ber of members not less than three; and
(3) Summary courts-martial, which shall consist of one
officer other than a warrant officer.
Section 15. The National Guard or Active Militia shall
have court-martial jurisdiction over all subject persons.
Section 16. General courts-martial shall have jurisdiction
to try subject persons for any offense for which they may be pun
ished by this code, and, in connection therewith, shall have the
power to fix the following punishments:
(1) Confinement with hard labor for not exceeding two
hundred days;
(2) Fines not exceeding two hundred dollars;
( 3 ) Confinement with hard labor in lieu of fines imposed,
not exceeding one day for each dollar of fine imposed;
(4) Forfeiture of pay and allowances, not exceeding two
hundred dollars;
(5) Dishonorable discharge;
(6) Bad conduct discharge;
( 7 ) Reprimand;
(8) Reduction of noncommissioned officers to the ranks;
or
( 9 ) A combination of any two or more of the punishments
provided herein.
Section 17. (1) Special courts-martial shall have juris
diction to try subject persons for any offense for which they
may be punished by this code, and, in connection therewith,
shall have the power to fix the following punishments:
(a) Confinement with hard labor for not exceeding one
hundred days;
(b) Fines not exceeding one hundred dollars;

Chapter 99 303
Section 19. The provisions of this code conferring juris
diction upon courts-martial shall not be construed as depriving
military commissions, provost courts, or other military tribunals,
of concurrent jurisdiction in respect of offenders or offenses
that by statute or by the law of war may be tried by such military
commissions, provost courts, or other military tribunals.
Section 20. General courts-martial may be convened by:
( 1 ) The Governor;
(2) The Adjutant General;
(3) When empowered by the Governor, any other com
manding officer in any force of the National Guard or Active
Militia, who is not an accuser.
Section 21. (1) Special courts-martial may be convened
by:
( a ) Any person who may convene a general court-martial;
(b) The commanding officer of a garrison, fort, camp,
station, air base or other place where members of a force of the
National Guard or Active Militia are on duty;
(c) The commanding officer of a division, brigade, regi
ment, detached or separate battalion, wing, group, detached or
separate squadron, or corresponding unit of the National Guard;
(d) The commanding officer of any separate or detached
command or group of detached units of any of the forces of the
National Guard or Active Militia placed under a single com
mander; or
(e) The commanding officer or officer in charge of any
other command, when empowered by the Adjutant General.
(2) When any such officer is an accuser, the court shall
be convened by superior competent authority, and may in any
case be convened by such authority when deemed advisable
by him.
Section 22. (1) Summary courts-martial may be con
vened by:
(a) Any person who may convene a general or special
court-martial;
(b) The commanding officer of a detached or separate
company or corresponding unit or other detachment of the Na
tional Guard or Active Militia;

mony of previously examined witnesses or a stipulation thereof

Chapter 99 307
(a) That the signer has personal knowledge of, or has
investigated, the matters set forth therein; and
( b ) That the same are true in fact to the best of his knowl
edge and belief.
(2) Upon the preferring of charges, the proper authority
shall take immediate steps to determine what disposition should
be made thereof in the interest of justice and discipline, and
the person accused shall be informed of the charges against him
as soon as practicable.
Section 30. (1) No subject person shall compel any
person to incriminate himself or to answer any question the
answer to which may tend to incriminate him.
(2) No subject person shall interrogate, or request any
statement from, an accused or a person suspected of an offense
without first informing him of the nature of the accusation
and advising him that he does not have to make any statement
regarding the offense of which he is accused or suspected and
that any statement made by him may be used as evidence against
him in a trial by court-martial.
(3) No subject person shall compel any person to make
a statement or produce evidence before any military tribunal if
the statement or evidence is not material to the issue and
may tend to degrade him.
(4) No statement obtained from any person in violation
of this section, or through the use of coercion, unlawful in
fluence, or unlawful inducement shall be received in evidence
against him in a trial by court-martial.
Section 31. (1) No charge or specification shall be re
ferred to a general court-martial for trial until a thorough and
impartial investigation of all the matters set forth therein has
been made. This investigation shall include inquiries as to the
truth of the matter set forth in the charges, form of charges, and
the disposition which should be made of the case in the interest
of justice and discipline.
(2) The accused shall be advised of the charges against
him and of his right to be represented by counsel at such in
vestigation. Upon his own request he shall be represented by
civilian counsel if provided by him, or military counsel of his
own selection if such counsel be reasonably available, or by

Section 32. When a person is held for trial by general

Section 33. ( 1 ) Before directing the trial of any charge
by general court-martial, the convening authority shall refer

martial and the law officer of a general court-martial may be

of challenges for cause, and shall not receive a challenge to
more than one person at a time. Challenges by the trial counsel
shall ordinarily be presented and decided before those by the
accused are offered.
(2) Each accused and trial counsel shall be entitled to
one peremptory challenge, but the law officer shall not be
challenged except for cause.
Section 41. (1) The law officer, all interpreters, and, in
general and special courts-martial, the members, the trial counsel,
assistant trial counsel, the defense counsel, assistant defense
counsel, and the reporter shall take an oath or affirmation in the
presence of the accused to perform their duties faithfully.
(2) All witnesses before courts-martial shall be examined
on oath or affirmation.
Section 42. (1) A person charged with mutinv may be
tried and punished at any time without limitation.
(2) Except as otherwise provided in this section, a person
charged with desertion or absence without leave shall not be
liable to be tried by court-martial if the offense was committed
more than one year before the receipt of sworn charges and
specifications by an officer exercising summary court-martial
jurisdiction over the command.
(3) Except as otherwise provided in this Act, a person
charged with any offense shall not be liable to be tried by
court-martial or punished under section 13, if the offense was
committed more than two years before the receipt of sworn
charges and specifications by an officer exercising summary
court-martial jurisdiction over the command or before the im
position of punishment under section 13.
( 4 ) Periods in which the accused was absent from territory
in which the state has the authority to apprehend him, or in
the custody of civil authorities, shall be excluded in computing
the period of limitation prescribed in this section.
Section 43. ( 1 ) No person shall, without his consent, be
tried a second time by a military court of the state for the same
offense.

Section 48. (1) At any time after charges have been
signed as provided in section 29, any party may take written
depositions. If a deposition is to be taken before charges are
referred for trial, the trial authority may designate officers to
represent the prosecution and the defense and may authorize
such officers to take the deposition of any witness.
( 2 ) The party at whose instance a deposition is to be taken
shall give to every other party reasonable written notice of
the time and place for taking the deposition.
(3) Depositions may be taken before and authenticated
by any military or civil officer authorized by the laws of the
state or by the laws of the place where the deposition is taken
to administer oaths.
(4) A duly authenticated deposition taken upon reason
able notice to the other party, so far as otherwise admissible
under the rules of evidence, may be read in evidence before
any court-martial, military commission or in any proceeding
before a court of inquiry, if it appears:
(a) That the witness resides or is beyond the state in which
the court-martial, military commission or court of inquiry is
ordered to sit, or beyond the distance of one hundred miles
from the place of trial or hearing;
(b) That the witness by reason of death, age, sickness,
bodily infirmity, imprisonment, military necessity, nonamena-
bility to process, or other reasonable cause, is unable or refuses
to appear and testify in person at the place of trial or hearing; or
(c) That the present whereabouts of the witness is un
known.

Section 50. ( 1 ) Voting by members of a general or spe
cial court-martial upon questions of challenge, on the findings,
and on the sentence, shall be by secret written ballot. The
junior member of the court shall in each case count the votes,
which count shall be checked by the president, who shall forth
with announce the result of the ballot to the members of the
court.
(2) The law officer of a general court-martial and the
president of a special court-martial shall rule upon interlocutory
questions, other than challenge, arising during the proceedings.
Any such ruling made by the law officer of a general court-
martial upon any interlocutory question other than a motion for
a finding of not guilty, or the question of accused's sanity, shall
be final and shall constitute the ruling of the court; but the
law officer may change any such ruling at any time during the
trial. Unless such ruling be final, if any member objects thereto,
the court shall be cleared and closed and the question decided
by a vote as provided in section 51, viva voce, beginning with
the junior in rank.
( 3 ) Before a vote is taken on the findings, the law officer
of a general court-martial and the president of a special court-
martial shall, in the presence of the accused and counsel, in
struct the court as to the elements of the offense and charge the
court as follows:

Section 54. (1) The punishment which a court-martial
may direct for an offense shall not exceed the limits prescribed
by this code.
(2) Punishment by flogging, or by branding, marking, or
tattooing on the body, or any other cruel or unusual punishment,
shall not be adjudged by any court-martial or inflicted upon any
subject person. The use of irons, single or double, except for
the purpose of safe custody, is prohibited.
Section 55. ( 1 ) Whenever a sentence of a court-martial,
as lawfully adjudged and approved, includes a forfeiture of pay
or allowances in addition to confinement not suspended, the
forfeiture may apply to pay or allowances becoming due on or
after the date such sentence is approved by the convening
authority and to any pay or allowances accrued before such date.
(2) Any period of confinement included in a sentence of
a court-martial shall begin to run from the date the sentence is
adjudged by the court-martial, but periods during which the
sentence to confinement is suspended shall be excluded in com
puting the service of the term of confinement.
(3) All other sentences of court-martial shall become ef
fective on the date ordered executed.
Section 56. (1) Any sentence of confinement adjudged
by a court-martial or other military court, whether or not such
sentence includes discharge or dismissal, and whether or not
such discharge or dismissal has been executed, may be carried
into execution by confinement in any place of confinement un
der the control of the National Guard or Active Militia, or in
any jail, penitentiary or prison designated for that purpose as
prescribed in section 10 of this code; and persons so confined in
such a jail, penitentiary or prison shall be subject to the same
discipline and treatment as persons confined or committed to
such jail, penitentiary or prison by the courts of the state or of
any political subdivision thereof.
(2) The omission of the words "hard labor" in any sen
tence of a court-martial adjudging confinement shall not be

classification.title: Acts of the General Assembly, 1954, Section 54-56
classification.summary: This statute outlines the limits and procedures for punishments and sentences imposed by court-martials within the National Guard or Active Militia. It prohibits cruel and unusual punishments like flogging or branding, regulates the use of irons, and details how sentences of confinement and forfeiture of pay are to be executed and computed. It also specifies that persons confined in designated jails or prisons are subject to the same discipline as those committed by state courts, and that the omission of 'hard labor' in a sentence does not prevent its imposition.
classification.reasoning: The statute deals with military justice procedures for court-martials. It sets limits on punishments and outlines the execution of sentences. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. The statute applies to all individuals subject to court-martial proceedings under the National Guard or Active Militia, regardless of race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 60. (1) If a specification before a court-martial
has been dismissed on motion and the ruling does not amount
to a finding of not guilty, the convening authority may return
the record to the court for consideration of the ruling and any
further appropriate action.
(2) Where there is an apparent error or omission in the
record or where the record shows improper or inconsistent ac
tion by a court-martial with respect to a finding or sentence
which can be rectified without material prejudice to the sub
stantial rights of the accused, the convening authority may return
the record to the court for appropriate action. In no case, how
ever, may the record be returned for the following:
(a) Reconsideration of a finding of not guilty of any
specification, or a ruling which amounts to a finding of not
guilty;
(b) Reconsideration of a finding of not guilty of any
charge, unless the record shows a finding of guilty under a
specification laid under that charge, which sufficiently alleges
a violation of some section of this code; or
( c ) Increasing the severity of the sentence unless the sen
tence prescribed for the offense is mandatory.
Section 61. (1) If the convening authority disapproves
the findings and sentence of a court-martial he may, except
where there is lack of sufficient evidence in the record to
support the findings, order a rehearing, in which case he shall
state the reasons for disapproval. If he disapproves the findings
and sentence and does not order a rehearing, he shall dismiss
the charges.
(2) Every rehearing shall take place before a court-mar
tial composed of members not members of the court-martial
which first heard the case. Upon such rehearing the accused
shall not be tried for any offense of which he was found not
guilty by the first court-martial, and no sentence in excess of
or more severe than the original sentence shall be imposed unless
the sentence is based upon a finding of guilty of an offense not
considered upon the merits in the original proceedings or unless
the sentence prescribed for the offense is mandatory.

Section 62. In acting on the findings and sentence of a
court-martial, the convening authority shall approve only such
findings of guilty, and the sentence or such part or amount of
the sentence, as he finds correct in law and fact and as he, in
his discretion, determines should be approved. Unless otherwise
indicated, approval of the sentence shall constitute approval of
the findings and sentence.
Section 63. (1) When the convening authority is the
Governor, his action on the review of any record of trial shall

(a) When the convening authority has taken final action
in a general court-martial case, he shall forward the entire
record, including his action thereon and the opinion or opinions

proved by an officer exercising general court-martial jurisdiction

opinions of the staff judge advocate or legal officer, shall be
forwarded to the state judge advocate for review.

(4) In all cases reviewable by the state judge advocate
which do not affect a general officer or extend to the dismissal
of an officer, the state judge advocate shall take final action.
(5) In any case reviewable by the Governor where he
is not the convening authority, and in any case reviewable by
the state judge advocate, the Governor or the state judge
advocate, as the case may be, shall have authority to:
(a) Act only with respect to the findings and sentence
as approved by the convening authority;
(b) Affirm only such findings of guilty, and the sentence
or such part or amount of the sentence as he finds correct in
law and fact and determines on the basis of the entire record,
should be approved;
(c) Weigh the evidence, judge the credibility of witnesses,
and determine controverted questions of fact, recognizing that
the trial court saw and heard the witnesses;
( d ) Order a rehearing if he sets aside the findings and sen
tence, except where the setting aside is based on lack of suffi
cient evidence to support the findings;
(e) Order that the charges be dismissed if he sets aside
the findings and sentence and does not order a rehearing.
(6) The state judge advocate shall, unless there is to be
further action by the Governor, instruct the convening authority
to take action in accordance with his decision on a review. If
he has ordered a rehearing but the convening authority finds
a rehearing impracticable, he may dismiss the charges.
(7) The state judge advocate may constitute one or more
boards of review each composed of not less than three officers
of the National Guard or Active Militia each of whom shall be
a member of the bar of this state, which boards shall review
the record of any trial by court-martial referred to it by the state
judge advocate. Such boards of review shall have the same
authority and powers on the review of a record as the state
judge advocate has under the provisions of this section.
Section 64. A finding or sentence of a court-martial shall
not be held incorrect on the ground of an error of law unless the
error materially prejudices the substantial rights of the accused.

bation. If he so desires the probationer shall be represented at
such hearing either by counsel provided by him or by counsel

in section 66 of this code.

Chapter 99 323
a position vacancy is available under applicable tables of or
ganization. All time between the dismissal and such reap
pointment shall be considered as service for all purposes.
Section 71. The proceedings, findings, and sentences of
courts-martial as reviewed and approved, and all dismissals
and discharges carried into execution pursuant to sentences by
courts-martial following review and approval, shall be final
and conclusive and orders publishing the proceedings of courts-
martial and all action taken pursuant to such proceedings shall
be binding upon all departments, courts, agencies, and officers of
the state, subject only to action upon a petition for a new trial
as provided in section 68.
Section 72. The state judge advocate or his assistants shall
make frequent inspections in the field in supervision of the
administration of military justice in the National Guard and
Active Militia.
Section 73. Convening authorities, judge advocates and
legal officers may communicate directly with one another in
matters relating to the administration of military justice without
going through regular channels of military communication.
Section 74. (1) Courts of inquiry to investigate any matter
may be convened by any person authorized to convene a
general court-martial or by any other person designated by the
Governor for that purpose whether or not the persons involved
have requested such an inquiry.
(2) A court of inquiry shall consist of three or more of
ficers. For each court of inquiry the convening authority shall
also appoint counsel for the court.
(3) Any subject person whose conduct is subject to inquiry
shall be designated as a party. Any subject person or person
employed in the Military Department of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky who has a direct interest in the subject of inquiry
shall have the right to be designated as a party upon request
to the court. Any person designated as a party shall be given
due notice and shall have the right to be present, to be repre
sented by counsel, to cross-examine witnesses, and to introduce
evidence.
(4) Members of a court of inquiry may be challenged by
a party, but only for cause stated to the court.

Section 83. The jurisdiction of the military courts and

of proof shall rest on any person seeking to oust such courts or

Section 84. The Governor is authorized to delegate any
authority vested in him under this code, and to provide for the
subdelegation of any such authority.
Section 85. This Act shall be known and may be cited
and referred to as the Kentucky Code of Military Justice.
Section 86. All offenses committed and all penalties, for
feitures, fines, or liabilities incurred prior to the effective date
of this Act under any law embraced in or modified, changed or
repealed by this Act may be prosecuted, punished and enforced,
and action thereon may be completed, in the same manner and
with the same effect as if this Act had not been passed.
Section 87. Any subject person who commits an offense
punishable by this code, or aids, abets, counsels, commands, or
procures its commission; or causes an act to be done which if
directly performed by him would be punishable by this code,
is a principal offender.
Section 88. An accused may be found guilty of an offense
necessarily included in the offense charged or of an attempt to
commit either the offense charged or of an offense necessarily
included therein.
Section 89. (1) An act, done with specific intent to com
mit an offense under this code, amounting to more than mere
preparation and tending but failing to effect its commission, is
an attempt to commit that offense.
(2) Any subject person who attempts to commit any of
fense punishable by this code shall be punished as a court-
martial may direct, unless otherwise specifically prescribed.
(3) Any subject person may be convicted of an attempt to
commit an offense although it appears on the trial that the
offense was consummated.

Section 99. Any subject person who, while on active state
duty and without justification or excuse, unlawfully kills an
other human being under any of the following circumstances
is guilty of murder:
(1) With a premeditated design to kill.
(2) With an intention to kill or to inflict great bodily
harm.
(3) While engaged in an act which is inherently danger
ous to others and in which a wanton disregard of human life
is evinced.
(4) While engaged in the perpetration or attempted perpe
tration of burglary, sodomy, rape, robbery, or aggravated arson.
Section 100. (1) Any subject person who, while on active
state duty and with an intent to kill or inflict great bodily
harm, unlawfully kills another human being in the heat of sud
den passion caused by adequate provocation is guilty of volun
tary manslaughter.
(2) Any subject person who, while on active state duty and
without an intent to kill or inflict great bodily harm, unlawfully
kills another human being by culpable negligence, or while per
petrating or attempting to perpetrate an offense, other than
that specified in section 106, directly affecting the person, is
guilty of involuntary manslaughter.
Section 101. (1) Any subject person who, while on active
state duty, commits an act of sexual intercourse with a female
not his wife, by force and without her consent, is guilty of rape.
(2) Any subject person who, while on active state duty,
under circumstances not amounting to rape, commits an act
of sexual intercourse with a female not his wife who has not
attained the age of sixteen years, is guilty of carnal knowledge.
(3) Penetration, however slight, is sufficient to complete
the offenses prescribed in subsection (1) and (2).

Chapter 100 337
General Assembly hereby signifies in advance its approval and
ratification of such compact:
SOUTHEASTERN INTERSTATE FOREST FIRE
PROTECTION COMPACT
Article I
The purpose of this compact is to promote effective pre
vention and control of forest fires in the Southeastern region
of the United States by the development of integrated forest
fire plans, by the maintenance of adequate forest fire fighting
services by the member states, by providing for mutual aid in
fighting forest fires among the compacting states of the region
and with states which are party to other regional forest fire
protection compacts or agreements, and for more adequate forest
protection.
Article II
This compact shall become operative immediately as to
those states ratifying it whenever any two or more of the states
of Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Kentucky, Mississippi, North Caro
lina, South Carolina, Tennessee, Virginia, and West Virginia,
which are contiguous have ratified it and Congress has given
consent thereto. Any state not mentioned in this article which
is contiguous with any member state may become a party to
this compact, subject to approval by the legislature of each of
the member states.
Article III
In each state, the state forester or officer holding the
equivalent position who is responsible for forest fire control
shall act as compact administrator for that state and shall
consult with like officials of the other member states and shall
implement cooperation between such states in forest fire pre
vention and control.
The compact administrators of the member states shall
coordinate the services of the member states and provide adminis
trative integration in carrying out the purposes of this compact.
There shall be established an advisory committee of legis
lators, forestry commission representatives, and forestry or forest
products industries representatives which shall meet from time
to time with the compact administrators. Each member state
shall name one member of the Senate and one member of the

342 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 102
(H. B. 84)
AN ACT relating to mechanics' and materialmen's liens.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Any person engaged in the business of selling,
repairing, or furnishing accessories, or supplies for any kind of
equipment or machinery, including motors, shall have a lien on
the equipment, machine, machinery, or motor, for the reasonable
or agreed charges for repairs, work done, accessories, parts, and
supplies furnished for the equipment, machine, machinery, or
motor until the reasonable or agreed charge therefor has been
paid. The Hen shall not be lost by the removal of the equipment,
machine, machinery or motor from the premises of the person
performing labor, repairing, or furnishing accessories, parts or
supplies therefor, if the lien shall be asserted within three months
by filing in the office of the County Clerk a statement showing
the amount and cost of materials furnished or labor performed on
the equipment, machine, machinery, or motor. This statement
shall be filed in the office of the County Court Clerk of the county
in which the owner of the equipment, machine, machinery, or
motor resides, unless such owner be a non-resident, in which
event the lien statement shall be filed in the office of the County
Court Clerk of the county in which such equipment, machine,
machinery, or motor is at the time being kept or used.
The lien provided for in this Act shall attach regardless of
whether or not the equipment, machine, machinery, motor, or
motors, are ever upon the premises of or in the possession of the
person making the repairs, furnishing the parts, or the supplies
for such repairs.
Section 2. The lien statement referred to in Section 1 of
this Act shall be construed to mean a statement in writing in
which is stated the amount due the claimant, with all just credits
and set-offs known to him, together with a description of the
property intended to be covered by the lien sufficiently accurate
to identify it and the name of the owner. This statement shall be
subscribed and sworn to by the person claiming the lien or by
someone in his behalf.
The County Court Clerk shall endorse upon each statement
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 363

The statement must include the amount due, a description of the property, and the owner's name, and must be subscribed and sworn to.

CHAPTER 103
(H. B. 85)
AN ACT relating to statutory liens; providing additional security for
the payment of claims for labor, materials and supplies furnished
for use on certain public works; prescribing the procedure for per
fecting and enforcing liens to better secure the payment of such
claims; defining the terms "labor," "materials," and "supplies," as
used in this Act; providing for the discharge of such liens on
public funds by payment of such claims out of funds due the con
tractors; and, repealing all laws and parts of laws in conflict here
with to the extent of such conflict.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Any person, firm or corporation who performs
labor or furnishes materials or supplies for the construction,
maintenance or improvement of any canal, railroad, bridge,
public highway or other public improvement in this state by
contract, express or implied, with the owner thereof or by sub
contract thereunder shall have a lien thereon, and upon all the
property and the franchises of the owner, except property owned
by the state, a subdivision or agency thereof, or by any county
or city. If the property improved is owned by the state or by any
subdivision or agency thereof, or by any county or city, the
person furnishing the labor, materials or supplies shall have a
lien on the funds due the contractor from the owner of the
property improved. Except as hereinafter provided, the lien
shall be for the full contract price of the labor, materials and
supplies furnished, and shall be superior to all other liens there
after created.
Section 2. As used in this Act:
(1) "Labor" shall include all work done by teams, trucks,
machinery, and mechanical equipment, whether the owner
furnishes a driver or operator or not, but it shall not include
labor performed by any officer, superintendent or stockholder
of any corporation even though he may perform manual labor
on the public work;
(2) "Materials" shall include all materials of every kind or
character used in the public improvement which shall remain
as a part of the completed improvement, and all the materials
substantially consumed or the value thereof substantially des

CHAPTER 105
(H. B. 87)
AN ACT relating to deputy constables and declaring an emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 70.320 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) The appointment of deputy constables shall be author
ized only in counties containing a first or second class city. In
such counties each constable mav appoint one or more deputies
with the consent of the county judge. The constable and his
surety shall be liable on his bond for all the acts and omissions
of his deputies.
(2) Deputy constables may be removed at any time for
any cause deemed sufficient by the constable by order of the
county court entered after filing of a written direction by the
constable.
(3) Each deputy constable in counties containing a city of
the first class, shall be compensated for his services by salarv fixed
by the fiscal court and paid out of the county levy.
Section 2. Whereas, if it is deemed necessarv to authorize
an increase in the compensation of deputy constables in counties
containing a first class city, an emergency is declared to exist and
this Act shall take effect upon its passage and approval by the
Governor.
Approved March 15, 1954

CHAPTER 106
(H. B. 88)
AN ACT relating to the organization and powers of water districts, and
declaring an emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 74.120 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) All or any part of an incorporated city may be included
in the boundaries of any existing water district or water district
being newly organized, provided the governing body of such city
by resolution or ordinance gives, or has given, its consent.
(2) The commission may contract with any city which is
not included in the boundaries of the district for the purpose of
furnishing water or gas to the residents of such city and may con
tract with any city for the purpose of obtaining water or gas for
the use of the district. The assignment to the commission or to
the water district of any valid outstanding franchise for the dis
tribution of water or natural, artificial, or mixed gas to the resi
dents of any city shall constitute a contract with the city within
the meaning of this section.
Section 2. Whereas, it is imperative that contracts be made
immediately to prevent future shortages of water and gas
throughout the Commonwealth, an emergency is declared to
exist and this Act shall take effect upon its passage and approval
by the Governor.
Approved March 1, 1954

AN ACT relating to annexation by a third-class city located in two or
more counties.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Whenever a city of the third class located in two
or more counties desires to annex any unincorporated territory,
the city legislative body shall pass an ordinance accurately de
fining the boundary of the territory proposed to be annexed,
declaring it to be desirable to annex the territory, and providing
that the question of whether or not the territory shall be annexed
shall be submitted to the qualified voters residing therein at the
next regular election in which all of such voters may vote, if the
next regular election is not held within sixty days after passage
of the ordinance.
Section 2. The mayor of the city shall deliver a certified
copy of the ordinance to the county clerk of the county in which
the territory proposed to be annexed is located, or if such terri
tory is located in more than one county, to the county clerks of
such counties, who shall have printed on the ballots provided
for use in each precinct embraced in whole or in part within the
territory proposed to be annexed the question: "Are you in favor
of being annexed to the City of "? Opposite
the question shall be printed "Yes" and "No," with squares for
stamping the cross mark to indicate the preference. If only a
part of any precinct is embraced within the territory proposed
to be annexed the clerk of the election shall mark an "X" with
the black ink stencil in the squares opposite both the words "Yes"
and "No" upon the ballots of all persons voting who reside in
the precinct but not within the territory proposed to be annexed,
before he delivers the ballots to those voters. The clerk or clerks
shall cause the sheriff or sheriffs to deliver to the election of
ficers in each precinct in the appropriate counties copies of the
ordinance proposing to annex.
Section 3. If one-half or more of those voting on the propo
sition vote in favor of annexation, the city shall pass an ordinance
declaring the territory annexed, and such territory shall then be
come a part of the city.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 370

classification.title: AN ACT relating to annexation by a third-class city located in two or more counties.
classification.summary: This act outlines the process for a third-class city located in multiple counties to annex unincorporated territory. It requires the city to pass an ordinance defining the territory, declare its desirability, and submit the annexation question to the qualified voters residing in the territory in a regular election. The mayor must deliver a certified copy of the ordinance to the relevant county clerks, who will then have the annexation question printed on ballots. If a majority of voters in the territory approve, the city can pass an ordinance to finalize the annexation.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 241.060 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Functions of board. The board shall have the following
functions, powers and duties:
(1) To adopt reasonable regulations governing the pro
cedure relative to applications for and revocations of licenses and
relative to all other matters over which the board has jurisdiction,
and for the supervision and control of manufacture, sale, trans
portation, storage, advertising and trafficking of alcoholic bever
ages. Regulations need not be uniform in their application, but
mav varv in accordance with reasonable classifications.
(2) To limit in its sound discretion the number of licenses
of each kind or class to be issued in this state or any political sub
division, and restrict the locations of licensed premises. To this
end the board may make reasonable division and subdivision of
the state or any political subdivision into districts. Regulations
relating to the granting, refusal and revocation of licenses may be
different within the several divisions or subdivisions.
(3) To hold hearings, subpoena witnesses, compel their
attendance, administer oaths, examine any person under oath
and require the production of any books or papers relative to the
inquiry, regardless of whether the witnesses are licensees. The
power provided in this subsection may be delegated by the board
to anv member or employee of the department. Provisions of the
Civil Code of Practice shall apply to subpoenas issued pursuant
to this subsection, but such subpoenas shall be effective through
out the state and shall be enforceable by petition to a court of
competent jurisdiction. The department may pay witnesses the
per diem and mileage provided in KRS 421.010.

wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. It is proposed that Section 170 of the Constitu

There shall be exempt from taxation public property used

towns and cities and two acres of ground in the country ap
purtenant thereto; household goods of a person used in his home;

CHAPTER 112
(H. B. 110)
AN ACT relating to the office of jailer in cities of the second class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Subsection (1) of Section 84.350 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(1) At the regular election every four years, beginning
with 1895, the voters of the city shall elect a mayor, city treasurer,
city attorney and assessor, each to hold office for four years and
until his successor is elected and has qualified.
Section 2. Section 441.320 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
In cities of the second class operating under the council-
manic form of government, the voters shall elect a city jailer,
who shall be not less than twenty-five years of age and a quali
fied voter in the city provided the city maintains a jail. He shall
hold his office for a term of four years. He shall perform such
duties as the general council prescribes by ordinance. His com
pensation for his duties shall be not less than fifteen hundred
dollars nor more than twenty-five hundred dollars per annum.
He may appoint a deputy, who shall perform such duties as the
general council prescribes by ordinance, and whose compensa
tion shall be not less than nine hundred dollars nor more than
twelve hundred dollars per annum. All the property, machinery
and equipment of the city jail shall be under the immediate con
trol of the city jailer.
Approved March 22, 1954

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 61.160 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended

Public offices in counties containing a city of the first,
second or third class may close at noon Saturday. The public
offices in other counties may close at noon any one day of the
week.

356 Acts of the General Assembly
WHEREAS, with such regard, the General Assembly has found the
following facts to exist:
(1) The entire population of the state as of the last Federal census
is 2,944,806,
(2) That upon the basis of population, additional judicial districts
may be established to the forty-two judicial districts now
existing, pursuant to sections 132 and 138 of the Constitution,
(3) That the counties of Whitley and McCreary have a combined
population of 48,100 as of the last census,
(4) That the counties of Knox and Laurel have a combined popula
tion of 56,206 as of the last Federal census,
(5) That the combined population of Clay, Jackson and Leslie
counties have a combined population of 51,754, as of the last
Federal census,
(6) That the county of Bell has a total population of 47,602 as of
the last Federal census, and its population is rapidly increasing,
(7) That currently and heretofore, the amount of business of the
Bell Circuit Court is heavy and much more than in many single
districts now existing, and circuit court is held in both Middles-
boro and Pineville and the business of Bell county combined
with the business of Leslie county makes it difficult for the
court to properly take care of the business of both counties;
that the combined business of the counties of Clay, Laurel and
Jackson as the 27th Judicial District as it now exists is too
much; and the combined business of Knox, Whitley, and
McCreary as the 34th Judicial District as now existing is too
much, and that these counties should be re-arranged and re
grouped,
(8) That in the present 41st Judicial District as it now exists com
posed of the counties of Bell and Leslie, in order to go from
one county seat to the other, by the usual means of travel, it is
necessary to travel through the county of Harlan; that the
counties of Clay, Leslie and Jackson, are contiguous and there
is a good highway connecting the county seats of these counties,
and they have many interests in common and are geographic
ally, commercially and otherwise homogeneous,
(9) That the counties of McCreary and Whitley join each other;
and the counties of Knox and Laurel join each other,
(10) That it will be convenient and advantageous from the stand
point of business, territory and population, and will comply
with the spirit and intent of the Constitution and expedite the
business of the courts, and make it more convenient to the
citizens of the counties involved, to create a new judicial dis
trict of the county of Bell, and to change the 41st Judicial
District so that it will be composed of the counties of Clay,
Leslie and Jackson; and to change the 27th Judicial District
so that it will be composed of the counties of Laurel and Knox;
and to change the 34th Judicial District so that it will be com
posed of the counties of Whitley and McCreary;

Chapter 119 361
(3) "Veterinarian" means a licensed practitioner of veter
inary medicine, accredited by the Bureau of Animal Industry,
United States Department of Agriculture;
(4) "Qualified person" means a person granted a permit by
the State Board of Health to vaccinate animals against rabies;
and may include owners or operators of licensed kennels;
(5) "Vaccination" means the injection by a veterinarian or
other qualified person of vaccine approved by, and administered
in accordance with the regulations of the State Board of Health.
Section 2. On or before the first of July, 1954, and of each
year thereafter, every owner shall have his dog vaccinated
against rabies. The veterinarian or qualified person who vac
cinates a dog shall issue to the owner thereof a vaccination cer
tificate on a form prescribed and supplied by the State Depart
ment of Health. The vaccination certificate shall be prepared
and issued in triplicate, one copy to be retained by the issuing
veterinarian or other qualified person, one copy to be given to
the owner of the dog vaccinated, and one copy to be forwarded
by the veterinarian or qualified person to the local health depart
ment, or, if none is maintained, to the State Department of
Health. Each certificate shall bear the name and address of
the veterinarian or qualified person who issued it and a serial
number. The veterinarian or qualified person shall also furnish
each owner with a metal tag bearing the certificate number and
the year of vaccination. The tag may bear the name of the
veterinarian or qualified person issuing it. It shall be affixed by
the owner to a collar or harness furnished by him and shall be
worn by the dog for which the certificate was issued. No one
except the owner or his duly authorized agent shall remove the
collar with the attached tag from the dog.
Section 3. The State Board of Health, in its sound dis
cretion may by such rules and regulations as it deems advisable:
(1) Exempt from the provisions of Section 2 of this Act the
owners of dogs in any county or counties in which the incidence
of rabies decreases sufficiently to warrant this action;
(2) Extend the period required for re-vaccination if a vac
cine is developed which will give longer periods of immunity;
(3) Exempt from the provisions of Section 2 of this Act the
owners of dogs which are brought into this state for a period
not to exceed thirty days.

Section 4. Any owner who has had his dog vaccinated
against rabies in another state by the proper authority therein
shall not be required to have such dog re-vaccinated when
brought into this state provided the requirements of such state
under which the vaccination was made were of a standard not
lower than those required in this state and provided further that
such dog wears a tag affixed to his collar or harness bearing the
date of such vaccination. One year after the date of such vac
cination such dog must be re-vaccinated unless provided other
wise by the rules and regulations of the State Board of Health.
The State Board of Health may make rules and regulations gov
erning the matter of reciprocity with other states.
Section 5. The State Department of Health may contract
with veterinarians to vaccinate dogs or animals at scheduled mass
immunization clinics at fees not in excess of one dollar and fifty
cents per animal vaccinated, to be paid by the owner to the
State Department of Health; provided, however, that if the
services of veterinarians are not available the State Department
of Health may contract with other qualified persons. Fees so
collected shall be paid into the State Treasury and credited to a
trust and agency fund to be used in defraying the costs and ex
penses of administering this Act.
Section 6. Whenever a local board of health has reason
to believe or has been notified by the State Department of
Health that there is danger that rabies may spread within the
county or any portion thereof, the board shall publish a notice
requiring owners of dogs, or other specified animals in the area
designated to confine the animals for such periods as may be
necessary to prevent the spread of rabies. If it is deemed advis
able in the interest of public health the local board of health shall
order all dogs in the area re-vaccinated against rabies and if the
local board fails or neglects to do so, the State Department of
Health may order the re-vaccination of all dogs or other animals
in the area except animals that have been previously vaccinated
within the past six months under the provisions of this Act. The
State Department of Health may aid the local health department
in the execution of such emergency vaccinations.
Section 7. Every physician shall, within twelve hours after
his first professional attendance of a person bitten by a dog, cat
or other animal report to the local health department the name,

age, sex, color, and precise location of the person so bitten. If

Section 8. The State Board of Health may administer the
provisions of this Act through the local health departments and
may make such rules and regulations and employ such personnel
as are necessary to effectuate the purposes of this Act.
Section 9. A health officer or his agent shall have the
authority to quarantine for a period not to exceed ninety days
any animal bitten by another animal known or suspected to have
rabies, and to quarantine for a period not to exceed fourteen days
any animal which has bitten a human being or which exhibits
symptoms of rabies. An animal so quarantined may be confined
by the health officer at a designated place at the owner's expense.
Whenever a dog dies with rabies or is suspected of having died
with rabies or is destroyed because of having been suspected of
being rabid, the owner thereof, whether the dog had been pre
viously quarantined or not, shall at his own expense send the
head of such dog to a laboratory approved by the State Board
of Health in the manner prescribed by the rules and regulations
of the State Board of Health.
Section 11. (1) Any person who violates Sections 2, 4, 6,
7 or 9 of this Act shall be fined not less than ten dollars nor more
than one hundred dollars. Each day of violation shall constitute
a separate offense.
(2) The owner of any dog not vaccinated according to the
provisions of this Act shall be liable to pay all damages for per
sonal injuries resulting from the bite of such dog, if rabid.
Section 12. As used in Sections 12 to 41 of this Act, unless
the context requires otherwise:
(1) "Department" means the Department of Agriculture,
Labor and Statistics;

Labor and Statistics;
(3) "Committee" means the advisory committee created by
Section 14 of this Act;

Section 15. There is hereby created a revolving fund to be
known as the Livestock Fund from which shall be paid all ex
penses of the department in administering the provisions of
Sections 12 to 41 of this Act. Expenditures from the fund shall
not exceed two hundred thousand dollars in any fiscal year with
out the approval of the committee. On January 1, 1957, and on
January 1 of each year thereafter, all money in the Livestock
Fund in excess of two hundred thousand dollars shall revert to
the General Expenditure Fund.

Section 17. (1) A dog license bearing a serial number and

ing with the serial number of the license. If a license or tag is

department. The department may, if the committee deems such
a plan practical, arrange for the tattooing of dogs under a system
whereby tattoo marks shall serve as proof of licensing in the
event a tag is lost or is removed for good reason by a dog owner
or keeper, but such tattooing shall be done only upon the request
of the owner or keeper.

by the owner which shall be kept on the dog at all times, and
only the owner or his authorized agent shall remove the collar
with attached tag from any dog.

to another dog except as provided in Section 19 of this Act.
(4) Except when a tattoo is used, any dog not bearing a tag
shall prima facie be deemed to be unlicensed, and in any pro
ceedings under this Act the burden of proof of the fact that a dog
has been licensed shall be on the owner of the dog.

dog and kennel licenses issued and shall report to the department
monthly his license sales on a form prescribed and supplied by
the department. The record maintained shall be a public record.
Section 22. ( 1 ) On or before July 1, 1954, the fiscal court
of each county shall employ a dog warden. On or before July 1,
1955, the fiscal court of each countv shall establish and maintain
a dog pound as a means of facilitating and administration of this
Act. In counties of small population, arrangements may be made
for the joint establishment and operation of dog pounds by two
or more counties on a mutually satisfactory basis. Instead of
setting up pounds, one or more counties acting jointly may take

relative to pound standards, the naming of persons who shall

tain the extent of the claim, and if approved, make an appraisal

prove the appraisal report, he shall ascertain the amount of
damage sustained.

CHAPTER 121
(H. B. 141)
AN ACT relating to the appointment of deputy coroners, the holding of
inquests, and amending Section 72.040 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 72.040 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) If the coroner is in the county and able to attend to
his duties no person other than the coroner shall hold an inquest
except a deputy coroner authorized by him to act as coroner.
The coroner may appoint a maximum of two deputy coroners
at no additional expense to the county or city, who may hold
inquests and act as coroner in the absence of the coroner or at
any time authorized by the coroner.
(2) Every deputy coroner shall execute bond with sureties
approved by the county judge in the same manner as provided
for coroners in KRS 72.010.
Approved March 24, 1954

CHAPTER 125
(H. B. 146)
AN ACT relating to the compensation of coroners, and amending Sec
tion 64.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Coroners may charge and receive a fee of twelve
dollars for holding an inquest on a dead body. This sum shall be
paid out of the estate of the deceased, if sufficient; if not, out of
the county treasury, unless the inquest is held over an unburied
body in a city containing a population of over 30,000, in which
case the coroner's fees shall be paid by the city.
Section 2. For all other services, coroners shall be allowed
the same fees allowed to sheriffs and constables for similar serv
ices.
Section 3. In addition to the compensation provided for in
the above Sections, the coroners may receive out of the county
treasury such monthly compensation as the fiscal court of each
county may fix.
Approved March 24, 1954

CHAPTER 129
(H. B. 165)
AN ACT relating to dairying and creating a dairy commission.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context other
wise requires:
( 1 ) "Commission" means the Kentucky Dairy Commission;
(2) "Person" means any individual, corporation, partner
ship, association, cooperative, or other business unit;
(3) "Producer" means every person who produces milk or
cream from cows and thereafter sells the same as milk, cream,
or other dairy products;
(4) "Handler" means any person in his capacity as the
operator of a milk plant or receiving station in the state of Ken
tucky at which milk, cream, or other dairy products are received
from producers for the purpose of weighing, testing, processing
or manufacturing;
(5) "Producer-handler" means any person who maintains
his own herd, prepares for human consumption the milk or cream
or other dairy products produced from said herd, and distributes
and sells his or its own products, partially or exclusively, to
customers or to others for sale to consumers.
Section 2. ( 1 ) There is hereby created a "Kentucky Dairy
Commission" composed of six members. The membership of
this commission shall be composed of the Commissioner of Agri
culture, the head of the dairy section of the Agricultural Experi
ment Station and four members to be appointed by the Director
of the Agricultural Experiment Station. The four at large mem
bers shall be selected as follows: one from the names of two
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 402

(2) All members appointed on this commission shall serve
a term of four years and until their successors are appointed and
qualified. All members shall be citizens of the United States
and residents of this state. Whenever an appointment is made
the names of two qualified persons shall be submitted to the
director.
(3) The four members appointed on the first commission
shall hold office as follows: one for one year, one for two years,
one for three years, and one for four years, from the effective
date of this Act. The terms of the office of the Commissioner
of Agriculture and the head of the dairy section of the Agri
cultural Experiment Station shall be continuous and in succession
of office of those persons holding such offices.
(4) The commission shall select a chairman from among
its members, who shall preside at all meetings and vote as any
other member. A secretary may be selected from among the
commission members or hired from without.
(5) Vacancies occurring during a term shall be filled by
appointment for the unexpired term and in case a member whose
appointment must be made from the first four or second four
congressional districts, moves from the districts for which he
was appointed, his office shall automatically become vacant;
whereupon the commission shall forthwith notify the Director
of Agricultural Experiment Station and the American Dairy
Association of Kentucky of such vacancy, and the said Director
shall thereupon fill such vacancy by appointment as originally
provided in subsection (1) of this section.
(6) All members of the commission shall be reimbursed
for their actual necessary expenses and mileage at the rate of
seven cents per mile, and each of the appointed members shall be
paid, in addition thereto, ten dollars for each day spent in the
discharge of his official duties.

(c) Establish such offices at such places as it

(d) Enter into contracts and agreements
deemed necessary to promote the sale and consumption of dairy
products and to effectuate the provisions of this Act.
( e ) Conduct scientific research for the purpose
of developing and discovering health, food, therapeutic, dietetic
and industrial uses for dairy products.
(f) Plan and conduct campaigns for dairy
commodity advertising, publicity, and sales promotion, research
and educational campaigns, to increase the consumption of
dairy products, and contract for any advertising, publicity, sales
promotion, research and educational service.

not to exceed the amount of bond. Payment shall be made from

credited.

or falsely report any weights or to otherwise fraudulently
manipulate scales to produce a weight other than the true and
actual weight of any livestock consigned to and sold at any
stockyard.

provisions of this Act or any applicable provision of KRS Chapter

classification.title: An Act to amend Section 140.260 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes relating to taxation of life insurance and annuity contracts.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 140.260 to clarify the taxability of life insurance and annuity contract proceeds. It specifies that proceeds payable to the assured or their estate are taxable, while those payable to a designated beneficiary are tax-free. It also exempts certain government life insurance policies and railroad retirement pensions from taxation. Additionally, it allows life insurance companies to pay out taxable policy proceeds before tax assessment, provided they notify the Department of Revenue, with an exception for payments of $1000 or less.

CHAPTER 133
(H. B. 172)
AN ACT relating to special judges and amending Sections 23.220 and
23.230 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes, and repealing Sections
23.240 and 23.250 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 23.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The regular circuit judges may act as special judges of the
circuit courts. The commission issued to each regular circuit
judge shall have the effect of commissioning him a special judge
of the Commonwealth with jurisdiction coextensive with the
state, and the judge shall remain such as long as he continues
to hold office under his commission as regular judge.
Section 2. Section 23.230 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) When, from any cause, a judge of any circuit court,
whether of continuous session or terms, fails to attend, or being
in attendance cannot properly preside in an action pending in
the court, or if a vacancy occurs or exists in the office of circuit
judge, or if either party files with the clerk of the court his affi
davit that the judge will not afford him a fair and impartial trial,

Chapter 134 395
As used in this Chapter unless the context requires other
wise:
(1) "Commissioner" means the Commissioner of Health;
(2) "Department" means the Department of Health;
(3) "Food" includes any article used by man for food,
drink, confectionery or condiment, or which enters into the
composition of the same whether simple, blended, mixed or
compounded;
(4) "Frozen food locker plant" means a location or estab
lishment in which space in individual lockers is rented to persons
for storage of frozen food and which is equipped with a chill
room, sharp freezing facilities and facilities for cutting, pre
paring, wrapping and packaging meats and meat products,
fruits and vegetables;
(5) "Branch frozen food locker plant" means a location or
establishment in which space in individual lockers is rented to
persons for storage of frozen food after preparation for storage
at a frozen food locker plant;
(6) "Sharp frozen" means the freezing of food in a room
in which the temperature is zero degrees (Fahrenheit) or lower.

forcement of KRS 221.010 to 221.100.

tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:

CHAPTER 135
(H. B. 177)
AN ACT relating to births and deaths.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 213.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The health officer or health administrator in each
county shall be the local registrar of vital statistics in the county.
The health officer or health administrator may appoint one or
more employes of the local health department as deputy regis
trars to discharge the duties of the local registrar as prescribed
by law and by the rules and regulations of the State Department
of Health and may, with the approval of the State Department of
Health, appoint such other deputy registrars as may be necessary
who need not be employes of the local health department.
(2) If any county health department is without the services
of a health officer or health administrator, the principal clerk of
the health department shall be the local registrar until a health
officer or health administrator is appointed.
(3) In counties in which a county department of health is
not maintained the State Department of Health shall appoint a
person to be the local registrar. Such person shall possess the
training and qualifications prescribed by the State Department of
Health, and shall hold office at the pleasure of the department;

The local registrar shall supply blank forms of certificates

all original certificates registered by him. When a birth, still
birth or death certificate filed with a local registrar shows the
residence of the mother or the deceased to be in another county,
the registrar shall mail a copy of the certificate to the local reg
istrar of the county of residence. If no births, stillbirths or deaths
occur in any month, he shall, on the first and fifteenth of each
month, report that fact to the Division of Vital Statistics.

CHAPTER 137
(H. B. 179)
AN ACT relating to the Kentucky Disabled Ex-Service Men's Board.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
( 1 ) There is created a board to be known as the Kentucky
Disabled Ex-Service Men's Board, which shall consitute an inde
pendent agency of the state government within the meaning of
Chapter 12 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. The board shall
be composed of the Lieutenant Governor, who shall act as chair
man, the Adjutant General, and three members appointed by
the Governor. The appointed members shall serve for terms of
four years and until their successors are appointed and have
qualified, except that the members of the first board shall be
so appointed that the terms of one member will be for two years,
another for three years and the other for four years. All appoint
ments to the board, including appointments of successors and
appointments to fill vacancies, shall be made by the Governor,
provided, however, that said appointees shall be veterans with
service during time of war or hostilities wherein American
troops were engaged in conflict with an armed enemy. The

AN ACT relating to the condemnation of property for the transporta
tion of material from a mine or quarry.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 277.040 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
Any person operating a mine or a stone quarry within three
(3) miles of any railroad or navigable stream may, for the pur
pose of transporting material between the railroad or stream and
the mine or quarry, construct and operate a line of railroad,
truck road, overhead conveyor, or pipeline from the mine or
quarry to the most convenient and accessible point on the rail
road or stream, and may, under KRS 416.010 to 416.080, condemn
the land necessary for tract, truck road, or supports for conveyor,
or pipeline, not exceeding fifty (50) feet in width for each track,
roadway, conveyor, or pipeline necessarily constructed, and the
land for necessary buildings at the railroad or stream, not ex
ceeding two (2) acres. The owner or operator of such railroad
shall be governed by the laws relating to other railroads, so far as
applicable, and shall have the same rights and privileges granted
to corporations owning and operating railroads.

until one-half hour before sunrise, and at such other periods as
atmospheric conditions render visibility as low as or lower than is

utes is amended to read as follows:

(3) "Temporary Loan" means any loan by a credit union

utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The membership of the corporation shall consist of

nerships, and corporations, as have been duly elected to member
ship, and have subscribed for one or more shares and have paid
for the shares in whole or in part, together with the entrance
fee as provided in the bylaws and have complied with such other
requirements as the articles of incorporation and the bylaws
specify.

those persons, societies, associations, partnerships, and corpora
tions, and the members of the immediate family living in the
same household of such persons, societies, associations, partner
ships, and corporations, which, independent of their member
ship in the credit union, have a mutual affiliation by either:
(a) Duly enrolled membership in a religious, social or ed
ucational group or association;
(b) Identity of profession, occupation, trade or business,
so long as members of such profession, occupation, trade or busi
ness are bona fide members of a common bona fide profession,
occupation, trade or business group;

Section 5. Section 290.200 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) A credit union may lend its funds to its members at rates
of interest not exceeding one percent per month on the unpaid
balance of the loan, which amount shall be in lieu of all other
charges incident to the making of the loan, except fines for delin
quent payments, and in case of secured loans, premiums incurred
in the carrying of insurance against the loan, mortgage recording
fees, title examination fees, and such other reasonable charges
as are necessarily incident to the effective protection of the loan
by the security offered.
(2) In the case of a temporary loan, a credit union may
charge a fee of twenty-five cents when a loan is in an amount
of twenty-five dollars or less, and a fee of fifty cents when the
loan is in an amount more than twenty-five dollars.
Section 6. Section 290.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
In addition to the manner provided in KRS 386.030, the
capital, deposits, undivided profits, reserve fund and other as
sets of a credit union, to the extent such resources are not uti
lized for loans to members as provided in this Chapter, may be
invested in the following ways:
(1) They may be deposited to the credit of the corporation
in state credit unions; state or national banks doing commercial
banking; Federal credit unions operating in Kentucky; and in
savings and loan associations.
(2) Not more than ten percent of the capital stock and
reserve funds of a credit union may be invested in the stock of
other credit unions, including Federal credit unions operating in
Kentucky.

CHAPTER 145
(H. B. 199)
AN ACT relating to sheriffs' revenue bonds.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Subsection (1) of Section 134.230 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes is amended to read as follows:
The sheriff shall, before he qualifies and before he assumes
the duties of the office for the first time, enter into bond with
surety for the faithful performance of his duties as revenue col
lector. This bond shall cover liability to account, as provided by
law, for all charges of taxes made against him, and for all money
received by him as revenue collector from the date he assumes
the duties of the office to and including May 31 of the same year.
He shall enter into bond with surety thereafter before or as of
June 1 of each succeeding year of the term of his office, except
that the expiration date of the bond for the last year in the term
shall be to and including March 15. The county judge shall hold
court at any time the sheriff may request for the purpose of ex
ecuting bond.
Approved March 13, 1954

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common

Subsection (5) of Section 138.020 of the Kentucky Revised

(a) Nothing in this Section shall be construed to impose
a tax upon admissions to athletic contests, plays, concerts or
other forms of amusements or entertainment presented by non

clubs or associations if no professional performers are employed
and all profits are used exclusively for civic, charitable, religious

sociation, corporation, organization or individual shall be entitled

dered in the ordinary conduct of business.
(b) If any professional entertainers or performers are em
ployed, all amounts paid for admission are subject to tax unless
at least seventy-five per cent of the gross proceeds of admissions
are used for charitable, religious or educational purposes within
this state.

CHAPTER 148
(H. B. 203)
AN ACT relating to motor vehicle liability insurance.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Whenever an automobile dealer or bank or
other lending institution which handles automobile loans pro
cures a policy of insurance for the purchaser of an automobile,
new or used, if such policy does not cover the insured as to
public liability insurance, then such person or institution shall
cause to be stamped or written on the face of the purchaser's
copy of such policy a statement that such policy does not cover
bodily injury or property damage insurance.
Section 2. Any person who shall violate the provisions of
this Act shall be fined not more than one hundred dollars or
imprisoned not more than thirty days, or both.
Approved March 13, 1954

Section 1. Subsection (1) of Section 136.120 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
Every railway company, sleeping car company, chair car
company, dining car company, gas company, water company,
ferry company, bridge company, street railway company, inter-

tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
If the lines or route of a domestic or foreign railway com
pany, gas company, water company, ferry company, bridge com
pany, street railway company, express company, electric light

company, press dispatch company, bus line company, common

Chapter 149 421
Section 3. A new Section of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
created to read as follows:
Boats, tugs, barges and other water craft of any nonresident
person, corporation, partnership, or any other business association
whose route or system is partly within this state and partly within
another state or states, shall be valued by the Department of
Revenue for purposes of taxation and shall be assessed by the
Department of Revenue; and the department shall fairly divide,
allocate and certify such assessments to each county, city, town,
or other taxing district within this state, within or through which
such route or system is operated, the division, allocation, and
certification to be determined in the following manner:
( 1 ) The proportion of the value of the property which the
length of the lines or route operated in this state bears to the
total length of lines or route operated in this state and elsewhere,
shall be considered in fixing the value of the property for taxation
in this state. Any other reasonable evidence of value shall be
considered in fixing the value but such evidence must be pre
scribed by departmental regulations.
(2) After ascertaining the portion of the system valuation
of such property attributable to this state, the Department of
Revenue shall allocate the value of the property among the
counties, cities, towns, and other taxing districts. The propor
tion which the length of line or route operated in that jurisdiction
or taxing district bears to the total length of lines or route oper
ated in this state shall be considered in this allocation and such
other reasonable evidence of value as the Department of Revenue
may by regulations prescribe.
Section 4. A new Section of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
created to read as follows:
On or before March 1, 1955, and each year thereafter, each
nonresident person, corporation, partnership or other business
association owning or operating boats, tugs, barges, or other water
craft whose route or system is partly within this state and partly
within another state or states, shall on forms provided by the
Department of Revenue provide the Department of Revenue
with a detailed description of all such property as well as a de
tailed description of the entire route or system traversed and
such other information as the Department of Revenue may by
regulation prescribe.

CHAPTER 150

AN ACT relating to property taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 133.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The board of supervisors shall convene at the county
seat on the first week day in May in 1954 and on the second
Monday in May in each year thereafter; except that no meeting
shall be held until the tax roll has been completed and until
after the inspection period provided by law. All records of the
county tax commissioner shall be available to the board of
supervisors while meeting.
(2) The board of supervisors shall continue in session
until all appeals are heard and decided; except that it shall
not continue in session more than ten days after it convenes in
1954 nor more than five days after it convenes in each year
thereafter; unless an extension of time is authorized by the
Department of Revenue upon request of the county judge.
Each supervisor shall be paid ten dollars for each day he serves,
except that if the board consists of more than three members,
each supervisor shall be paid five dollars for each day he serves.
This compensation shall be paid one-half out of the county levy
and the other half out of the State Treasury.
Section 2. Section 133.045 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows :
The tax roll shall be open for inspection in the tax com
missioner's office from April 15 to May 1, 1954, and for five days
beginning on the first Monday in May of each year thereafter.
In case of necessity, the Department of Revenue may order a
reasonable extension of time for the inspection period of the
tax roll or it may order that the inspection period be at a different
time than that provided herein. It shall be the duty of the tax
commissioner to publish a notice as to the time of the inspection
period in the newspaper having the largest bona fide circulation
in the county at least three days before the beginning of such
period. The cost of such notice shall be paid by the fiscal court

AN ACT relating to state tuberculosis hospitals and the State Tuber
culosis Hospital Commission.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context requires
otherwise:
( 1 ) "Hospital" means one of the state tuberculosis hospitals
performing medical or surgical treatment;
(2) "Commission" means the State Tuberculosis Hospital
Commission;
(3) "District" means one of the state tuberculosis hospital
districts enumerated in Section 6 of this Act;
(4) "Paying patient" means a person of any race hospitalized
or seeking hospitalization in one of the hospitals who is able to
pay all or any part of the hospitalization cost through insurance
of any kind or by any other means;
(5) "Medically indigent patient" means a person of any
race hospitalized or seeking hospitalization in one of the hospitals
who is unable to pay any part of the hospitalization cost because
of economic conditions and personal responsibilities.
Section 2. ( 1 ) The commission shall consist of five mem
bers. The Commissioner of Health shall be ex efficio a member
and the other four members shall be appointed by the Governor.
The appointed members shall be appointed for terms of four
years. Any vacancy shall be filled by appointment by the Gov
ernor for the unexpired term.
