(3) The members of the commission, other than the Com
missioner of Health, shall receive a per diem of ten dollars for
each meeting attended and all members shall be reimbursed for
their actual and necessary expenses in attending meetings of the
commission and otherwise in performing their duties. The per
diem and expenses of the commission members shall be paid out
of appropriations for the ordinary recurring expenses of operating
the state tuberculosis hospitals.
Section 3. (1) The commission shall have charge of the
government and operation of the hospitals located in each of the
districts enumerated in Section 6 of this Act and shall have
authority to prescribe rules and regulations for that purpose.
( 2 ) All funds and properties of the hospitals shall be under
the management and control of the commission, subject to the
provisions of Chapters 12, 41, 42 and 45 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes.
(3) The commission may receive property by legacy, con
veyance or otherwise, on behalf of the Commonwealth, in aid
and furtherance of the objects of the hospitals or of any individual
hospital. Any donation for a specified use shall be applied to
such use and no other.
(4) The commission shall furnish to the Governor an annual
report covering the operations of the hospitals and such other
reports as the Governor may require.
Section 4. Any appropriations made by the General Assem
bly, including revolving trust or agency fund appropriations, for
the ordinary recurring expenses of operation of the hospitals,
shall be made available to the commission. The commission, with
the approval of the Commissioner of Finance, shall allocate and
apportion such appropriations among the various hospitals as in
its judgment will best meet the needs and purposes of the
hospitals.

Chapter 151 427
for the total administration of the hospitals, within policies
established by the commission, and for other such duties as may
be prescribed by the commission. The executive director shall
have such qualifications as may be prescribed by the commission
and the Division of Personnel Efficiency of the Department of
Finance.
Section 8. (1) The commission shall employ and fix the
compensation of such qualified professional, technical and ad
ministrative personnel as it deems necessary for the operation of
the hospitals and may regulate the terms of service and types of
duties.
( 2 ) Physicians serving in executive capacities shall be gradu
ates from accredited schools of medicine; have full licenses to
practice medicine in the state of Kentucky, have had at least five
years of clinical experience in the field of tuberculosis and be
members, in good standing, of the Kentucky State Medical As
sociation.
( 3 ) Physicians not serving in executive capacities shall have
licenses to practice medicine in Kentucky.
(4) Other professional, technical and administrative per
sonnel serving in executive capacities shall have such professional
qualifications as may be prescribed by the commission and the
Division of Personnel Efficiency of the Department of Finance.
Section 9. The commission shall prescribe the method of
allocation of beds and in so doing shall make available for medi
cally indigent patients at least one-half of the accommodations.
Subject to the approval of the Governor, the commission shall
fix rates for hospitalization.
Section 10. ( 1 ) Subject to the provisions of Sections 9 and
11 of this Act, subsection (2) of this Section, and to such rules
and regulations as the commission may prescribe, any medically
indigent patient or paying patient who is a resident of the hos
pital district, sent to the hospital by any county or city board of
health physician or private physician of the hospital district, with
the certification as to economic conditions and residential status
by the county judge of the county of which the patient is a resi
dent, may be admitted for treatment.

Section 1. Section 186.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Before the owner or operator of a motor vehicle, other
than a motor vehicle engaged in the transportation of passengers
for hire operating under a certificate of convenience and neces
sity, may operate it or permit its operation upon a highway,
the owner shall apply for registration to the county clerk of
the county in which he resides except if the motor vehicle is
to be operated principally in a county other than the county of
the owner's residence, the owner may apply for registration
to the county clerk of the county in which the motor vehicle
is to be principally operated. A motor vehicle last registered
in another state and purchased by a person for operation in this
state shall be first registered in this state by and in the name
of the purchaser and not in the name of the seller. The applica
tion shall be retained by the clerk and shall be accompanied by:
(a) A bill of sale, if the application is for the registration
of a new motor vehicle; or
(b) The owner's registration receipt, if the motor vehicle
was last registered in this state; or
(c) A bill of sale and the previous registration receipt, if
last registered in another state where the law of such state does
not require the owner of a motor vehicle to obtain a certificate
of title or ownership; or
(d) A certificate of title, if last registered in another state
where the law of such state requires the owner of a motor vehicle
to obtain a certificate of title or ownership.
After that, he shall register his motor vehicle on or before
March 1 of each year. Insofar as the owner is concerned,
registration with the clerk shall be deemed to be registration
with the department.

state is unable to renew the registration of his motor vehicle

hours during which said parking facility is open for business. It

ation of such parking facility, unless the entrance to said parking
lot or garage is controlled by a mechanically operated gate, door
or other entrance device which is operated by the customer or
unless the parking lot is operated on an honor system which re
quires no attendant, or by a system of meters at each individual
stall. This Section shall not apply to operators who lease a
specific space to a customer for a period of a month or longer at
a time.
Section 3. Every operator of an automobile parking lot or
storage garage who takes possession of the customers automobile
and requires keys to be left with the automobile or the employees
of such parking lot or garage, shall be liable as bailee for hire
and shall be obligated to safely keep and return such automo
biles so parked or stored and shall be liable for any loss or damage
to such automobiles except such as is caused by persons or forces
beyond the control of the operator and his employees. The lia
bility shall expressly include liability for theft, and for damage re
sulting from collision of any type on the lot or in the garage
where the event is in the control of or should be in the control
of the operator and his employees.
Section 4. The customer may contract to check personal
articles, not properly the equipment of an automobile, by obtain
ing an additional check for such articles. All parking lots and
garages which park or store automobiles for hire and assume to
act as bailees for said automobile shall post a sign of not less than
24 inches by 36 inches having bold lettering bearing the inscrip

classification.title: An Act relating to automobile parking lots and storage garages.
classification.summary: This act establishes regulations for automobile parking lots and storage garages. It declares that contracts limiting an operator's liability for loss or damage to parked vehicles, except for acts of nature or forces beyond control, are against public policy. It mandates that parking facilities post their operating hours and have an attendant on duty unless automated systems or honor systems are in place. Operators who require keys to be left with the vehicle are liable as bailees for hire, including liability for theft and damage from collisions within their control. Customers can check personal articles separately, and facilities must post a sign stating they are only responsible for contents checked separately.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation of automobile parking lots and storage garages, focusing on liability, operating hours, and attendant duties. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law. ment of a public health taxing district for the county it shall be so
established and shall constitute and be a taxing district within

of the public health taxing district and shall perform the duties
attendant thereto in addition to their duties as members of the
county or city-county board of health. The officers of the county
or city-county board of health shall be the officers of the public
health taxing district.
Section 2. If, after the establishment of the public health
taxing district, as provided in Section 1 of this Act, the tax levy
ing authorities of the district, in the opinion of the county or
city-county board of health, do not appropriate an amount suffi
cient to meet the public health needs of the county or the city-

cient to meet the standards prescribed by the State Board of

pose by resolution a special ad valorem public health tax in such

copy of said resolution, include in the next county ad valorem tax
levy said special public health tax imposed by the county or city-

classification.title: An Act relating to public health taxing districts
classification.summary: This act allows for the establishment of public health taxing districts within counties or city-counties. It outlines the process for voter approval, the composition and duties of the governing body (county or city-county board of health), and the mechanism for imposing a special ad valorem public health tax if the initial appropriation is deemed insufficient by the board of health, with approval from the State Department of Health. The tax is to be used solely for the maintenance and operation of the county or city-county health department.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the establishment and operation of public health taxing districts. It focuses on the administrative and financial mechanisms for funding local health departments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 157
(H. B. 222)
AN ACT relating to public health.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The General Assembly of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky recognizes and hereby declares that it is an essential
function, duty and responsibility of the government of this Com
monwealth to adequately safeguard the health of all its citizens,
and to establish, maintain, implement, promote and conduct
appropriate facilities and services for the purpose of protecting
the public health. This Act is enacted with the expressed legis
lative intention of effectuating the aforesaid objectives in the
manner provided herein.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 456

classification.title: AN ACT relating to public health.
classification.summary: This act declares that safeguarding public health is an essential government function and establishes provisions for funding public health departments through county ad valorem taxes, subject to voter approval for increases or decreases. It also repeals Section 212.150 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns public health funding and governance. It outlines a process for county voters to approve or reject changes to the maximum tax rate for public health departments. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial discrimination. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

Section 14. Whenever, in the opinion of the commissioner,
a public health emergency exists in any county, or whenever any
county fails to establish, maintain and operate a local health de
partment therein meeting the standards prescribed by the de
partment, the department may assign to said county such of its
own personnel as may be designated by the commissioner. Such
personnel so assigned shall have the full power and authority of
local health department employees in addition to their power
and authority as representatives of the department. Whenever
such assignment results by reason of the lack of a local health
department or of a local health department meeting the standards
prescribed by the department, any funds appropriated or allo
cated to the local health department by either the Common
wealth or the Federal government may be used to reimburse the
department.
Section 15. The department may examine into all nuisances,
sources of filth and causes of sickness that may, in its opinion,
be injurious to the health of the inhabitants in any county in this
state, or in any vessel within any harbor or port in any county in
this state. Whenever any such nuisance, source of filth or cause
of sickness is found to exist on any private property, or in any
vessel within any port or harbor in any county in this state, or
upon any watercourse in this state, the department may order, in
writing, the owner or occupant thereof, at his own expense, to
remove the same within twenty-four hours, or within such rea
sonable time thereafter as the department may order.

classification.title: An Act relating to the enforcement of public health laws
classification.summary: This act outlines the powers and procedures for public health officials in Kentucky. It grants investigators and inspectors the authority to enter premises, view evidence, and interrogate individuals for the purpose of enforcing public health laws. It also details the process for issuing subpoenas and compelling obedience through circuit courts in cases of non-compliance or refusal to testify. Furthermore, it mandates that county, city, and Commonwealth's attorneys, along with the Attorney General, prosecute violations of public health laws. Finally, it prohibits department employees and officers from engaging in active political management or campaigns, or using their office to influence elections or coerce political action.
classification.reasoning: The provided statute text details the enforcement mechanisms for public health laws in Kentucky. It grants broad powers to health department officials for inspections, investigations, and legal proceedings. It also includes provisions regarding the prosecution of violations and restrictions on political activities for department employees. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law.

classification.title: An Act relating to taxation of property.
classification.summary: This act specifies certain classes of property that are exempt from state taxation. These include farm implements and machinery, livestock, capital stock of building and loan associations, manufacturing machinery and products, unmanufactured agricultural products (with some exceptions for local taxation on tobacco and other products), and money, notes, bonds, accounts, credits, and shares of stock (with exceptions for local taxation of corporate franchises, bank shares, and domestic life insurance companies). The act became effective for the taxable year 1955.

446 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 160
(H. B. 246)
AN ACT relating to the providing of facilities for circuit courts and
county officers by fiscal courts in counties in which circuit court is,
or may be, held at two places, and providing for the issuance of rev
enue bonds for the payment for construction involved therein, and
amending Section 23.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 23.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is hereby
amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) Any city, or portion of a city, coming within the pro
visions of KRS 23.060 shall be entitled to the benefits of KRS
23.060 to 23.080, whenever suitable provision is made by the
fiscal court for furnishing a courtroom and a safe and suitable
vault and depository for all books, papers and records pertaining
to the circuit court in such city. Whenever it is made to appear
to the circuit judge that the necessary provisions for holding
circuit court have been made in the city, the judge, upon ap
plication of the city on the first day of the first term after filing
the application, shall enter an order directing the latter portion
of said term and each term thereafter to be held in the city,
and all cases shall be assigned for trial at one or the other of
said places.
( 2 ) In counties having a population of less than one hun
dred and fifty thousand and containing a city of the second class
which is not the county seat and in which city the fiscal court
does not own, operate or maintain a courthouse, where terms
of the circuit court are held in both the county seat and the city
of the second class, the fiscal court shall provide quarters for all
courts and county officers in said city of the second class and
in the county seat. The fiscal court may acquire land for the
purpose of constructing a courthouse, and may issue bonds to
provide for the payment of said construction, or it may contract
with the city of the second class for the rent or lease of suitable
quarters for the holding of court and the offices for count)7
officers for a rental of not less than one thousand dollars per
month, nor more than two thousand dollars per month, the terms
of the lease to be agreed upon by the fiscal court of such county
and the governing body of such city.
Approved March 24, 1954
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 467

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the providing of facilities for circuit courts and county officers by fiscal courts in counties in which circuit court is, or may be, held at two places, and providing for the issuance of revenue bonds for the payment for construction involved therein, and amending Section 23.090 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
classification.summary: This act amends KRS 23.090 to allow fiscal courts to provide facilities for circuit courts and county officers in counties where circuit court is held in two locations. It outlines requirements for furnishing courtrooms and vaults, and allows fiscal courts to acquire land, issue bonds for construction, or contract with cities for rental of suitable quarters. This applies to counties with a population under 150,000 containing a second-class city that is not the county seat and where the fiscal court does not own a courthouse.
classification.reasoning: The statute addresses the logistical and financial aspects of establishing and maintaining court facilities in counties with multiple locations for circuit court. It focuses on the responsibilities of fiscal courts and the acquisition or leasing of property for court operations. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly suggest racial segregation or discrimination. The statute's scope is purely administrative and financial regarding court infrastructure. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 161
(H. B. 247)
AN ACT relating to state ad valorem taxes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 132.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is herebv
amended to read as follows:
An annual ad valorem tax for state purposes of five cents
upon each one hundred dollars of value of all real property di
rected to be assessed for taxation, and five cents upon each one
hundred dollars of value of all tobacco directed to be assessed
for taxation, and twenty-five cents upon each one hundred dollars
of value of all money in hand, shares of stock, notes, bonds, ac
counts and other credits, whether secured by mortgage, pledge or
otherwise, or unsecured, five cents upon each one hundred dollars
of value of unmanufactured agricultural products, and fifty
cents upon each one hundred dollars of value of all property
directed to be assessed for taxation shall be paid by the owner
or person assessed except as provided in KRS 132.030, 132.050,
132.200, 136.270, 136.300, 136.320 and other Sections providing
a different tax rate for particular property.
This Act shall become effective on September 1, 1956.
Approved March 22, 1954

gineering, a department of sanitation and a department of build

CHAPTER 163
(H. B. 255)
AN ACT relating to the regulation of buildings, dwellings and structures
by cities of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. In order to protect the health, safety and gen
eral welfare of its citizens and the public at large, a city
of the first class may enact and enforce ordinances regulating
the construction, reconstruction, alteration, maintenance, sani
tation, sanitary facilities, inspection, rehabilitation, safety and
control of, and the prevention of fire in, buildings, dwellings
and structures of all types and descriptions, whether used for
human habitation or otherwise, within such city.

classification.title: Amendments to Kentucky Revised Statutes concerning city finances and pensions
classification.summary: This statute amends several sections of the Kentucky Revised Statutes. Section 1 amends KRS 90.230 to grant legislative bodies of first-class cities the power to pass ordinances and make appropriations for purposes outlined in KRS 90.110 to 90.240. Section 2 amends KRS 95.290 to allow city legislative bodies in first-class cities to enact ordinances for pension systems for retired and disabled police and fire department members, including appropriating funds and allotting fines and forfeitures. Section 3 amends KRS 97.700 to require annual appropriations from the general fund for maintaining city memorials and carrying on the work of commissions, supplemented by gifts or earnings.

CHAPTER 165
(H. B. 257)
AN ACT relating to the finance and revenue of boards of registration
commissioners of cities of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Subsection (1) of Section 117.705 of the Kentucky Re
vised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(1) In order to meet the expenses of the board of registra
tion commissioners, the legislative body of each city of the
first class shall annually appropriate from the general fund of
the city such sums as in the judgment of such legislative body
shall be reasonably necessary for such purposes. Funds so
appropriated may be paid over to such board by the director
of finance of such city in regular monthly installments and shall
be receipted for by the board.
Approved March 1, 1954

CHAPTER 166
(H. B. 258)
AN ACT relating to the finance and revenue of municipal universities
in cities of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 165.030 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The board of aldermen of a city of the first class having
a municipal university may make an annual appropriation from
the general fund of the city for the support of such university.
Such appropriation shall be such sum as in the judgment of
the board of aldermen shall, when supplemented by other
income of the university, be reasonably necessary for such
purposes.
Section 2. Section 165.040 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The funds appropriated by the board of aldermen of a
city of the first class as provided in KRS 165.030 may be paid
to the treasurer of the university by the director of finance of
the city in regular monthly installments.
Section 3. Section 165.050 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The board of aldermen of a city of the first class having
a municipal university may devote to university purposes funds
derived from other sources as well as from taxes levied for
general city purposes.
Approved March 1, 1954

CHAPTER 170
(H. B. 262)
AN ACT relating to the finance and revenue of joint city-county air
boards in counties containing a city of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 183.190 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
In order to provide money for the purchase of lands and
other property necessary for the establishment of airports, and
to establish, equip and maintain necessary buildings for the pur
poses of airports, the fiscal court of the county in which such
airports exists may make an annual appropriation from the gen
eral fund of the county, and the legislative body of each city of
the first class within such county may make an annual appropria
tion from the general fund of such city. The appropriations,
when in the judgment of the fiscal court and such legislative
bodies reasonably necessary for such purposes, shall be made by
the county and such cities in such amounts, in such proportion
and upon such terms as the fiscal court of the county and the
legislative bodies of such cities may agree and provide. All funds
derived from such appropriations shall be turned over to the
board for the purposes set forth in KRS 183.150. Monies so ap
propriated may be paid over to the board by the county and the
cities in regular monthly installments.
Section 2. Section 183.200 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The board may borrow money on its own credit in antici
pation of the revenue to be derived from appropriations or other
income for the fiscal year in which the money is borrowed, and
for such purposes the board may pledge the appropriations, if
any, authorized under KRS 183.190.
Approved March 1, 1954

AN ACT relating to the giving of notice to cities of claims for injuries
growing out of defects in public ways.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 411.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
No action shall be maintained against any city in this state
because of any injury growing out of any defect in the condition
of any bridge, street, sidewalk, alley or other public thorough
fare, unless notice has been given to the mayor, city clerk or
clerk of the board of aldermen in the manner provided for
the service of notice in actions in the Rules of Civil Procedure.
This notice shall be filed within ninety days of the occurrence
for which damage is claimed, stating the time of and place
where the injury was received and the character and circum
stances of the injury, and that the person injured will claim
damages therefor from the city.
Approved March 1, 1954

Chapter 175 467
one who with or without the use of cards, crystal ball, tea leaves,
or any other object or device, engages in the practice of telling
the fortune of another (but this last clause shall not be construed
to apply to persons pretending to tell fortunes as part of any play,
exhibition, fair or amateur show presented or offered by any
religious, charitable, or benevolent institution). It shall not mean
a private home at which bona fide guests are entertained, drive-
in theatres, places of business conducted only as filling stations
for motor vehicles or grocery stores, nor transient or temporary
entertainment such as circuses, carnivals and county fairs.
Section 2. Section 231.110 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
No person who has been granted a permit shall allow:
(1) Drunken, disorderly or boisterous persons, or persons
of lewd or lascivious reputation to congregate in or about the
premises.
(2) People to congregate there for immoral or unlawful
purposes or to permit any man or woman who are not married
to each other to occupy any cabin, cottage or secreted room or
place from which the view of the public is excluded.
(3) The premises to be used as a place of assemblage or
entertainment at later hours than those which are stated in the
permit or recorded on the order book of the county court.
(4) Engaging in fortunetelling at any location except that
specifically stated in his permit.
(5) Engaging in fortunetelling without first posting in a
conspicuous place, both inside and outside the premises at which
he is authorized to engage in fortunetelling, and without first
filing with the county clerk of the county in which the premises
are located, a schedule showing in detail the fees charged for
readings, predictions, and services of any nature.
Section 3. A new Section of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is created to read as follows:
The conviction of the owner or manager of a place of enter
tainment for violation of the alcohol beverage control laws may
be an additional ground for revocation and forfeiture of the place
of entertainment permit by the county court.
Approved March 24, 1954

CHAPTER 176
(H. B. 277)
AN ACT relating to the compensation of certain elective state officers.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 64.480 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
Effective, with respect to the offices of Governor and Lieu
tenant Governor, on the fifth Tuesday following the regular
November election in 1955, and with respect to the other
offices named in this Section, on the first Monday in January,
1956, the compensation of the following named officers, pay
able monthly out of the State Treasury, shall be the sum per
annum designated for the respective offices, as follows:
Governor, $15,000.
Lieutenant Governor, $6,000, this to be in addition to any
per diem received during sessions of the General Assembly
while serving in the capacity of President of the Senate, and
any compensation received while acting in the place of the
Governor.
Attorney General, $11,000.
Superintendent of Public Instruction, $10,000.
Commissioner of Agriculture, Labor and Statistics, $9,000.00.
Secretary of State, $9,000.00.
State Treasurer, $9,000.00.
Auditor of Public Accounts, $9,000.00.
Clerk of the Court of Appeals, $9,000.00.

472 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 178
(H. B. 280)
AN ACT relating to transportation of malt beverages by distributors and
amending KRS 243.180.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 243.180 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is amended
to read as follows:
(1) A distributor's license shall authorize the licensee to
purchase, import or store malt beverages and to sell them only,
from the licensed premises, to other distributors, to retailers or
to consumers for personal use and not for resale. A distributor
shall transport malt beverages only by a vehicle owned, rented
or leased and operated by himself, which has affixed to its sides
at all times a sign of form and size prescribed by the state board,
containing among other things the name and license number of
the licensee. No distilled spirits or wine shall be transported
on the same truck or vehicle with malt beverages, except by a
common carrier.
(2) A distributor's license must be obtained for each
separate warehouse, agent, distributor, broker, jobber or place
of business from which orders are received or beverages are
distributed unless it is a licensed brewery.
Became law without Governor's signature March 24, 1954
CHAPTER 179
(H. B. 281)
AN ACT relating to the collection of state, county and district taxes,
amending Sections 134.290 and 134.310 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes and declaring an emergency.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 134.290 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is hereby amended to read as follows:
(1) The sheriff shall be allowed by the Department of
Revenue, for collecting state taxes, a commission of ten percent
upon the first five thousand dollars and four percent upon the
residue collected or accounted for and paid into the State Treas
ury in each year.

Chapter 179 473
(2) He shall be allowed by the county treasurer, for col
lecting the county revenue, ten percent upon the first five thou
sand dollars collected, and four percent upon the residue.
(3) In counties containing a population of less than sev
enty-five thousand, the aggregate annual compensation of the
sheriff for official services shall not exceed seven thousand two
hundred dollars, independent of the compensation of legally au
thorized deputies and assistants and reasonable expenses as
limited by the fiscal court.
Section 2. Section 134.310 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is hereby amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) The sheriff shall annually settle his accounts for county
and district taxes with the fiscal court, before making settlement
with the Department of Revenue. At the regular October term
each year the fiscal court shall appoint some competent person
other than the Commonwealth's or county attorney to settle the
accounts of the sheriff for money due the county or district.
The report of this settlement shall be filed in the county
clerk's office and approved by the county court. The settlement
shall show the amount of poll tax and ad valorem tax collected,
and an itemized statement of the money disbursed.
(2) The settlement shall be published for at least two
weeks in a paper published and having general circulation
in the county or, if there is no such paper, it shall be published
by handbills posted at the front door of the courthouse and at
least three other public places in the county. The report of
the settlement shall be subject to exceptions by the sheriff or by
the county attorney, who shall represent the state and county,
and the county court shall try and determine the exceptions.
( 3 ) An appeal may be prosecuted by either party from the
judgment of the county court on the settlement, in the same
manner as provided by law for appeals from judgments of the
quarterly court, except that the county attorney shall not be
required to give an appeal bond; or actions may be instituted
in any court of competent jurisdiction to correct the settlement.
The settlement, when approved, shall be recorded in the county
clerk's office.
(4) On the final settlement the sheriff shall pay to the
county treasurer all money that remains in his hands, and take
receipts as provided in KRS 134.300.

(5) In counties containing a population of less than

(a) A complete statement of all funds received by his

(6) There shall be deducted from the total of such ex

of such expenses, shall be deducted the commissions allowed
for collecting state and county taxes as prescribed by sub
sections (1) and (2) of Section 1 of this Act. The balance,
if any, of the expenses shall be paid to the sheriff from the
commissions allowed to him for the collection of school taxes,
but in no event shall the amount of such commissions exceed
four percent of the total amount of school taxes collected by
him. The amount paid the sheriff for collecting school taxes
shall be prorated among the school districts in the county in
the proportion that the amount of school taxes collected from
each district bears to the total school taxes collected.
Whereas, conflict and confusion now exists in many coun
ties of the Commonwealth as to the manner of determining rea
sonable compensation of the sheriff for collecting school taxes; and
Whereas, it is necessary to have a fair, reasonable and
certain method for determining the compensation which the
sheriff shall receive for collecting school taxes,
Now, Therefore, an emergency is hereby declared to
exist, and this Act shall become effective upon its passage and
approval by the Governor.
Approved March 13, 1954

Section 8. The commissioner shall notify any applicant or
owner by registered mail of any decision of the board to refuse
to grant a license or to revoke a license within ten days after
such decision, and the applicant or owner shall have thirty days
after such notification within which to petition the commis
sioner for a rehearing. The commissioner shall within ten
days after the said rehearing notify the applicant or owner
of its decision, and if the decision be unfavorable, said applicant
or owner shall have thirty days within which to appeal such
decision to the circuit court of the county in which he resides
or maintains his principal place of business. If an owner takes
such appeal within the time herein provided, the revocation
by the board shall not become final until finally adjudicated,
however the board may require the posting of a reasonable and
appropriate bond pending final adjudication.
Section 9. This Act shall not apply to any individual work
ing on his own premises, or to regularly employed wage hands
working thereon, or to persons who furnish or sell insecticides,
fungicides or rodenticides for application or use by others, or to
aerial applicators holding a valid permit from the Department of
Aeronautics.
Section 10. The monies received from the license fees col
lected by the department hereunder shall be deposited in the
State Treasury to the credit of the General Fund.
Section 11. Any person violating any of the provisions of
this Act shall upon conviction thereof be guilty of a misde
meanor. Each separate violation of this Act shall be a separate
misdemeanor, punishable by a fine of not more than five hun
dred dollars nor more than one year in jail, or both.
Section 12. Whereas, the lack of adequate control of the
use of insecticides, fungicides and rodenticides has proved dan
gerous to human health and welfare, and
Whereas, it is now imperative that the use of such chemicals
be immediately controlled, now
Therefore, an emergency is declared to exist and this Act
shall be in full force and effect immediately upon its passage
and approval by the Governor.
Approved March 24, 1954

CHAPTER 181
(H. B. 286)
AN ACT relating to the promotion of the Rural Kentucky Medical
Scholarship Fund by the State Department of Health and making
an appropriation therefor.
WHEREAS, the Rural Kentucky Medical Scholarship Fund is a re
volving fund which was established in 1946 by the Kentucky State
Medical Association in cooperation with the University of Louisville
School of Medicine as a means of providing better medical care for the
people of rural areas in Kentucky by the careful selection of students
who are willing to practice medicine in a rural area for at least as many
years as they receive financial assistance from the fund; and
WHEREAS, the fund is administered by a board of trustees com
posed of the Dean of the Medical School chartered in the State of Ken
tucky, the State Health Commissioner, representatives from the Ken
tucky State Medical Association, the Kentucky Congress of Parents and
Teachers, the Kentucky State Bar, the University of Louisville School
of Medicine, the Kentucky Press, Kentucky Industry, Kentucky Farm
Bureau and any other persons deemed necessary; and
WHEREAS, applications are welcomed from any young man or
woman who is interested in becoming a doctor of medicine and prac
ticing in a rural area in Kentucky,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Kentucky State Department of Health shall
aid, assist and promote the Rural Kentucky Medical Scholarship
Fund with such sums as may, from time to time, be appropriated
by the General Assembly for this purpose.
Section 2. For the purposes of this Act, there is hereby
appropriated to the State Department of Health out of the
General Expenditure Fund in the State Treasury, the sum of
five thousand dollars per year for each of the fiscal years 1954-55
and 1955-56 without lapse.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 183 483
the public interest. Optometry is hereby declared to be a
learned profession and it is further declared to be a matter of
public interest and concern that the practice of optometry as
defined in this Act and the optometric profession merit and
receive the confidence of the public and that only qualified
persons be admitted to the practice of optometry and be per
mitted to so practice in the Commonwealth of Kentucky. This
Act shall be liberally construed to carry out these objects and
purposes in accordance with this declaration of policy.
Section 2. As used in this Act, unless the context requires
otherwise:
(1) "Board" means the Kentucky Board of Optometric
Examiners.
(2) "Practice of Optometry" means the employment of
any means other than the use of drugs, medicine or surgery to
examine the human eye, to determine the visual efficiency of
the human eye, or to determine the powers or defects of vision;
the prescribing, providing, furnishing, adapting, using or em
ploying lenses, prisms, contact lenses, visual training, orthoptics,
ocular exercise or any other means or device other than the
use of drugs, medicine or surgery for the aid, relief or cor
rection of vision.
(3) "Visual Aid Glasses" means eyeglasses, spectacles or
lenses designed or used to correct visual defects; provided,
however, that nothing in the provisions of this Act relating
to the practice of optometry shall be construed to limit or re
strict, in any respect, the sale of sunglasses designed and used
solely to filter out light; and further provided that nothing
in this Act relating to the practice of optometry shall be con
strued to limit or restrict, in any respect, the sale of completely
assembled eyeglasses or spectacles designed and used solely to
magnify.
An orthoptic technician is a person who trains and directs
individuals to engage in ocular exercises designed to correct
visual defects, and shall not be required to be licensed under
the provisions of this Act provided that such training and di
rections are done pursuant to and under the instructions of
a duly licensed physician, osteopath or optometrist and con
sists solely of visual training, orthoptics or ocular exercises.

Section 4. (1) The Kentucky Board of Optometric Exam
iners shall consist of five practicing optometrists well grounded in
the practice of optometry appointed by the Governor from a list
containing three names for each appointment recommended by
the Kentucky Optometric Association. The members shall serve
for a term of four years and until their successors are appointed
and have qualified. They shall serve without compensation but
shall be reimbursed for actual expenses incurred in the carry
ing out of their duties under this Act.
(2) Each member shall be well grounded in the theory
and practice of the profession of optometry and shall have
been an actual resident and licensed practicing optometrist of
this state for not less than five years immediately preceding
his appointment to the board. He shall not be in any way con
nected with or interested in any optometric school, college or
institution of learning or optometric supply business.
(3) Any vacancy occurring on the board as the result of
a member not completing the term for which he was appointed
shall be filled, for the unexpired term, in the same manner as
is provided in subsection (1) for the appointment of members.

stitute a quorum for the transaction of business.

tucky Board of Optometric Examiners under the provisions

Chapter 183 485
provisions of this Act and shall serve until the expiration of the
term for which they were appointed and until their successors

Section 5. (1) The board shall meet at least once each
year at which time it shall choose from among its members the
president, vice-president, and secretary-treasurer. In addition,
the board upon call of its officers, may hold meetings at such time
as it deems necessary. The secretary-treasurer shall keep a full
record of its proceedings which shall be open to inspection at all
reasonable times.
(2) The board shall keep a register containing the name,
address and license number of every person licensed to prac
tice optometry in this state.
(3) The Attorney General shall render to the board such
legal services as it may require in carrying out and enforcing
the provisions of this Act.
(4) Subject to and consistent with the provisions of this
Act, the board shall make and promulgate reasonable rules and
regulations and do any and all things which it may deem neces
sary or proper for the effective enforcement of this Act and
for the full and efficient performance of its duties hereunder
and the reasonable regulation of the profession of optometry
and the practice thereof by licensed optometrists.
(5) The board shall have a common seal.
(6) The board shall have authority to administer oaths
and to require the attendance of witnesses, the production of
books, records and papers pertinent to any matters coming be
fore the board by the issuance of process which shall be served
and returned in the same manner as in civil actions and for the
disobedience of which the board shall have the power to in
voke the same rights as are provided in the event of disobedience
of a subpoena or subpoena duces tecum in a civil action.
(7) The board shall make rules and regulations govern
ing applicants and applications for registration or license under
this Act and governing the conduct of licensing examinations.
(8) The board shall from time to time accredit schools
and colleges teaching optometry, the graduates of which shall
be eligible to licensing examinations.

Section 13. (1) Proceedings to revoke or suspend a license
may be taken by the board from the matters within its knowledge
or upon the information of another. If the informant is a member
of the board, the other members shall constitute the board for
the purpose of judging the accused. Every accusation shall be
in writing, verified by some person familiar with the facts
charged in it. Three copies shall be filed with the secretary-
treasurer of the board. Upon receiving the accusation the
board shall, if it considers it sufficient, make an order setting
the accusation for hearing at a specified time and place, and
requiring the accused to appear and answer it. The secretary-
treasurer shall cause a copy of the order and accusation to be
served upon the accused at least ten days before the day ap
pointed in the order for the hearing.
(2) The person accused shall appear at the time appointed
in the order, answer the charges in writing and make his de
fense, unless for sufficient cause the board assigns another day
for that purpose. If he does not appear the board may proceed
and determine the accusation in his absence. If the accused
pleads guilty or refuses to answer the charges, or if upon the
hearing the board finds any of them true, it may proceed to a
judgment of either revoking his license or suspending it. The
board and the accused may have the benefit of counsel. The
board may administer oaths, take the depositions of witnesses
as in civil cases and compel them to attend before it in person
by the issuance of subpoenas over the signature of the secretary

Section 14. A licensee whose license has been revoked by
the board may appeal from the revocation order within thirty
days of the cancellation of his license. The appeal shall be to
the circuit court of the county in which the accused regularly
practices. Any person who takes such an appeal shall serve
upon the secretary-treasurer, a written notice of the appeal, and
shall file with the clerk of the circuit court to which such
appeal is taken a certified copy, under the seal of the board,
to be approved by the clerk, to secure the payment of any
costs charged against him upon the appeal. The secretary-
treasurer, within ten days after the service of notice of appeal,
shall transmit to the clerk of the circuit court to which the
appeal is taken a certified copy, under the seal of the board,
of the accusations on which the revocation was based and of
the order of revocation. The clerk of the circuit court shall
docket the case. It shall be tried de novo and in all respects
like an ordinary civil action. Either party may appeal from
the judgment of the circuit court to the Court of Appeals as in
a civil action. The judgment shall be stayed from the date of
the approval of the bond until final determination of the appeal.
Section 15. Upon application the board may reissue a
license to practice optometry that has been revoked. No such
application shall be made prior to one year after revocation.
The board may prescribe the manner and form of application.

CHAPTER 185

AN ACT relating to the Youth Authority, and amending Sections 208.570
and 208.580 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 208.570 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The director of the Youth Authority shall be the executive
officer of the authority and shall be responsible for the ad
ministration of the functions of the authority, in accordance
with policies established by the Commissioner of Welfare. The
director shall serve as secretary of the Youth Authority Board.
Administrative personnel of the authority shall be appointed by
the director.
Section 2. Section 208.580 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
The governing board of the Youth Authority shall serve in
an advisory capacity and shall recommend policies for carrying
out the functions of the authority to the Commissioner of
Welfare and to the director of the Youth Authority. The Com
missioner of Welfare shall revise the policies of the Youth Au
thority in accordance with the recommendations of the board,
and the disposition of children committed to the Youth Authority
shall be in accordance with the policies so established. At
least once each year the board shall review the case of each
child committed to the authority, so long as the child remains
under the jurisdiction of the authority.
Approved March 13, 1954

CHAPTER 187
(H. B. 298)
AN ACT relating to the Department of Finance.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 42.020 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The Department of Finance shall be headed by a Com
missioner of Finance and shall be divided for administrative pur
poses into a Division of the Budget, a Division of Accounts, a
Division of Purchases, a Division of Personnel, and a Division of
Services.
(2) The Commissioner of Finance shall be the chief finan
cial officer of the state and the advisor of the Governor and the
General Assembly in financial matters, and shall at all times
protect the financial interests of the state. He shall be responsible
to the Governor for the administration of the department.
(3) The Commissioner of Finance may designate a deputy
commissioner of finance who shall have such administrative
authority to sign for the commissioner as the commissioner
designates in writing. The deputy commissioner may be a
director of one of the divisions of the Department of Finance.

Section 2. Section 42.050 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The Division of Accounts shall be headed by a con
troller, preferably a certified public accountant, who has had
substantial experience during the preceding four years in Federal,
state or local accounting or auditing.
(2) The Division of Accounts shall perform such record

Section 3. Section 42.060 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The Division of Purchases shall be headed by a pur
chasing agent, who has had four years of responsible experience
in purchasing or equivalent experience.
(2) The Division of Purchases, under the direction of the
Commissioner of Finance, shall purchase for the combined re
quirements of all spending agencies of the state all supplies,
materials, and equipment, except library books and scientific
equipment, or shall control or delegate the purchase thereof.
The division shall have supervision over all purchases by the
various spending agencies, except as otherwise provided by law,
and may prescribe rules to govern purchasing by or for all such
agencies, subject to the approval of the Commissioner of Finance;
and no purchase or contract shall be binding on the state or any
agency thereof unless approved by the division or made under
general regulations which the Department of Finance may
prescribe. The division shall maintain perpetual inventories of
plant and equipment. It shall have power, with the approval
of the Commissioner of Finance, to transfer between depart
ments, to salvage, to exchange, and to condemn supplies and
equipment. It shall sell all property (including real estate) of
the state determined, with the approval of the Commissioner of
Finance and the Governor, not to be needed for public use
or to have become unsuitable for such use. All deeds covering
the sale of real estate shall be executed and signed by the Com
missioner of Finance and approved and signed by the Governor.

the occupation in which he was engaged at the time of his ap
pointment or election even though the pursuit thereof should
include the legitimate solicitation of business from the Common
wealth of Kentucky, either for a salary or a commission.

CHAPTER 188
(H. B. 299)
AN ACT relating to motor transportation and motor vehicles.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 281.010 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
As used in this Chapter, unless the context requires other
wise:
( 1 ) The term "person" means any individual, firm, partner
ship, corporation, company, association or joint stock association,
and includes any trustee, assignee, or personal representative
thereof.
(2) The term "department" means the Department of
Motor Transportation.
(3) The term "certificate" means a certificate of public
convenience and necessity issued under this Chapter to common
carriers by motor vehicle authorizing their operation thereunder.
(4) The term "permit" means a permit issued under this
Chapter to contract carriers by motors vehicle and to persons
engaging in the business of U-Drive-It.
(5) The term "state" means the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky.
(6) The term "interstate commerce" means commerce be
tween any place in a state and any place in another state.
(7) The term "intrastate commerce" means commerce be
tween any place in this state and any other place in this state.
(8) The term "highway" means all public roads, highways,
streets and ways in this state, whether within a municipality or
outside of a municipality.

510 Acts of the General Assembly
Section 8. Section 281.690 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is hereby amended to read as follows:
(1) No common carrier shall make any change in any rate
that has been duly established under this Chapter except after
thirty days' notice to the department, which notice shall state
plainly the changes proposed to be made and the proposed
effective date of such change. The carrier shall also give notice
of the proposed change to other interested persons in such man
ner as the department directs in its rules and regulations. All
proposed changes shall be shown by filing new tariffs in the
form and manner prescribed by the department. The depart
ment may, in accordance with regulations prescribed therefor,
upon the showing of good cause, provide for the allowance of a
change in rates by holders of certificates authorizing only trans
portation of property upon notice less than that herein specified.
(2) Whenever any tariff setting forth a change in rates is
filed, the department may, upon its own initiative, and shall,
upon protest filed in accordance with the regulations of the de
partment, enter upon a hearing concerning the lawfulness of the
proposed rate. At least thirty days before the hearing the depart
ment shall mail written notice of the hearing to the applicant,
the protestant and to any other person who, in the opinion of
the department, may be interested in or affected by the proposed
change in rate. The department may suspend the operation of
the new rates for a period of not more than six months from the
proposed effective date by order, giving reasons therefor. If
after the hearing the department finds the proposed rate, or any
part thereof, to be unjust, unreasonable, or unjustly discrimin
atory, or in violation of law, the department shall determine the
just and reasonable rate to be charged and shall fix the same by
order.
(3) No contract carrier shall make any change in its con
tract filed with the department except upon the approval of the
department.
Section 9. Section 281.780 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is hereby amended to read as follows:
( 1 ) An appeal to the Franklin Circuit Court may be taken
from any final order of the department by anyone who was a
party to the proceedings before it.

CHAPTER 189
(H. B. 308)
AN ACT relating to airport zoning.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. All of the provisions, powers and duties relating
to airport zoning in KRS 183.750 to KRS 183.758, are hereby
conferred upon and delegated to the Department of Aeronautics.
Such powers and duties shall not be in derogation of those
powers and duties granted political subdivisions by KRS 183.750
to KRS 183.758.
Section 2. The powers and duties conferred upon the
department by this Act are to be exercised in relation to air
ports owned or controlled by the Commonwealth.
Section 3. Whenever the department adopts zoning regu
lations a zoning board shall be created. Such board shall be
composed of the commissioner who shall act as chairman and
two citizens of the eounty wherein the airport, or major portion
thereof, is situated. One of the citizen members shall be ap
pointed by the county judge and the other shall be appointed
by the commissioner. The citizen members shall hold no other
public office or position and shall serve without pay, but may
be reimbursed for any necessary expenses incurred in the per
formance of their duties. The commissioner may appoint an
officer or employe of the department to serve as chairman in
his place on any such board.
Approved March 13, 1954

514 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 190
(H. B. 309)
AN ACT relating to the uniform reciprocal enforcement of support for
dependents and amending Section 407.010 and repealing Sections
407.020, 407.030, 407.040, 407.050, 407.060, 407.070, 407,080, 407.090 of
Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 407.010 of Kentucky Revised Statutes is
hereby amended and re-enacted to read as follows:
407.010 Short Titles Purpose. (1) This Chapter may be
cited and referred to as the "Uniform Support of Dependents
Act."
(2) The purposes of this Act are to improve and extend by
reciprocal legislation the enforcement of duties of support and to
make uniform the laws with respect thereto.
Section 2. Definitions. As used in this Act unless the con
text requires otherwise:
(1) "State" includes any state, territory or possession of
the United States and the District of Columbia in which this or
a substantially similar reciprocal law has been enacted.
(2) "Initiating state" means any state in which a proceed
ing pursuant to this or a substantially similar reciprocal law is
commenced.
(3) "Responding state" means any state in which any
proceeding pursuant to the proceeding in the initiating state is or
may be commenced.
( 4 ) "Court" means the circuit or county court of this state
and when the context requires, means the court of any other state
as defined in a substantially similar reciprocal law.
(5) "Law" includes both common and statute law.
(6) "Duty of support" includes any duty of support im
posed or imposable by law, or by any court order, decree or
judgment, whether interlocutory or final, whether incidental to
a proceeding for divorce, legal separation, separate maintenance
or otherwise.
( 7 ) "Obligor" means any person owing a duty of support.
(8) "Obligee" means any person to whom a duty of sup
port is owed.

ury that have been allowed by the court, and transmit the

of each warrant issued for the payment of such claims."

AN ACT relating to the disposition of persons convicted of crime in

wealth of Kentucky:

house or county jail and kept in custody until bond is given or
the time for which it was required has expired, and until the fine
is paid or satisfied by labor.

arrested under a capias pro fine for a violation of an ordinance
of a city of the first class, shall, unless discharged by authority
of law or by payment of such fine, be committed to the work
house or county jail and shall be there detained, until the fine
against him is paid or is satisfied by his earnings by compulsory

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the disposition of persons convicted of crime in police courts in cities of the first and fourth classes.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 26.440 and 26.450. It allows police courts in cities of the first and fourth classes to require offenders of drunkenness or disorderly conduct to post bond to keep the peace. If the offender fails to provide bond or pay a fine, they are committed to a workhouse, station house, or county jail until the fine is paid or satisfied by labor. It also states that persons convicted of a misdemeanor in a city of the first class police court, or arrested under a capias pro fine for an ordinance violation, will be committed to the workhouse or county jail until the fine is paid or satisfied by their earnings through compulsory labor, unless discharged by law or payment.

It outlines procedures for fines, bonds, and commitment to workhouses or jails, including the satisfaction of fines through labor. While these types of laws were often enforced disproportionately against Black individuals during the Jim Crow era, this specific statute does not contain any explicit or implicit racial language. It applies generally to all persons convicted of these offenses in the specified cities. Therefore, it is classified as 'no' for Jim Crow, with a high confidence. CHAPTER 200
(H. B. 326)
AN ACT relating to investments in real property by insurers.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 304.437 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) It is hereby declared to be the policy of the Common
wealth of Kentucky that the investment of funds in real and
personal property as now or hereafter provided by KRS Chap
ter 304 be recognized as a normal, proper, necessary, and
integral part of the legitimate business of insurance companies,
including corporate insurers, organized in Kentucky or authorized
to transact insurance in Kentucky.
(2) An insurer shall own or have its funds invested in
ownership and repair, alteration, furnishing, or improvement of
the following real property only:
(a) The building in which is located its home office and
the land upon which it is situated, together with such other real
property as is requisite for its accommodation in the convenient
transaction of its business, all subject to the provisions of
KRS 304.438;
(b) Real property acquired in satisfaction of or on account
of loans, mortgages, liens, judgments, or other debts previously
owing to the insurer in the course of its business;
(c) Real property acquired by gift or devise;
(d) Unencumbered real property acquired in exchange

CHAPTER 201
(H. B. 327)
AN ACT relating to fire prevention and protection.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context otherwise
requires :
( 1 ) "Commissioner" means the Commissioner of Insurance;
(2) "Department" means the Department of Insurance;
( 3 ) "Fire loss" means loss of or damage to property, loss of
life or personal injury, by fire, lightning or explosion;
( 4 ) "Owner" means any person who owns, occupies or has
charge of any property;
(5) "Property" means property of all types, both real and
personal, movable and immovable;
(6) "Rule" or "regulation" means a general order of the
commissioner, designed for the prevention of fire loss, which
affects or may affect property rights of a designated class of own
ers or for the prevention of fire loss by certain indicated hazards;
( 7 ) "Order" or "special order" means an order of the com
missioner, designed for the prevention of fire loss, which affects
or may affect the property rights of a particular owner or desig
nated property.
Section 2. Any power, duty or function vested in the com
missioner, by any provision of this Act may be exercised, dis
charged, or performed by any deputy or assistant of the depart
ment acting in the commissioner's name and by his delegated
authority.
Section 3. ( 1 ) The commissioner shall enforce or aid in
the enforcement of all laws, regulations and ordinances of the
state and its political subdivisions relating to fire loss as herein
defined :
( a ) The prevention of reduction of loss by fire or by other
hazard or risk insured by property or casualty insurance com
panies doing business in this state, except as to disability insur
ance and workmen's compensation, and shall enforce any other
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 558

ance to state and local governmental agencies in relation to fire

(f) Conduct fire prevention and educational campaigns;
(g) Conduct examinations into the cause, origin, or cir
cumstances of fire losses;
(h) Hold such hearings as he may deem necessary or de
sirable as to any matter within the scope of this Act.

Section 4. The commissioner shall appoint a State Fire Mar
shal and such deputy fire marshals, assistants and employes as are

enforce and administer the provisions of this Act and rules and
regulations issued hereunder. The chief of each city fire de
partment and the sheriff of each county, shall be deemed depu

Section 5. The deputy fire marshal for any city or county
in which fire loss occurs, shall immediately investigate the origin
and circumstances of the fire and determine the cause of the fire
so far as practicable. If it appears to the deputy that the fire is
of suspicious origin he shall immediately notify the department.
Section 6. Every insurer authorized to transact business in
this state shall report to the department, such loss or damage by
fire, lightning, or explosion occurring to property insured by
such insurer in this state, as may be, and in the manner pre
scribed by the commissioner. Provided, however, the commis
sioner may waive the reporting of such losses which are deemed
unimportant because of the small amount involved to the end
that a saving in time and expense will result.
Section 7. From the reports made to him, the commissioner
shall keep a record of fire losses occurring in this state and of
facts concerning them. He shall make such compilations, investi
gations and statistical summaries as he deems proper, all of
which shall be kept as permanent records in his office. All such
records shall be public, except that the commissioner may, in his
discretion, withhold from the public statements and testimony
taken in an investigation or examination, correspondence re
lating to an investigation or examination, confidential reports of
private persons and agents, and reports of investigations of fire
losses.

ministration and enforcement of all laws, ordinances, rules and

Section 11. (1) The commissioner shall promulgate rea
sonable rules and regulations based upon good engineering prac
tice and principles as embodied in recognized standards of fire
prevention and protection, providing for a reasonable degree of
safety for human life against the exigencies of fire and panic, and
ensuring as far as is practicable against fire loss. Such rules and
regulations shall be known as the Standards of Safety.
( 2 ) In making such rules and regulations the commissioner
shall establish minimum fire prevention and protection require
ments, including but not limited to requirements for design, con
struction, installation, operation, storage, handling, maintenance
or use of the following: structural requirements for the various
types of construction; building restrictions within congested dis
tricts; exit facilities from structures; fire alarm systems and fire
extinguishing systems; fire emergency drills; requirements for
buildings of public assembly; flue and chimney construction;
heating devices; electrical wiring and equipment; air condition
ing, ventilating and other duct systems; refrigeration systems;
flammable liquids, oil and gas wells; garages, repair and service
shops; application of flammable finishes, explosives; acetylene,
liquefied petroleum gas and similar products; calcium carbide
and acetylene generators; dry cleaning and dyeing plants; flam
mable motion picture film; combustible fibres; airports and air
port buildings; hazardous chemicals; rubbish; open flame de
vices; parking of vehicles; dust explosions; lightning protection;
and other special fire hazards.
Section 12. The commissioner shall conduct a hearing prior
to the issuance of rules and regulations promulgated pursuant to
Section 11 of this Act. At such hearing interested parties shall be
given an opportunity to be heard in person or by counsel. At
least ten days in advance of the hearing the commissioner shall
cause a notice of such hearing to be published in two or more
daily newspapers of general circulation published and circulated
in this state. No defect or inaccuracy in the notice or in its pub
lication shall invalidate any such rules or regulations.
Section 13. This Act shall not deprive the authorities of any
county, city or other political subdivision, of any power or juris

classification.summary: This statute outlines the powers and duties of a commissioner related to fire prevention and safety. It details the commissioner's role in promulgating rules and regulations for fire safety, including requirements for building design, construction, exit facilities, fire alarm systems, and the handling of hazardous materials. The statute also specifies the process for conducting hearings and publishing notices for these rules and regulations, and clarifies that it does not supersede the powers of local authorities.

There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, this statute is not a Jim Crow law. The document type is 'session_laws' as it is presented as a chapter from a legislative session's acts.

Section 18. ( 1 ) The chief of the fire department of a city,
or any officer or member of his department designated by him for
that purpose, is authorized to inspect all property for the pur
pose of ascertaining and causing to be corrected any conditions
likely to cause fire loss, or determining the cause or origin of any
fire loss, or discovering any violation of a law or ordinance relat
ing to fire prevention and protection. This authority shall apply
to the interior of occupied, private dwellings only when a fire
loss has occurred therein or when the officer has reason to be
lieve that unsafe conditions exist in the building. Inspections of
property in the territory served by the fire department shall be
made as often as practicable or as often as the city legislative
body may direct.
( 2 ) A written report of each inspection shall be made and
kept on file in the office of the chief of the fire department. Re
ports of investigations of fire losses conducted by a fire depart
ment may, in the discretion of the chief of the fire department, be
withheld from the public.
Section 19. (1) Whenever the chief of the fire depart
ment of a city, or any officer or member of his department desig
nated by him for that purpose, finds any property which, for
want of repairs, lack of sufficient fire escapes, age, dilapidated
condition or any other cause, is especially liable to fire loss, or
whenever an officer finds in any property combustible or explo
sive matter or inflammable materials likelv to result in fire loss,
he shall order it to be remedied. The order shall forthwith be
conformed to by the owner of the property.
(2) The owner may appeal to the commissioner within
ten days following receipt of the order. The commissioner shall,
within twenty days, review the order and file his decision. The
order shall remain in full force until it is revoked or modified
by the commissioner.

Section 21. (1) No owner shall fail to furnish and use

Section 22. Any person who violates any provision of this
Act or any provision of a lawful order, rule or regulation made
under the provisions of this Act, or who induces another to vio
late any provisions of this Act or of any lawful order, rule or
regulation made thereunder, shall be fined not less than ten
dollars nor more than five hundred dollars, or confined in the
county jail for not more than sixty days, or both. Each day such
violations exist shall, in the discretion of the courts, be considered
as a separate offense.
Section 23. Sections 227.010 to 227.190 and 227.990 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes are hereby repealed and a new Chap
ter of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is hereby created to be
numbered Chapter 227 which shall contain this Act.
Approved March 22, 1954

Section 304.426 (1) allows insurers to invest in obligations of the International Bank for Reconstruction and Development. Section 304.426 (2) permits investment in management investment companies registered under the Federal Investment Company Act of 1940, with limitations on aggregate investment. Section 304.450 addresses the disposal of lawfully acquired personal property or securities by an insurer that do not meet investment standards, requiring disposal within four years unless the security becomes eligible or is acquired through bulk reinsurance, merger, or consolidation.

expiration of the initial thirty-day period. At the expiration of
any such period as so extended, and in the absence of such prior
affirmative approval or disapproval, any such form shall be
deemed approved. Approval of any such form by the commis
sioner shall constitute a waiver of any unexpired portion of such
initial thirty-day waiting period.
(3) The commissioner's order disapproving any such form
shall state the grounds therefor. The commissioner may, for
cause after a hearing, withdraw a previous approval.
(4) No such form shall knowingly be so issued or delivered
as to which the commissioner's approval does not then exist.
(5) The commissioner may, by order, exempt from the re
quirements of this Section for so long as he deems proper, any
insurance document or form or type thereof as specified in such
order, to which in his opinion this Section may not practicably
be applied, or the filing and approval of which are, in his opinion,
not desirable or necessary for the protection of the public.

utes is amended to read as follows:
Except as provided in Section 15 of this Act, insurers that
take fire or storm risks on real property in this state shall, in case

Chapter 203 553
fixed on the face of the policy, except that the estimated value
of the property insured may be diminished to the extent of any
depreciation in the value of the property occurring between the

erty insured, the liability of the insurer shall not exceed the

Section 15. There is hereby created a new section of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes to read as follows:
Insurers of property against fire or storm may contract with
the insured that the insured shall, during the life of the policy,
maintain insurance upon the property so insured to the extent
of an agreed proportion of the actual cash value of the property
at the time of loss, and that if the insured fails to do so, the in
sured shall be a co-insurer to the extent that the insurance then
in force is less than the amount of the agreed proportion, and
shall bear his part of any loss, as co-insurer, to that extent. The
acceptance of a policy containing such a provision shall be at the
option of the insured, and a reduced premium rate shall apply
to the policy. No such provision shall be valid unless the form
setting up the terms of the provision has been approved by the
Commissioner of Insurance.
Section 16. There is hereby created a new section of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes to read as follows:
(1) On any contract of title insurance hereafter issued by
any domestic insurer, there shall be reserved initially a sum
equal to ten percent of the risk premium charged thereon. At
the end of each year, for a period of twenty years thereafter,
there shall be a reduction in the sum so reserved in the amount
of one-twentieth of such sum. Said sums herein required to be
reserved for unearned premiums on contracts of title insurance,
shall at all times and for all purposes be considered as, and shall
constitute, unearned portions of such risk premiums. In calcu
lating reserves, all contracts of title insurance shall be assumed
to be dated in the middle of the year in which they are issued.
(2) The insurer shall invest funds constituting the unearned
premium reserves created by this Section, only in such invest
ments as are eligible.

Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
1. Section 412.070 of the 1953 edition of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes is repealed, and in lieu thereof is enacted the
following Section, to read and be numbered as follows:
412.070. In actions for the settlement of estates, or for
the recovery of money or property held in joint tenancy, copar
cenary, or as tenants in common, or for the recovery of money
or property which has been illegally or improperly collected,
withheld or converted, if one or more of the legatees, devisees,
distributees or parties in interest has prosecuted for the benefit
of others interested with him, and has been to trouble and
expense in that connection, the court shall allow him his neces
sary expenses, and his attorney reasonable compensation for his
services, in addition to the costs. This allowance shall be paid
out of the funds recovered before distribution. The persons in
terested shall be given notice of the application for the allow
ance, provided, however, that if the court before whom the ac
tion is pending should determine that it is impracticable and
too expensive to notify all of the parties individually, then by
order of said court, personal notice may be dispensed with and
in lieu thereof, notice of the application shall be given by an
advertisement in a newspaper of general circulation in the county
in which the action is pending. Two such newspaper advertise
