CHAPTER 205
(H. B. 339)
AN ACT relating to police departments in second-class cities taking
charge of lost or stolen property coming into the possession of such
police departments and providing for the disposition of unclaimed
property by police departments in second-class cities.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
(1) The police department in cities of the second class,
shall take charge of property, within their jurisdiction, alleged
to be or suspected of being the proceeds of crime, property
taken from the person of a prisoner, lost or abandoned property
taken into the custody of any member of the police force or
criminal court, and property taken from persons supposed to
be insane, intoxicated or otherwise incapable of taking care of
themselves. The officer or court having custody of such prop
erty shall as soon as practicable deliver it into the custody of
the police department.
(2) All such property shall be particularly described and
registered by the police department in a book kept for that pur
pose, containing the name of the owner, if ascertained, the
place where found, the name of the person from whom taken,
with the general circumstances, the date of its receipt, the
name of the officer recovering the property, the names of all
claimants thereto, and any final disposition of the property.
The police department shall advertise the property for the in
formation of the public as to the amount and disposition of
the property.
(3) If any property in the custody of the police de
partment is desired as evidence in any criminal court, such

Section 24. There is hereby created a new Section of the

There may be a provision as follows:
"CANCELLATION: The insurer may cancel this policy at
any time by written notice delivered to the insured, or mailed
to his last address as shown by the records of the insurer, stating
when, not less than five days thereafter, such cancellation shall
be effective; and after the policy has been continued beyond its
original term the insured may cancel this policy at any time by
written notice delivered or mailed to the insurer, effective upon
receipt or on such later date as may be specified in such notice.
In the event of cancellation, the insurer will return promptly
the unearned portion of any premium paid. If the insured
cancels, the earned premium shall be computed by the use of the
short-rate table last filed with the state official having super
vision of insurance in the state where the insured resided when
the policy was issued. If the insurer cancels, the earned premium
shall be computed pro rata. Cancellation shall be without
prejudice to any claim originating prior to the effective date of
cancellation."

quirements of such statutes."

(3) "Independent trailer coach" or "independent mobile
home" means a trailer coach with self-contained toilet and bath
or shower facilities;
(4) "Trailer coach park" or "park" means an area of land
upon which two or more occupied trailer coaches are harbored,
either free of charge or for revenue purposes, and shall include
any building, structure, tent, vehicle, or enclosure used or in
tended for use as a part of the equipment of such trailer coach
park;
(5) "Trailer coach space" or "trailer coach site" means
a plot of ground within a trailer coach park designated for the
accomodation of one trailer coach;
(6) "Department" means the State Department of Health;
(7) "Board" means the State Board of Health.
Section 2. No one shall operate a trailer coach park with
out having first obtained a permit as provided for in this Act.
An application for a permit to conduct a trailer coach park shall
be made to the department upon forms provided by it and shall
contain such information as the department reasonably requires,
which may include affirmative evidence of ability to comply with
such reasonable standards, rules and regulations as may be pre
scribed. Each application shall be accompanied by a permit fee
of twenty-five dollars.
Section 3. ( 1 ) Upon receipt of an application for a permit
to operate, accompanied by the permit fee, the department shall
issue a permit, if the trailer coach park meets the standards and
requirements of this Act and the rules and regulations of the
board.
(2) Any one operating a trailer coach park which is in
operation on July 1, 1954, shall be issued a permit to operate,
provided that, by January 1, 1955, the trailer coach park shall
comply with all requirements of sanitation established under
this Act including the rules and regulations adopted therefor.
However, failure to comply with the required standards within
the six months period, shall result in the institution of an action
for revocation or suspension of the permit.
(3) Each permit to operate, unless sooner suspended or
revoked, shall expire on June 30 following its issuance, and be
renewable annually, upon application for a fee of ten dollars.

operate or to be constructed under the provisions of this Act
shall:
(1) Comply with all local zoning regulations;

at all times;

not endanger any water supply;

quality, approved by the department, as well as an adequate
supply of hot water;
(7) Provide adequate toilet, lavatory and bathing facilities
for occupants of dependent trailer coaches;
(8) Maintain buildings at a temperature of at least sixty-

CHAPTER 208
(H. B. 343)
AN ACT relating to flammable liquids.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. As used in this Act, unless the context re
quires otherwise:
(1) "Flash point" means the minimum temperature, in
degrees Fahrenheit, at which a flammable liquid will give off
flammable vapors as determined by test procedure and ap
paratus specified in the "Standard Method of Test for Flash
Point by Means of the Tag Closed Tester" (A.S.T.M. D 56-52)
published by the American Society for Testing Materials;
(2) "Class A liquid" means any flammable liquid which
has a flash point of one hundred degrees Fahrenheit, or less;
(3) "Class B liquid" means any flammable liquid which
has a flash point above one hundred degrees Fahrenheit.
Section 2. No person shall have for sale, sell, give or de
liver to another any product known, advertised, sold or offered
for sale as kerosene, coal oil, fuel oil, stove oil, range oil, fur
nace oil, distillate or diesel fuel, or by a trade name commonly
identified with any such product, unless the product consti
tutes a Class B liquid.
Section 3. No dealer shall sell or deliver kerosene, coal
oil, fuel oil, stove oil, range oil, furnace oil or other Class B
liquid except from a container having the name of the liquid
sold plainly marked or labeled on the container, or on the pump
or delivery equipment serving such container.
Section 4. No compartment of a tank vehicle which has
contained a Class A liquid on the prior loading shall be loaded
with Class B liquid until such compartment and all piping and
accessory delivery equipment connected thereto have been
drained of all Class A liquid and then flushed with a quantity
of Class B liquid sufficient to clear them of any remaining
Class A liquid; or cleared of Class A liquid by some equally
effective method.
Section 5. In the event that a tank vehicle is equipped
with a pump, meter, and hose, or any combination thereof,

classification.summary: This act defines "flash point" and "Class A" and "Class B" liquids based on their flash points. It prohibits the sale, gift, or delivery of certain fuel products (kerosene, coal oil, fuel oil, etc.) unless they are Class B liquids. It also mandates that Class B liquids be sold or delivered from containers clearly labeled with the product name, and that tank vehicles be properly flushed of Class A liquids before loading Class B liquids.

Section 6. In the event that compartments of a tank ve
hicle are connected to a common outlet or manifold, no com
partment so connected shall contain Class A liquids while any
other compartment so connected contains Class B liquids.
Section 7. No manifold, pump, meter, or hose shall be

pump, meter, or hose is required for the delivery of Class A
and Class B liquids on the same trip, then separate delivery
equipment shall be provided. Such equipment shall be tagged
or painted to clearly indicate the class of liquid for which it
is used.
Section 8. (1) Any person violating the provisions of
this Act shall be fined not less than one hundred dollars or more
than five hundred dollars, or imprisoned in the county jail for
not more than sixty days.
(2) Any firm or corporation violating the provisions of
this Act shall be fined not less than one hundred dollars or more
than five hundred dollars.
Approved March 22, 1954

It focuses on the administrative and operational aspects of public health management.

The powers granted are general and apply to the entire population for the purpose of public health. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 213
(H. B. 350)
AN ACT relating to the establishment of public pounds for animals in
counties containing a city of the first class.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Each city-county board of health created by
KRS 212.350 shall establish, maintain and operate a public
pound for animals in which, except as otherwise provided by
law, shall be impounded all stray, vicious or diseased animals
taken up or collected in such county, whether in incorporated
or unincorporated areas, by any public officer or authority or by
any other person. Each such board shall, throughout the county,
including all municipalities therein, enforce all statutes, ordi
nances of cities, orders or resolutions of the fiscal court of
the county and regulations of such board or any other govern
mental body or agency authorized to adopt such regulations,
relating to the taking up, collection and impounding of strav,
vicious or diseased animals. Each such board may promulgate
and enforce reasonable and necessary rules and regulations
providing for the collection and impounding of such animals
and governing the use and operation of such public pounds.
Section 2. Subsection (1) of Section 83.170 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
(1) The department of public safety shall be under the
supervision and direction of a director of safety, and shall have
exclusive control, under the city ordinances, of all matters
relating to the division of the police department and the di
vision of the fire department, the inspector of weights and
measures, and such other matters as are provided by statute
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 610

CHAPTER 214
(H. B. 365)
AN ACT relating to public school education: to provide for the estab
lishment, operation, and financing of a foundation program for the
public schools in the Commonwealth of Kentucky and for the
apportionment, distribution, and use of the public school foundation
program fund.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. In this Act, it is the intention of the General
Assembly to assure substantially equal public school educational
opportunities, through a foundation program, for those in at
tendance in the public schools of the Commonwealth, but not
to limit nor to prevent any school district from providing edu
cational services and facilities beyond those assured by the
foundation program; and to provide, as additional state funds
are made available for the public schools, for the use of such
funds for the further equalization of educational opportunities.
This Act shall be interpreted as a measure to provide for an
efficient system of public schools throughout the Common
wealth, as prescribed by Section 183 of the Constitution of
Kentucky, and for the manner of distribution of the public
school fund among the districts and its use for public school
purposes, as prescribed by Section 186 of the Constitution.
Section 2. As used in this Act, unless the context other
wise requires:
(1) "Average daily attendance" means the aggregate davs
attended by pupils in a public school, divided by the actual
number of days the school is in session for the year;

(5) "District" means any school district as defined by law;
(6) "Elementary school" means a school consisting of
grades one through eight, or any appropriate combination of
grades within this range, as determined by the plan of organ
ization for schools authorized by the district board;
(7) "Foundation program" means the level of educational
services and facilities, as defined in this Act, which is to be
provided in each district from the required local tax effort and
the public school foundation program fund;
(8) "Isolated school" means an elementary or secondary
school which had fewer than one hundred pupils in average
daily attendance during the previous school year and which
meets such standards for isolation as shall be prescribed by the
regulations of the State Board of Education, based on factors
to include distance by the nearest passable road from another
appropriate school center which is able to receive the pupils,
and the time for transportation necessary to attend another
school; provided, however, that the Superintendent of Public
Instruction, under regulations of the State Board of Education,
may annually classify a school as "temporarily isolated" for a
period not to exceed five years if satisfactory facilities for the
pupils cannot be provided at another appropriate school center;
(9) "Public school foundation program fund" means the
fund created by Section 3 of this Act for use in financing edu
cation in public elementary and secondary schools;
(10) "Regulations of the State Board of Education" means
those regulations which the State Board of Education may adopt
upon the recommendation and with the advice of the Super
intendent of Public Instruction. The Superintendent of Public
Instruction shall recommend for adoption of the State Board of
Education such rules and regulations as he deems necessary for
carrying out the purposes of this Act;

(2) In determining the required local tax effort for dis
tricts which participate in the equalization account, including
the capital outlay allotment, the Superintendent of Public In
struction shall divide the aggregate assessed value of all property
in the Commonwealth subject to taxation for school purposes
by one hundred and multiply the quotient thereof by one dollar
and ten cents. The product thereby obtained shall be multiplied
by each district's percentage of the total equalized value of all
property in the Commonwealth subject to taxation for school
purposes. In determining the required local tax effort for
districts which do not participate in the capital outlay allotment
of the equalization account, the multiplier shall be one dollar.
(3) Any district's levy shall be at such rate as is necessary to
provide the required local tax effort.
(4) A portion of funds received by any district from the
Federal government in lieu of taxation of property shall be
added to the district's required local tax effort. The portion
of such Federal funds added to the district's required local tax
effort shall be the percentage the required local tax effort is
of the total levy for school current expense purposes made in
the district during that year; provided, however, that any part
of Federal funds received by the district that is charged against
the district in determining its allotment under Public Law 874 or
similar laws shall not be added to the districts required local tax
effort, and, provided further, that any funds received by any
districts under KRS 96.895 shall not be added to the districts'

CHAPTER 216
(H. B. 373)
AN ACT relating to property taxation.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 132.590 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows:
(1) In the year 1954 and thereafter, the compensation of
the county tax commissioner shall be computed on the basis of
ten cents on each one hundred dollars of the first two million
dollars and two cents on each one hundred dollars of the excess
over two million dollars of the annual assessment. However,
each county tax commissioner shall receive a minimum monthly
payment of two hundred dollars as part or all of his compensation.
(2) In counties in which the assessed value of property
exceeds eight million dollars, the county tax commissioner shall
be allowed by the state as compensation to his deputies and for
other authorized expenditures two cents on each one hundred
dollars of the property assessed in excess of eight million dollars
but the total sum allowed to any county tax commissioner as
deputv hire and for other authorized expenditures shall not
exceed twenty thousand dollars in any county except in a county
having a city of the first class, which shall be governed by KRS
132.630. No state funds available to any county tax commissioner
as compensation for deputies or for other authorized expenditures
shall be paid without authorization of the Department of Revenue
prior to the employment by the tax commissioner of deputies or
other assistants or the incurring of other expense.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 626

CHAPTER 217
(H. B. 379)
AN ACT relating to Federal lands within the Commonwealth.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. KRS 3.010 is amended to read as follows:
"The Commonwealth of Kentucky consents to the acquisition
by the United States of all lands and appurtenances in this state,
by condemnation, gift or purchase, which are needful to their
constitutional purposes, but said acquisition shall not be deemed
to result in a cession of jurisdiction by this Commonwealth."
Section 2. Whenever the United States, or any agency
thereof, shall request the Commonwealth to cede jurisdiction
over any area, it shall be the duty of the Governor to transmit
such request to the next session of the General Assembly for such
action as it may deem proper.

CHAPTER 219
(H. B. 383)
AN ACT relating to the practice of chiropody.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Subsection (2) of Section 311.380 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes is amended to read as follows:
"Chiropody" means the diagnosis, and the local, medical,
mechanical and surgical treatment, of ailments of the human
foot, and massage in connection therewith, except amputation of
the foot or toes, or the use of anesthetics other than local, or the
use of drugs or medicine other than local; provided, however,
that in connection with such treatment analgesics, antipyretics,
and codeine may be used orally and antibiotics or sulfanalamide
agents may be used through local application.
Section 2. Section 311.410 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
(1) There is hereby established a State Board of Chi
ropody which shall consist of five practicing chiropodists, each
appointed by the Governor. All appointments, including ap
pointments to fill vacancies, shall be made from lists of three
names for each position submitted by the Kentucky Association
of Chiropodists. Any vacancy shall be filled for the unexpired
term by the Governor, as provided in the original appointment.
(2) A person to be eligible for appointment as a member
of said board shall be at least twenty-one years of age, of good
moral character, a resident of this state, and a licensed and prac
ticing chiropodist in this state for at least five consecutive years
next preceding the date of his appointment. No member of the
board shall be a stockholder, officer or member of the faculty or
board of trustees of any school, college or institution of
chiropody.
(3) The State Board of Chiropody, established by Chapter
197 of the Acts of 1952, is hereby abolished, and all rights, func
tions, powers and duties of said board and of the State Board of
Health under said Act are hereby transferred to and vested in the
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 630

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the practice of chiropody.
classification.summary: This act amends Kentucky Revised Statutes Section 311.380 to define chiropody and its permissible treatments, and Section 311.410 to establish a State Board of Chiropody. The board will consist of five practicing chiropodists appointed by the Governor from lists submitted by the Kentucky Association of Chiropodists. Eligibility for appointment requires being at least 21 years old, of good moral character, a resident, and a licensed practicing chiropodist for at least five consecutive years. Board members cannot be affiliated with any school, college, or institution of chiropody. The act also abolishes the State Board of Chiropody established in 1952 and transfers its rights, functions, powers, and duties to the new board.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the regulation and practice of chiropody in Kentucky. It defines the scope of practice, establishes a state board for chiropody, and outlines appointment procedures and eligibility criteria for board members. There is no mention of race, nor are there any provisions that implicitly or explicitly discriminate based on race. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

CHAPTER 221
(H. B. 394)
AN ACT relating to the terms of court in the Marshall County Circuit
Court.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
In addition to the terms of court prescribed by KRS 23.050
(2) and an Act of the 1954 General Assembly entitled "AN ACT
relating to judicial districts; creating an additional district and
making changes in the apportionment of counties to the Second,
Third and Fourth Districts in order to comply with the mean
ing and intent of the Constitution with respect to the division
of the state into judicial districts; providing for the continuation
in office of the present circuit judges and Commonwealth's
attorneys and creating the offices of circuit judge and Com
monwealth's attorney for the new district established by this
Act; providing for the holding of court in each district affected
by this Act; and amending Sections 23.040 and 23.050 of the
Kentucky Revised Statutes", effective June 28, 1954, there
shall be a regular term of circuit court in Marshall county, for
the year 1954 only, commencing on the first Monday in July,
1954, and continuing, if the business requires it, for eighteen
juridical days.
Approved March 15, 1954

General Assembly to provide for the effective control of the

Chapter 223 615
Section 2. As used in this Act:
(1) "Health officer" means the health officer or health
administrator of any local health department;
(2) "Communicable tuberculosis" means tuberculosis
which is capable of being transmitted from one human being
to another.
Section 3. Any person who has communicable tubercu
losis shall:
(a) Avoid close contact with babies and small children;
(b) Cover his nose and mouth with disposable tissue
paper while coughing or sneezing and destroy the tissue paper
by burning it or by causing it to be burned;
(c) Collect all sputum in a sputum container or in some
other manner approved by the State Board of Health and burn
said sputum or cause it to be burned;
(d) Take such other reasonable precautions for the pre
vention of the spread of communicable tuberculosis as may be
required by the rules and regulations of the State Board of
Health adopted for this purpose.
Section 4. Whenever a health officer has reasonable
grounds to believe that a person has communicable tuberculosis
and that such person has failed to comply with the provisions
of Section 3 of this Act, he may make an affidavit before any
court or judge thereof reciting such facts and circumstances
and the court or judge thereof shall thereafter direct that the
person suspected of being afflicted with communicable tuber
culosis submit to an examination either by a licensed practicing
physician of his own choice at his own expense or by a physician
appointed by the health officer at the expense of the county.
The examination shall include both physical and laboratory ex
aminations and shall be conducted according to such rules and
regulations as may be promulgated by the State Board of Health
and shall be reported upon forms furnished by said board.
Section 5. (1) Any person who violates the provisions
of Section 3 of this Act shall be guilty of a misdemeanor and
shall be punished by a fine not exceeding five hundred dollars
or by imprisonment not exceeding six months, or both; provided,
however, that

CHAPTER 227
(H. B. 406)
AN ACT pertaining to the regulation of mines and minerals, and amend
ing Sections 352.220, 352.240 and 352.990 of the Kentucky Revised
Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 352.220 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is hereby amended to read as follows:
(1) The following shall apply to underground installa
tions:
(a) On all haulage roads, landings and partings, where men
are required to regularly work or pass under bare power wires
placed less than six and one half feet above the top of the rail,
suitable protection shall be provided. This protection shall con
sist of channeling the roof, placing boards along the wires and
extending below them, or the use of some other approved device
that affords protection.
(b) All machine feed wires shall be placed on glass or
porcelain insulators which shall be so placed as to prevent the
wires coming in contact with the coal.

It also includes a provision that the department may authorize different methods if they provide equal or greater safety, and may prescribe other regulations for mine operations.

Chapter 227 627
(8) Boreholes in coal shall not be drilled beyond the back
of the cut, nor into the solid rib, roof or floor.
(9) Boreholes shall be cleaned and checked to see that they
are placed properly and are of correct depth, in relation to the
cut, before being charged.
(10) To prevent blowthroughs, all portions of the bore
holes, where the height of the coal permits, shall have a burden
in all directions of at least eighteen inches before being fired.
(11) Boreholes shall be stemmed with at least twenty-four
inches of incombustible material, or at least two-thirds of the
length of the hole shall be stemmed.
(12) Examinations for gas shall be made immediately be
fore and after firing each shot where on-shift shooting is done
in gaseous mines.
(13) When blasting coal, charges exceeding one and one-
half pounds, but not exceeding three pounds, shall be used only
if boreholes are six feet or more in depth, and explosives are
charged in a continuous train, with no cartridges deliberately
deformed or crushed, with all cartridges in contact with each
other, and with the hole stemmed solidly for a depth of at least
twenty-four inches.
(14) Shots shall be fired by qualified shot firers.
(15) In mines where shooting is done on shift, all shots
or series of shots shall be fired immediately after charging.
(16) Only wooden tamping bars shall be used when charg
ing holes.
•
(17) Leg wires of electric detonators shall be kept shunted
or the ends twisted together until ready to connect to the firing
cable.
(18) Shots shall not be fired from the power or signal
circuit while any men are in the mine.
(19) Roof and faces of working places shall be tested be
fore and after blasting on shift.
(20) Ample warning shall be given before shots are fired,
and care shall be taken to ascertain that all persons are in the
clear. Men shall be removed from adjoining working places
when there is danger of a shot blowing through.
(21) Mixed charges shall not be charged or fired in any
borehole.

(22) Adobe (mudcap) or other open, unconfined shots shall
not be fired in any mine.
(23) Blasting cables shall be well insulated and as long
as may be necessary to permit the shot firer to get in a safe
place around the corner, short-circuited at the battery end
until ready to attach to the blasting unit, staggered as to length
or kept well separated when attached to the detonator leg wires,
and kept clear of power wires and all other possible sources of
active or stray currents.
(24) Where misfires occur with electric detonators, a wait
ing period of at least five minutes shall elapse before anyone
returns to the shot. After such failure, the blasting cable shall
be disconnected from the source of power and the battery end
short circuited before electric connections are examined. If
fuse is used a waiting period of twelve hours shall elapse before
returning to the face. Explosives shall be removed by firing a
separate charge at least two feet away from, and parallel to,
the misfired charge or by washing the stemming and the charge
from the borehole with water, or by inserting and firing a new
primer after the stemming has been washed out. A very careful
search of the working place, and, if necessary, of the coal after
it reaches the tipple shall be made after blasting a misfired hole,
to recover any undetonated explosive. The handling of a mis
fired shot shall be under the direct supervision of the foreman
or a competent person designated by him.
(25) The provisions governing the handling, storage and
transportation of explosives shall apply to the heater elements
of Cardox blasting devices prior to their installation in the shells.
Charged Cardox shells shall be transported underground in insu
lated cars or in insulated boxes placed in ordinary mine cars
except where transported on rubber belts and shall be stored on
wooden racks, built for that purpose, in a crosscut or an idle
room neck, at least ten feet from power lines and haulage tracks.

equally safe place.
(c) Blasting cables shall be as long as may be necessary

AN ACT changing the classification of the city of La Center, in Ballard
county.
WHEREAS, satisfactory information has been presented to the Gen
eral Assembly that the population of the city of La Center, in Ballard
county, is such as' to justify its being classified as a city of the fifth
class,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
The city of La Center, in Ballard county, is transferred
from the sixth to the fifth class of cities.
Approved March 22, 1954
CHAPTER 229
(H. B. 422)
AN ACT relating to the grazing of livestock on certain public grounds
and providing a penalty therefor.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 259.200 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is amended to read as follows:
No person shall permit any cattle to run or trespass upon
any state or national parks, encampment grounds, scout camps,
grounds dedicated to religious, educational or recreational pur
poses or floodwalls erected at public expense.
Section 2. Any person who violates KRS 259.200 shall be
fined not less than ten dollars nor more than one hundred dollars,
and each head of cattle trespassing shall constitute a separate
offense.
Approved March 24, 1954

CHAPTER 232
(H. B. 430)
AN ACT relating to parental permission for minors under eighteen
years of age to play pool, and amending Section 436.320 of the Ken
tucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 436.320 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is
amended to read as follows:
No person owning or controlling a billiard or pool table
shall permit, for compensation or reward, any minor under
eighteen years of age to play any game on the table, unless
such minor shall have first displayed an identification card
containing his name, age, photograph, and the signature of his
parents or guardian. The minor shall keep such identification
card on his person, and it shall be subject to inspection at any
time by any peace officer. The person owning or controlling
such billiard or pool table shall keep and maintain a registration
book in which each minor shall sign. The person owning or
controlling such billiard or pool table shall supply a blank
identification card to each parent or guardian who makes request
for same. Any person who violates this Act shall be fined not
less than ten nor more than one hundred dollars for each offense.
Became law without Governor's signature March 31, 1954

CHAPTER 233
(H. B. 436)
AN ACT relating to regulations of the sale and distribution of com
mercial feeds.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. This Act shall be administered by the Director
of the Kentucky Agricultural Experiment Station of the state of
Kentucky, hereinafter referred to as the "director".
Section 2. As used in this Act:
(1) "Person" includes individual, partnership, corporation,
firm, association, and agent;
(2) "Distribute" means to offer for sale, sell, barter, or
otherwise supply commercial feeds;
(3) "Sell" or "sale" includes exchange;
(4) "Commercial feed" means all materials which are dis
tributed for use as feed for animals except unmixed or unpro
cessed whole seeds; unground hay; and whole or ground straw,
stover, silage, cobs, and hulls when not mixed with other ma
terials;
(5) "Brand" means the term, design, or trademark and
other specific designation under which an individual commercial
feed is distributed in this Commonwealth;
(6) "Label" means a display of written, printed, or graphic
matter upon the container in which a commercial feed is dis
tributed;
(7) "Ton" means a net weight of two thousand pounds,
avoirdupois;
(8) "Percent" or "percentage" means percentage by weight;
(9) "Official sample" means any sample of commercial
feed taken bv the director or his agent.
Section 3. (1) Each brand of commercial feed shall be
registered before being offered for sale, sold, or otherwise dis
tributed in this Commonwealth. The application for registration
shall be submitted to the director on forms furnished by the
director, and if the director so requests shall also be accom
panied bv a proposed label or other printed matter describing
the product. All registrations are considered permanent unless
new registrations are called for by the director or modified by

CHAPTER 234
(H. B. 458)
AN ACT relating to the compensation of judges and commissioners of
the Court of Appeals.
WHEREAS, it is required by Section 112 of the Constitution of
Kentucky that the judges of the Court of Appeals be paid an adequate
compensation for their services; and
WHEREAS, this provision of the Constitution means that the com
pensation of the judges shall be equal, and it was so held in the case of
Stone y. Pryor, 45 S.W. 1053, and
WHEREAS, the General Assembly previously has determined that
$12,000 is an adequate compensation for the services of judges of the
Court of Appeals, but existing legislation provides for the payment of
such compensation only upon the commencement of new terms; with the
result that one judge of the Court of Appeals is now receiving the com
pensation so determined to be adequate, and commencing January 1,
1955, another judge would commence to receive such compensation, but
the other judges with longer experience on the court would continue
for a period of years to receive an unequal and inadeqaute compensa
tion,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The compensation of judges of the Court of
Appeals, payable out of the State Treasury, shall be $12,000
per annum for each judge. The compensation of commissioners

CHAPTER 235
(H. B. 461)
AN ACT relating to traffic regulation and amending Section 189.450 of
the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 189.450 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes is hereby
amended to read as follows:
"(1) No person shall stop a vehicle, leave it standing or
cause it to stop or to be left standing upon the main traveled
portion of a highway; provided, however, that this Section shall
not be construed to prevent parking off the main traveled por
tion of a highway or street in a city or suburban area where
such parking is otherwise permitted. This subsection shall not
apply to:
(a) A vehicle that has been disabled while on the main
traveled portion of such a highway in such a manner and to
such extent that it is impossible to avoid the occupation of the
main traveled portion or impracticable to remove it from the
highway until repairs have been made or sufficient help ob
tained for its removal and shall not apply to wreckers at the
scene of accidents or emergency vehicles.
(b) Motor vehicles when required to stop in obedience
to the provisions of any Section of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes or any traffic ordinance, regulation or sign or the command
of any peace officer.
(c) Vehicles operating as common carriers of passengers
for hire and school buses while taking passengers on such ve
hicle or discharging passengers therefrom, provided that such
vehicle shall not stop for such purposes at any point within 150
feet from the brow of a hill or within 150 from a curve or when
a view of any vehicle approaching from either direction is ob
structed for a distance of 150 feet.

CHAPTER 238
(H. B. 465)
AN ACT relating to courts.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. KRS 21.220 is amended to read as follows:
The judges of the Court of Appeals may appoint an admin
istrative director who shall hold office during the pleasure of
the court and perform the duties required of him by the court.
His salary shall be fixed by an order of the Court of Appeals
and paid monthly out of the Treasury. While holding his posi
tion he shall not practice law in any court of this Common
wealth. The administrative director shall perform the services
of administrative officer of the court, and as such he shall, under
the direction of the Court of Appeals, ascertain the necessity for
assignment of special circuit judges, make reports concerning
the performance of duties by special circuit judges, certify
the compensation and expenses payable to special circuit judges,
and generally perform such services as the Court of Appeals
may require in the supervision of the state judicial system. The
Court of Appeals may make appropriate orders to facilitate the
performance of his duties.
Section 2. KRS 21.155 is repealed.
Approved March 22, 1954

CHAPTER 242
(H. B. 487)
AN ACT to repeal, amend and re-enact Section 176.020 of the Kentucky
Revised Statutes, 1953 Edition, relating to the Department of High
ways.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
That Section 176.020 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes,
1953 Edition, be repealed, amended and re-enacted so that
when re-enacted said Section shall read as follows:
(1) The Department of Highways shall be headed by a
Commisioner of Highways who shall have sole charge of the
administration of the department. With prior approval of the
Governor in writing, the Commissioner of Highways shall ap
point as State Highway Engineer, a civil engineer who has had
at least six years executive engineering experience in the design,
supervision and construction of highways. He shall be the
technical advisor to the Commissioner of Highways, and under
the direction of the commissioner, he shall supervise all construc
tion and maintenance work of the department and perform such
other duties as are assigned to him by the commissioner.
(2) The department shall be divided into such divisions as
the commissioner may deem necessary to perform the duties and
functions of the department. Each division shall perform the
duties assigned it by the Commissioner of Highways and shall be
headed by a director who shall act under the direction and
supervision of the Commissioner of Highways.
(3) All officers, assistants and employes of the department
shall be paid their necessary traveling expenses while engaged
in the discharge of their official and departmental duties.
Approved March 22, 1954

CHAPTER 243
(H. B. 490)
AN ACT relating to condemnation by the Department of Highways,
and declaring an emergency. This Bill shall be known as the Jack
Trimble Act.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 177.084, Kentucky Revised Statutes,
is amended to read as follows:
Upon the application of the petitioner, and the filing of any
necessary affidavits, the clerk of the court shall issue process
against the owner to show cause why the petitioner does not have
the right to condemn the lands, or the use and occupation
thereof, and material sought to be condemned. The summons
shall contain a statement of the amount of the award. The
clerk shall make such orders as to non-residents and persons
under disability as are required by the statutes and Rules of
Civil Procedure in actions against them in circuit court, except
that any warning order attorney shall make his report at the
first regular term of county court 20 days after appointment,
and the case may proceed accordingly.
Section 2. Subsection (1) of Section 177.087, Kentucky
Revised Statutes, is amended to read as follows:
(1) Within thirty days from the date of a judgment au
thorizing the petitioner to take possession of the land or ma
terial, an appeal may be taken to the circuit court by either or
both parties by filing with the clerk of the circuit court a cer
tified copy of the judgment, a statement of the parties to the
appeal, and any exceptions the appellant has to the award made
by the commissioner. No transcript shall be required to be filed
by the appellant of other parts of the record, but when an ap
peal is filed, the clerk of the circuit court shall certify that fact
to the clerk of the county court and the latter immediately
shall transfer the original papers to the clerk of the circuit court.
The statement of grounds of appeal of either party may be
amended as provided in the Rules of Civil Procedure for the
amendment of a pleading. The appeal shall be tried de novo, but
shall be limited to the questions which are raised in the original
exceptions or statement of the grounds of appeal, or as amended;

Chapter 245 657
CHAPTER 245
(H. B. 492)
AN ACT relating to the State Investment Commission, amending KRS
41.380 and repealing KRS 41.295.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 41.380 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes
is amended to read as follows:
(1) There shall be a State Investment Commission com
posed of the Governor, the State Treasurer, and the Commissioner
of Finance. The commission shall have authority to and may
invest any and all surplus funds of the state in direct obligations
of the United States government, or of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky. All such investments shall be obligations that will
mature within five years from the date of investment.
(2) The commission shall also have authority and may,
whenever in its opinion the cash in the State Treasury is in excess
of the amount required to meet current expenditures, invest any
and all of said excess cash in United States Treasury bills or
certificates of indebtedness or any other United States govern
ment security which will mature within one year from the date
of investment.
(3) Interest earned from investments made pursuant to
this Section shall accrue to the credit of the General Expenditure
Fund, except that interest from investments of excess cash in
the Road Fund shall be credited to the Road Fund and interest
earned from the investment of funds accumulated solely by
means of contributions and gifts shall not be diverted to any
purpose other than that stipulated by the donor, when such
donor shall have designated the use to which such interest shall
be placed.
(4) The authority granted by this Section to the State
Investment Commission shall not extend to any funds that are
specifically provided by law to be invested by some other officer
or agency of the state government.
Section 2. Section 41.295 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is repealed.
Approved March 22, 1954
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 678

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the State Investment Commission, amending KRS 41.380 and repealing KRS 41.295.

It specifies investment maturity limits and how earned interest should be credited. KRS 41.295 is repealed.

has become increasingly important as a result of technological
advances, agricultural production problems and varied industrial,
municipal and recreational uses. Excessive rainfall at certain
seasons causes damages from overflowing streams. Prolonged
droughts at other seasons curtail industrial, municipal, agri
cultural and recreational uses of water and threaten the economic
well-being of the Commonwealth. The advancement of the
safety, happiness and the protection of property of the
people require that the power inherent in the people be utilized
to promote and to regulate the conservation, development and
proper use of the water resources. It is hereby declared that
the general welfare requires that the water resources of the
Commonwealth be put to beneficial use to the fullest extent
of which they are capable, and that the waste or non-beneficial
use of water be prevented, and that the conservation and
beneficial use of water be exercised in the interest of the
people. The proper investment of public and private funds
to promote the conservation and beneficial use of water re
sources is recognized as being in the public interest and such
investments shall be encouraged.
Section 2. Water occurring in any natural stream,
natural lake or other natural water body in the Commonwealth
which may be applied to any useful and beneficial purpose
is hereby declared to be a natural resource and public water
of the Commonwealth and subject to control and regulation
for the public welfare. Diffused surface water which flows
vagrantly over the surface of the ground shall not be regarded
as public water, and the owner of the land on which such
water falls or flows shall have the right to its use. Water left
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 680

classification.title: AN ACT relating to the conservation, development and use of water resources.
classification.summary: This act declares water in natural streams, lakes, and other natural water bodies in Kentucky to be a natural resource and public water, subject to control and regulation for the public welfare. It emphasizes the importance of conserving, developing, and properly using water resources for the economic well-being and safety of the Commonwealth. Diffused surface water is excluded from this public water classification.
classification.reasoning: The statute concerns the conservation and use of water resources in Kentucky. It does not contain any language, explicit or implicit, that suggests racial discrimination or segregation. The focus is solely on the management of water as a public resource for the general welfare. Therefore, it is not a Jim Crow law.

entries.ocr_text:
660 Acts of the General Assembly
standing in natural pools in a natural stream when the natural
flow of the stream has ceased shall not be regarded as public
water and the owners of the land contiguous to that water
shall have the right to its use for their purposes.
Section 3. (1) The owner of land contiguous to public
water shall at all times have the right to the use of water
therefrom in the quantity necessary to satisfy his needs for
domestic purposes, which shall include water for household
purposes, drinking water for livestock, poultry and domestic
animals. The use of water for such domestic purposes shall
have priority and be superior to any and all other uses.
(2) The owner of land contiguous to public water shall
have the right to such reasonable use of this water for other
than domestic purposes as will not deny the use of such water
to other owners for domestic purposes, or impair existing uses
of other owners heretofore established, or unreasonably interfere
with a beneficial use by other owners.
(3) An owner or group of owners of land contiguous to
public water shall have the right to impound and conserve
such water for their use by impounding such water behind a
dam in the natural stream bed or on their land or by pumping
such water from the stream or lake to a reservoir when the
flow in the stream or the level of the lake is in excess of
existing reasonable uses. An obstruction placed across a natural
stream shall provide an outlet for the release of water which
the owner is not entitled to use under this Act, and the owner
shall operate the outlet in accordance with this provision.
Section 4. The General Assembly recognizes that many
specific problems relating to water resources exist. To secure
additional information on this subject the Legislative Research
Commission is hereby directed to conduct a study of water
resources, usage and rights and report its finding to the General
Assembly at the 1956 session. All other agencies of the Common
wealth shall cooperate with the Legislative Research Commission
in making this study when requested by the commission to do so.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 248 661
CHAPTER 248
(H. B. 498)
AN ACT relating to motor vehicle headlights and amending Section
189.040 of the Kentucky Revised Statutes.
Be it enacted by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. Section 189.040 of the Kentucky Revised Stat
utes is hereby amended to read as follows:
1. Every motor vehicle other than a motorcycle or motor-
driven cycle shall be equipped with at least two head lamps
with at least one on each side of the front of the motor vehicle,
which head lamps shall comply with the requirements and
limitations set forth in this Section.
2. Every motorcycle and every motor-driven cycle shall
be equipped with at least one and not more than two head lamps
which shall comply with the requirements and limitations of
this Section.
3. Except as hereinafter provided, the head lamps or the
auxiliary driving lamps or the auxiliary passing lamp or combi
nations thereof on motor vehicles other than a motorcycle or
motor-driven cycle shall be so arranged that the driver may
control the selection between distribution of light projected to
different elevations, subject to the following requirements and
limitations:
(a) There shall be an uppermost distribution of light, or
composite beam, so aimed and of such intensity as to reveal
persons and vehicles at a distance of at least 350 feet ahead for
all conditions of loading.
(b) There shall be a lowermost distribution of light, or
composite beam so aimed and of sufficient intensity to reveal
persons and vehicles at a distance of at least 100 feet ahead;
and on a straight level road under any condition of loading none
of the high-intensity portion of the beam shall be directed to
strike the eyes of an approaching driver.
(c) Every new motor vehicle, other than a motorcycle or
motor-driven cycle, registered in this state after January 1, 1956,
which has multiple-beam road-lighting equipment shall be
equipped with a beam indicator, which shall be lighted whenever
the uppermost distribution of light from the head lamps is in

amended to read as follows:
(1) On or before January 1 of each year, and as a con
dition precedent to the use of the highways, every motor carrier
primarily engaged in transporting passengers for hire shall pay
to the Department of Motor Transportation for the privilege of
using the highways an annual tax upon each motor vehicle so
used, as follows: Five dollars for each regular seat for the first

one; except that taxicabs, city buses, industrial buses, suburban
buses, and U-Drive-Its, shall pay the fees set forth in subsection
(3) of this Section.
(2) Every motor carrier primarily engaged in transport
ing passengers for hire shall pay to the Department of Motor
Transportation for the privilege of using the highways, a mile
age tax as follows: one-thirty-sixth of a cent for each regular
seat in the motor vehicle multiplied by the total number of
miles traveled over which passengers are transported for hire,
except that taxicabs, city buses, industrial buses, suburban buses,
and U-Drive-Its are not required to pay the mileage tax imposed
by this Section.

U-Drive-Its, shall pay a license fee based upon seating capacity

entries.ocr_text:
Chapter 251 667
CHAPTER 251
(S. R. 10)
A JOINT RESOLUTION naming Jesse Hilton Stuart and Edwin Carlile
Litsey as Poets Laureate of Kentucky.
WHEREAS, the death of the Poet Laureate, the beloved J. T. Cotton
Noe, has left Kentucky in need of one who truly represents its great
spirit and traditions, and
WHEREAS, the people of Kentucky desire to honor their men and
women who have achieved distinction in any calling or profession, and
WHEREAS, there now resides within Kentucky two native sons,
Jesse Hilton Stuart and Edwin Carlile Litsey, who are known and recog
nized throughout the nation as writers and poets of eminent distinction,
and
WHEREAS, Jesse Hilton Stuart has written many short stories,
novels such as "Taps for Private Tussie" and "The Good Spirit of Laurel
Ridge", and the volumes of poetry, "Man With A Bull-Tongue Plow",
"Album of Destiny" and "Kentucky Is My Land", and
WHEREAS, Jesse Hilton Stuart was awarded the Guggenheim fel
lowship for European travel in 1937; the Jeanette Sewal Davis poetry
prize in 1934; a prize from the Academy of Arts and Sciences in 1941
and the Thomas Jefferson Southern Award in 1943, and
WHEREAS, Edwin Carlile Litsey, a resident of Lebanon, Marion
county, Kentucky has for more than sixty years been writing poetry
of such merit as to gain for him five prizes in nationwide poetry con
tests; and
WHEREAS, Edwin Carlile Litsey has achieved great distinction by
having twelve volumes of his work published, by having his work pub
lished in numerous magazines and broadcast over the radio; and
WHEREAS, Edwin Carlile Litsey is a Life Member of The Academic
Society of International History of Paris, France, of The Arts Club of
New York and of The Authors Club of London, England; and
WHEREAS, both Jesse Hilton Stuart and Edwin Carlile Litsey, in
their devoted and enthusiastic services to the Commonwealth, have
shown the greatest understanding and appreciation of Kentucky and
her people and have brought honor and glory to the Commonwealth of
Kentucky,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That Jesse Hilton Stuart and Edwin Carlile
Litsey are hereby declared to be the Poets Laureate of Kentucky
and each is entitled to enjoy all the honors incidental thereto.
This declaration of distinction, however, is honorary only and
bears no financial obligation upon the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 688

CHAPTER 254
(S. R. 17)
A JOINT RESOLUTION authorizing Margaret Ann Minix as adminis
tratrix of the estate of W. T. Stafford, Sr., to sue the Commonwealth
of Kentucky.
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
That Margaret Ann Minix as administratrix of the estate of
W. T. Stafford, Sr., be and she is hereby authorized to sue the
Commonwealth of Kentucky in the Lawrence Circuit Court for
damages resulting from the death of W. T. Stafford, Sr., on
September 7, 1953, caused by the falling of rock, dirt and other
debris against an automobile driven by Mitchell Preston and
in which the said W. T. Stafford, Sr., was a passenger or a guest,
on U. S. Highway No. 23, about one-half mile south of Shannons
Branch, in Lawrence county, Kentucky, and caused as the said
administratrix contends by the alleged negligence and careless
ness of the Department of Highways of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky, its agents, servants and employees in failing to keep
said highway in a proper condition for the traveling public.
Said suit shall be for a sum not in excess of ten thousand
dollars ($10,000.00).
The Attorney General of the Commonwealth of Kentucky
may investigate this claim against the Commonwealth and settle
it before or after suit is filed if he deems it advisable. Any amount
recovered in said suit or by settlement with the Attorney Gen
eral shall be paid out of the Road Fund upon a warrant drawn
by the Commissioner of Finance upon the State Treasury.
In the event suit is filed and judgment rendered by the cir
cuit court either party may appeal to the Court of Appeals of
Kentucky.
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 690

bert Kelly, to sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky and the State

fered serious and disabling injuries by reason of the occurrence of a
fire in the grandstand at the State Fairgrounds in Louisville and, as a
result of those injuries her husband, Herbert Kelly, has lost her com
panionship and services and has been required to expend large sums
for medical, hospital and nursing care for his wife; and
WHEREAS, there is considerable doubt as to whether the aforesaid
fire and the injuries sustained by Nannie Mae Kelly as a consequence
thereof were caused, brought about or contributed to by the joint and
concurring negligence of the State Fair Board and Fairgrounds Speed
way-, Inc., the concern which had previously been in possesion of the
said grandstand under a lease from the State Fair Board, or whether
the same were caused, brought about or contributed to solely by reason
of the independent negligence of one or the other of them; and
WHEREAS, justice requires that the above question be litigated in
one forum, which is impossible under existing law because Fairgrounds
Speedway, Inc. cannot be made a party to the proceedings instituted by
Nannie Mae Kelly and Herbert Kelly before the Kentucky Board of
Claims and the State Fair Board can obtain a dismissal of the suit in
stituted by them against it in the Jefferson Circuit Court; and

WHEREAS, justice further requires that Nannie Mae Kelly and
Herbert Kelly be adequately compensated for their injuries in the event

they have suffered, which cannot be accomplished under existing law
by reason of the monetary limit placed upon the jurisdiction of the

NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section I. That Nannie Mae Kelly be and she hereby is
authorized and permitted to sue the Commonwealth of Kentucky
and the State Fair Board, or either of them, in the circuit court
of Jefferson county, Kentucky in such sum as will fairly and
reasonably compensate her, but not to exceed the sum of
$10,000.00, for such injuries and damages as she may have sus
tained as a direct and proximate result of the negligence, if any,
of the State Fair Board and the Commonwealth of Kentucky,
through their agents and employees in causing, bringing about
or contributing to the occurrence of a fire and the conditions
created thereby on or about September 12, 1952, in the grand
stand on the State Fairgrounds in Louisville.
Section II. That Herbert Kelly, husband of Nannie Mae
Kelly, be and he hereby is authorized and permitted to sue the
Commonwealth of Kentucky and the State Fair Board, or either
of them, in the circuit court of Jefferson county, Kentucky, in
such sum as will fairly and reasonably compensate him, but
not to exceed the sum of $5,000.00, for the loss of society, com
panionship and services of his wife and the amounts expended
by him for medical, hospital and nursing care for her as a
consequence of the injuries sustained by her on the occasion
and under the circumstances set forth in Section I hereof.
Section III. That the statute of limitations shall not be
pleaded by the Commonwealth or the State Fair Board as a
defense to any suit brought pursuant to Sections I or II hereof if

of this Act with the Clerk of the Jefferson Circuit Court in the
action previously instituted therein by Nannie Mae Kelly and

678 Acts of the General Assembly
WHEREAS, on May 5, 1948, Carl William Riedling became an em
ployee of the Commonwealth of Kentucky as a guard at the Kentucky
State Penitentiary at Eddyville, Kentucky, and
WHEREAS, the said Carl William Riedling on July 26, 1950, while
regularly engaged in his duties as a guard of the prisoners at the Ken
tucky State Penitentiary at Eddyville, and while he was assigned to his
guard post in the wall tower of the penitentiary, was violently attacked
and injured by a number of prisoners who were attempting at the time
to escape from the said penitentiary as hereinafter stated:
On said date of July 26, 1950, the said Carl William Riedling noticed
that a group of prisoners was attempting to escape. He raised his fire
arm to fire at the escaping prisoners. While the said Carl William
Riedling was firing a shot at the prisoners, several of them threw incen
diary bombs at him which bombs had been crudely made within the
penitentiary. As a result of these bombs being thrown at the said Carl
William Riedling, his body and clothing burst into flames as well as did
the objects within the wall tower guard post. Notwithstanding the fact
that the said Carl William Riedling was in flames, he continued to fire
his revolver at the escaping prisoners. At the same time his clothes,
skin and hair were on fire and the heat in the wall tower was unbear
able. By firing at the escaping prisoners the said Carl William Riedling
stopped their escape. After having stopped the escape of the prisoners,
the said Carl William Riedling patted out the fire about his face and
body, and climbed outside the prison wall and took cover behind a tree
for the purpose of stopping any escaping prisoners who came over the
wall, the said Carl William Riedling having his revolver with him at
the time and ready to fire at the prisoners, all this notwithstanding the
great injury he had received as a result of his burns.
As a result of the aforesaid attack upon him, Carl William Riedling
received severe burns about his face and body which permanently scarred
and disfigured him, and he suffered great pain and mental anguish as
a result of the aforesaid burns. He further suffered large damages in
losing much time at his work and in being caused to incur large medical
and hospitalization expenses, having been confined to the hospital for
a period of approximately 62 days as a result of his aforesaid injuries.
WHEREAS, the said Carl William Riedling was not furnished any
hospitalization by the Commonwealth of Kentucky or any of its agencies,
or any other compensation by the Commonwealth of Kentucky or any
of its agencies, for any of his damages suffered as aforesaid, and
WHEREAS, the Commonwealth of Kentucky does not have any
workmen's compensation from which the said Carl William Riedling
may receive benefits, and he has not been paid anything for the loss of
his wages or permanent disfigurement, and
WHEREAS, great damages have resulted to him and to his family
all of which was brought about as a result of injuries which he sustained
in rendering heroic and public service to the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky as its employee, and
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 699

The act acknowledges that he received no hospitalization or compensation from the Commonwealth and that Kentucky had no workmen's compensation system at the time to cover his damages, including medical expenses, lost wages, and permanent disfigurement.

680 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 260
(S. R. 28)
A JOINT RESOLUTION changing the name of Levisa Fork of the Big
Sandy River to Big Sandy River.
WHEREAS, the fork of the Big Sandy River below the city of
Louisa in Lawrence county is officially designated as Levisa Fork; and
WHEREAS, said fork is commonly known and referred to as the
Big Sandy River; and
WHEREAS, it is to the best interest of the Commonwealth that its
rivers be easily and properly identified,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
That the body of water heretofore identified as the Levisa
Fork of the Big Sandy River be, and it is, hereby officially
designated as, and shall henceforth be known as, the Big Sandy
River.
Approved March 22, 1954

WHEREAS, Thomas Milton Durham and W. W. Durham, his father

such an employee, and

or either, for such injuries and damage, if any, thereby arising

ham, through his next friend and/or guardian.

tucky may investigate the claims against the Commonwealth
herein and settle such claims, or either of them, before or after

Road Fund upon a warrant or warrants drawn by the Commis

CHAPTER 262
(S. R. 37)
A JOINT RESOLUTION authorizing and directing payment of a claim
of the Kentucky Stone Company.
WHEREAS, in December, 1950, the Kentucky Stone Company, of
Louisville, Kentucky, furnished certain materials in the amount of
$343.70 to the Kentucky Department of Highways; and
WHEREAS, through inadvertence, this account has never been
paid,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Department of Highways is authorized
and directed to pay to the Kentucky Stone Company, of Louis
ville, Kentucky, out of any funds at the disposal of the depart
ment, the sum of $343.70, for materials furnished.
Section 2. The statute of limitations as now provided by
law shall not be a bar to the payment of the claim authorized
herein.
Became law without Governor's signature March 31, 1954

684 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 264
(S. R. 41)
A JOINT RESOLUTION providing for erection of a marker at the
grave of Matthew Lyon, for the construction of a state park around
and about his burial place, and making the burial place of Matthew
Lyon a state shrine.
WHEREAS, Matthew Lyon (1750-1822) was a distinguished patriot,
with a notable record of service as a Revolutionary War soldier, as a
member of Ethan Allen's famous military organization, Green Mountain
Boys; and
WHEREAS, he was closely associated with a number of patriotic
leaders in the early days of the republic, and was connected by mar
riage with three prominent families of those days, his first wife having
been a niece of the famed Ethan Allen, and his second wife a daughter
of Thomas Chittenden, the first Governor of Vermont; and
WHEREAS, he was a leader in the struggle for the establishment
and maintenance of democratic principles and practices, opposing mon-
archial customs and tendencies, and especially distinguished himself by
his militant opposition to the Alien and Sedition Laws, as a result of
which he suffered imprisonment; and
WHEREAS, leaving prison a hero in the eyes of his fellow citizens
of Vermont because of his able and valiant championship of the rights
and liberties of the people, he became a member of the lower house of
Congress, where he served four years; and
WHEREAS, while a member of Congress he cast the deciding vote
in the historic contest for the presidency of the United States between
Thomas Jefferson and Aaron Burr, resulting in the election of his friend
and associate, Jefferson; and
WHEREAS, in the year 1799, his restless and pioneering spirit led
him to seek out a new location in the unsettled area of western Ken
tucky, and he, with a few fellow emigrants, founded the town of Eddy-
ville; and
WHEREAS, he resided at Eddyville for nineteen years, operating
ship-yards, iron mills, a paper mill, a grist mill, saw mills and a tannery,
making the town of Eddyville a commercial center and one of the two
or three most important points in western Kentucky in the early 1800's,
a position maintained for half a century; and
WHEREAS, he pioneered, in Vermont, in manufacturing paper from
wood pulp, and has been recognized for his discovery or invention of
this process and application of same as worthy of consideration along
with Benjamin Franklin and Eli Whitney and other early American
inventors; and
WHEREAS, while a resident of Kentucky, he served eight years as
a member of the lower house of Congress from his district; and
WHEREAS, upon being appointed by President James Monroe as
Factor to the Cherokee Indian Nation in the Territory of Arkansas, he
removed to that Territory and resided there until his death; and
WHEREAS, it has been written of him—
"Matthew Lyon died at Spadra Bluff on the 1st of August, 1822,
lamented by the whole American people; a man of action and deeds
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 705

entries.ocr_text:
Chapter 264 685
which left their impress on his times; a patriot in every fibre, whose
vote made a President; a pioneer along whose pathway Romance
walked side by side with History; a hero whose memory is cherished
in Kentucky and Vermont among the foremost and bravest of their
sons"; and
WHEREAS, the remains of Matthew Lyon were re-interred at
Eddyville eleven years after his death, as narrated by an associate as
follows:
"The remains of Matthew Lyon were interred at Spadra Bluff, but
in the year 1833, his son, Chittenden Lyon, and other members of Col.
Lyon's family resolved to transfer the body to Kentucky, and re-inter it
at Eddyville. This was accordingly done, and the ceremonies of the
reburial drew together an immense assemblage of the friends of Mat
thew Lyon. A handsome monument was erected over the grave, with
suitable inscriptions commemorative of the founder of Eddyville. Old
men still living remember the impressive services, and speak of the
bringing home of 'bell and burial' for the beloved founder, as the most
solemn event in the history of the town"; and
WHEREAS, the remains of Matthew Lyon lie in Riverview Ceme
tery in Eddyville by the side of those of his daughter, Lorraine Lyon,
who was the first person to be buried in said cemetery, and also in close
proximity to the graves of two of his sons, viz., Chittenden Lyon and
Matthew Lyon, Jr., the former of whom served his district in the lower
house of Congress, and for whom the county of Lyon was named, and
also to the graves of others of the old patriot's descendants, among them
his grandson, Gen. Hylan B. Lyon,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
That in tribute to the memory of Matthew Lyon, the State
Historical Society is hereby authorized and directed to cause
to be erected at his grave a suitable and appropriate marker,
and to refurbish his grave and the stone now marking it and
also the graves and marking stones of his immediate family
descendants; and that the Department of Welfare with the
use of prison labor cause to be laid out around and about said
graves a park; and that the State Department of Highways be,
and it is hereby requested, to suitably and appropriately im
prove the road, about six-tenths of a mile in length, by which
said cemetery is reached, so as to render access easy and con
venient for the one combined purpose of making said part of said
cemetery a shrine for the interest and gratification of all patriotic
citizens of Kentucky and the United States.
Approved March 22, 1954

686 Acts of the General Assembly
CHAPTER 265
(S. R. 42)
A JOINT RESOLUTION providing for an investigation, study and sur
vey by the Legislative Research Commission regarding the laws of
Kentucky with respect to control of diseased livestock poultry and
related matters.
WHEREAS, many livestock and poultry diseases constitute a serious
threat both to the industries involved and also to the health of the
people of the Commonwealth; and
WHEREAS, it appears that certain of the laws now on the statute
books are adequate to institute a program of control of such diseases,
but are impossible of enforcement or implementation due to lack of
funds; and
WHEREAS, it is possible that such a program might be financed
through special taxes levied on the sale of all livestock at stockyards;
and
WHEREAS, it appears that in some instances farmers of this Com
monwealth have not only had to bear the economic losses arising from
disease in their flocks or herds, but have also been unable to take ad
vantage of certain favorable provisions of the Federal Internal Revenue
Code due to the lack of an active program of livestock condemnation in
this state,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Legislative Research Commission, in
cooperation with the Commissioner of Agriculture, Labor and
Statistics, the College of Agriculture of the University of Ken
tucky, the Commissioner of Health, and the members of any
special citizens committee which the Governor may see fit to
appoint for that purpose, is authorized and directed to make a
careful and impartial study of the laws of this Commonwealth
pertaining to control of livestock and poultry diseases, with par
ticular respect to the various problems suggested in the preamble
of this joint resolution.
Section 2. That the Legislative Research Commission shall
report to the next regular session of the General Assembly its
findings and recommendations with respect to such matters as
it deems essential to its study, including the following:
(1) Whether or not it is desirable and feasible to establish
a program for condemnation of diseased livestock or poultry by

CHAPTER 267
(S. R. 51)
A JOINT RESOLUTION directing the Legislative Research Commission
to study the desirability of merging all independent school districts
with their respective county school districts.
WHEREAS, it has been suggested that merger of independent school
districts with their respective county school districts would be a step
forward in improvement of the public schools of the Commonwealth of
Kentucky; and
WHEREAS, it is necessary that information be gathered on which
the members of future sessions of the General Assembly can base their
opinion on this subject; and
WHEREAS, the Legislative Research Commission has the staff to
make a comprehensive study of such subject,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Legislative Research Commission be
and it is hereby directed to make a study of the desirability
of merging independent school districts with their respective
county school districts, and that the findings of such study be
reported to the 1956 session of the General Assembly of the
Commonwealth of Kentucky.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 268 689
CHAPTER 268
(S. R. 52)
A JOINT RESOLUTION providing for an investigation, study and sur
vey by the Legislative Research Commission, in cooperation with
the Judicial Council, of the Judicial Department of the Common
wealth of Kentucky and related matters.
WHEREAS, the present courts and judicial system of the Common
wealth of Kentucky were provided for by the present Constitution of
Kentucky as embodied in Sections 109-144 inclusive, adopted in 1891;
and
WHEREAS, no substantial change has been made in same since
said time; and
WHEREAS, litigation in the state has increased in volume and com
plexity; and
WHEREAS, the Court of Appeals has more cases to dispose of than
any other court of last resort in the United States; and
WHEREAS, many of such cases do not present any questions of
novel or sufficient importance to justify appellate review; and
WHEREAS, there are many cases disposed of or pending in the
Court of Appeals that could or should have been disposed of by review
in a court of lesser importance; and
WHEREAS, the dockets of many circuit courts are congested re
sulting in unreasonable delay in the disposition of litigation and in
undue cost to the litigants;
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Legislative Research Commission, in
cooperation, and by and with the advice and counsel of the
Judicial Council, the Kentucky State Bar Association, and the
members of any special citizens committee which the Governor
may see fit to appoint for that purpose, is authorized and di
rected to make a careful and impartial study of the Judicial
Department of the Commonwealth of Kentucky with particular
respect to the various problems suggested in the preamble of

CHAPTER 270
(H. R. 1)
A JOINT RESOLUTION providing for the purchase and distribution of
"The Legislative Record," and declaring an emergency.
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Dunne Press, Incorporated is authorized
and directed to furnish 200 copies of "The Legislative Record,"
each day the House or Senate is in session, for delivery and dis
tribution as provided in Section 2 of this resolution, for which
said company shall be paid the sum of $2,800 out of the ap
propriation for expenses of legislative sessions for the current
fiscal year made by Chapter 106 of the Acts of 1952. The De
partment of Finance shall issue its warrant for payment of this
amount upon receipt of a voucher for the same endorsed and ap
proved by the Speaker of the House of Representatives and the
President of the Senate.
Section 2. The distribution of "The Legislative Record"
shall be as follows:
(a) 110 copies to the Clerk of the House for distribution
to the members of the House and to such officers of the House
as the Speaker may direct.
(b) 45 copies to the Clerk of the Senate for distribution
to the members of the Senate and to such officers of the Senate
as the President of the Senate mav direct.
(c) 45 copies to the Governor and to such officers and em
ployes of the Commonwealth as the Governor, the President of
the Senate and the Speaker of the House may direct.
Section 3. Whereas, in order that the functions of the
General Assembly may be expedited, it is essential that "The
Legislative Record" be available to the members during the pres
ent session, an emergency is declared to exist and this resolution
shall take effect upon its passage and approval by the Governor.
Approved January 15, 1954

WHEREAS, it appears that such a study will provide factual in
formation leading to future changes in the legislative process which will
make that process more democratic, and
WHEREAS, the Legislative Research Commission has the personnel
and facilities to make such a research study, and to make a comprehen
sive report based thereon,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
That the Legislative Research Commission be, and it is
hereby authorized and directed to make a comprehensive re
search study of the legislative process in the Commonwealth of
Kentucky; .
That in the study of this subject, particular attention be
given to the improvement of the legislative process to make it
more democratic, either through a change in the length and
frequency of sessions of the General Assembly or through im
proved legislative procedure, or both;
That the study contemplated by this resolution be com
pleted by the Legislative Research Commission not later than
December 1, 1955, and a report of the findings be published and
distributed to the members of the 1956 General Assembly.
Approved March 22, 1954
