CHAPTER 278
(H. R. 27)
A RESOLUTION concerning the naming of Kentucky Highway No. 503
in honor of Corporal Ernest E. West.
WHEREAS, Ernest E. West, a resident of the Wurtland community
in Greenup county, while serving as a Corporal in the army of the
United States during the late hostilities in Korea, did distinguish him
self by conspicuous gallantry above and beyond the call of duty in
voluntarily accompanying a patrol directed to locate and destroy an
enemy outpost of Communist forces near Sataeri, Korea, on October 12,
1952; and
WHEREAS, in nearing the objective, the patrol was ambushed and
sustained numerous casualties, including the leader thereof, who, though
wounded and lying in an exposed position, ordered his troops to with
draw; and
WHEREAS, Corporal West then braved intense enemy fire to reach
and assist his leader, protecting his officer with his own body while
under attack by three enemy soldiers employing hand grenades and
small arms fire; and
WHEREAS, Corporal West, though blinded in one eye and other
wise critically wounded, was able to carry his officer and two other
comrades to safety in two separate trips, killing all of his assailants; and
WHEREAS, Corporal West received the thanks of the United States
on January 12, 1954, when he was presented with the Congressional
Medal of Honor at the White House by President Eisenhower; and
WHEREAS, it is the desire of his home state to honor his courage
and devotion; and
WHEREAS, in the words of the late Senator and Commissioner of
Highways, Garrett Withers, the most important road in existence is
always the road to a man's own home,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the House of Representatives of the Com
monwealth of Kentucky, the Senate concurring therein:
(1) That Kentucky Highway No. 503, extending from
Wurtland, at its intersection with U.S. 23, to Ky. 207, be, and
it is hereby, designated the Corporal Ernest E. West Highway,
in honor of Corporal West who resides thereon; and
(2) That all governmental agencies, both Federal and
state, and all private agencies and other persons having occasion
to refer to that highway, be requested to use the name "Corporal
Ernest E. West Highway"; and
(3) That the Clerk of the House of Representatives is
directed to forward copies of this resolution to Corporal Ernest E.
West, and to the county court clerk of Greenup county for
filing in the records of that county.
Approved March 22, 1954

search Commission to make, or to have made, studies of the State

mendations concerning revision of the State General Fund tax
structure, and making an appropriation.
WHEREAS, in order that the state government may function on an
economically sound basis, and in accordance with sound principles of
fiscal management, the tax needs, requirements and responsibilities of
the state government should be ascertained and established pursuant to
a comprehensive and coordinate plan taking into consideration the
proper relationship between Federal, state and local government, and
the obligations of each; and
WHEREAS, the trend of expansion of the scope of state government,
and the increasing demands for benefits and services through state gov
ernment, have created the need for additional revenue through extended
and increased taxation; and
WHEREAS, the complexities of present day economic activity make
exceedingly difficult the selection and adoption of fair and equitable tax
measures producing sufficient revenues to meet the needs of the state;
and
WHEREAS, it is desirable that a re-examination of the existing
state General Fund tax structure and a study and survey of potential
tax sources be made in order that the General Assembly may be fully
advised in the consideration of an integrated program of revenues and
expenditures of the state government,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Legislative Research Commission shall
make, or shall cause to have made, comprehensive studies of the
State General Fund tax structure and of potential tax sources,
including analyses of administration and the economic effects
of present State General Fund taxes and the revenue derived
therefrom, a comparison of the tax sources existing in this state
with those existing in other states, and a survey of prospective
sources of revenue with an analysis of the economic effect of
the utilization thereof and an estimate of the administrative
cost and the revenue that may be produced thereby. The studies
shall be presented so as to enable the Governor and the General
Assembly to be informed as to the most feasible, equitable and
economically sound program of taxation for the Commonwealth.

CHAPTER 283
(H. R. 51)
A JOINT RESOLUTION directing the Legislative Research Commission
to study the desirability of exempting timberland from existing
taxation and imposing in lieu thereof, a severance tax on each tree,
and to report its findings to the 1956 General Assembly.
WHEREAS, the taxes now imposed on timberland are inequitable
and inadequate; and
WHEREAS, a severance tax imposed on each tree would eliminate
many of the tax inequities and inadequacies now existing,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Legislative Research Commission is hereby
directed to study the desirability of exempting timberland from
all taxes as now imposed, and imposing in lieu thereof, a sever
ance tax on trees utilized for commercial purposes.
Section 2. The Legislative Research Commission shall re
port its findings and recommendations to the 1956 General
Assembly.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 284 705
CHAPTER 284
(H. R. 52)
A JOINT RESOLUTION directing the Legislative Research Commis
sion to study the feasability of a state financed program of financial
assistance to deserving college students.
WHEREAS, Kentucky ranks low among the states in education gen
erally, and ranks forty-seventh with regard to the percentage of college
trained persons in the adult population; and
WHEREAS, an educated populace depends upon the development of
good schools, and good schools depend upon well trained teachers; and
WHEREAS, the shortage of well trained teachers in the Common
wealth is so critical that approximately three thousand persons are now
employed to teach in the schools who do not meet the minimum re
quirements for the issuance of a teachers certificate; and
WHEREAS, there is an acute need in the Commonwealth for col
lege trained men and women in agriculture, commerce, labor, industry,
and the professions; and
WHEREAS, economic circumstances often prevent capable high-
school graduates from attending college; and
WHEREAS, financial consideration should not be allowed to prevent
deserving young men and women of this Commonwealth from acquiring
a college education; and
WHEREAS, more than three-fourths of the states of the Union have
programs which provide state aid through scholarships or loans to de
serving college students,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Legislative Research Commission be
and it is directed to make a study of the feasability of inaugurat
ing a state financed program of financial assistance to deserving
college students, including the need for such a program in the
Commonwealth and an analysis of similar programs in other
states.
Section 2. That the Legislative Research Commission ap
point a citizens advisory committee to aid in defining the scope
of the study and to make recommendations regarding a suitable
program of state aid to college students.
Section 3. That the Legislative Research Commission re
port its findings to the 1956 session of the General Assembly.
Approved March 22, 1954
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 726

CHAPTER 285
(H. R. 53)
A JOINT RESOLUTION providing for an investigation, study and sur
vey by the Legislative Research Commission of milk prices in Ken
tucky.
WHEREAS, the dairy industry is an essential element in the econ
omy of the Commonwealth; and
WHEREAS, it appears that a great difference exists between the
price paid farmers for raw milk and the price charged consumers; and
WHEREAS, it appears that a thorough examination of the price
structure of the milk industry and the expenses entailed in milk produc
tion and distribution is desirable,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The Legislative Research Commission is hereby
authorized and directed to make a careful and impartial study of
milk prices and an analysis of the expenses entailed in milk pro
duction and distribution in the Commonwealth.
Section 2. The Legislative Research Commission shall sub
mit to the General Assembly, as soon as practicable but no later
than December 1, 1955, a report embodying the results and con
clusions of the study hereby authorized and directed.
Section 3. The Legislative Research Commission in effec
tuating this resolution shall have the cooperation and assistance
of the University of Kentucky College of Agriculture, the Agri
cultural Experiment Station, the Commissioner of Agriculture,
Labor and Statistics and such other agencies, officers and con
sultants as it may deem necessary.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 286 707
eminent of the United States of the "Pioneer National Monument," to
consist of the Blue Licks Battlefield, Fort Boonesborough, Boone's Sta
tion and Bryan's Station, in the State of Kentucky, when title thereto
shall have been vested in the United States, and that said "Pioneer
National Monument" should be administered by the National Park
Service; and
WHEREAS, by joint resolution Chapter 177, Acts 1944, provision
was made for the conveyance by the Commonwealth to the national
government of the United States to title to the Blue Licks Battlefield
State Park tract of land; and
WHEREAS, the sites of Fort Boonesborough, Boone's Station, and
Bryan's Station are presently in private ownership; and
WHEREAS, all of the above mentioned sites are not merely of local
and statewide significance, but also of national significance, inasmuch as
the Battle of Blue Licks was the last battle of the American Revolution;
Fort Boonesborough is the site of the first white settlement west of the
Appalachian Mountains; Boone's Station was built by Daniel Boone and
for a number of years was the home and headquarters of himself, his
family and connections; and Bryan's Station is the site of a famous
Indian siege in which the pioneer women of the station displayed great
heroism,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky in regular session assembled, the Senate
and House concurring, memorializes the 83rd Congress of the
United States to appropriate sufficient funds to complete the
acquisition of the nationally historic sites of the Battle of
Blue Licks, Fort Boonesborough, Boone's Station and Bryan's
Station; in Kentucky, and to consummate the project to place
these sites under the National Park Service as a permanent
"Pioneer National Monument."
Section 2. That the Senators and Representatives of Ken
tucky in the 83rd Congress are urged to introduce and support
legislation to accomplish the foregoing.
Section 3. That copies of this memorial, duly authenti
cated, be sent by the Clerk of the Senate to the President and
Chief Clerk of the Senate of the United States, the United
States Senators from Kentucky, the Speaker and Chief Clerk
of the House of Representatives of the United States, and the
Representatives in Congress from Kentucky.
Approved March 22, 1954

It highlights the national significance of these sites, including their roles in the American Revolution and early westward expansion.

WHEREAS, Mrs. Ethel Gist Cantrill has served Kentucky long and
faithfully as State Librarian and as Director of the Department of Li
brary and Archives; and
WHEREAS, Mrs. Cantrill was appointed as State Librarian in 1924
and reappointed in 1928 and 1932; and
WHEREAS, she was designated Director of the Department of Li
brary and Archives in 1948 and reappointed in 1952; and
WHEREAS, on June 30, 1954, Mrs. Cantrill will have completed over
twenty-four years of diligent and conscientious service to the members
of the General Assembly and the citizens of Kentucky; and
WHEREAS, it is the desire of the General Assembly to recognize
and honor Mrs. Cantrill,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
That Mrs. Ethel Gist Cantrill is hereby named State Li
brarian Emeritus for life, this provision to become effective on
July 1, 1954.

Chapter 293 713
WHEREAS, experience in the marketing of tobacco under the pro
visions of said Act demonstrates that the existing penalty for marketing
tobacco in excess of its assigned quota is not a sufficient deterrent to
such marketing; and
WHEREAS, the marketing of tobacco in excess of its assigned quota
has a seriously damaging effect upon the stability of the tobacco in
dustry and upon the prosperity of all persons engaged in such industry,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. That the Congress of the United States be
urged to enact appropriate legislation amending the Agricul
tural Adjustment Act of 1938, as amended, 7 U.S.C. 1314(a)
so as to increase the penalty for marketing tobacco in excess
of its assigned quota to sixty per centum of the average market
price for such tobacco for the immediately preceding market year.
Section 2. That the Secretary of State send copies of this
joint resolution to the Clerks of the Senate and House of Repre
sentatives of the Congress of the United States and to each
United States Senator and Representative from Kentucky.
Approved March 22, 1954

CHAPTER 294
(H. R. 84)
A JOINT RESOLUTION memorializing the U. S. Congrses to stress the
importance of the Tennessee Valley Authority, to make appropria
tions to continue and expand this program, and directing trans
mittal of copies of this resolution to U. S. Senators and Congress
men from Kentucky.
WHEREAS, there has been considerable speculation that the TVA
program will perhaps not be continued or expanded; and
WHEREAS, the REA program is vitally dependent upon the TVA
program; and
WHEREAS, the REA program was created largely for the benefit
of rural people; and
WHEREAS, the REA program has been an economic life saver to
the rural people of the Commonwealth by furnishing them with low cost
electricity; and
WHEREAS, if the TVA program were not continued and expanded
the REA program would suffer; and
WHEREAS, if the REA program suffered the rural people of the
Commonwealth would suffer,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the General Assembly of the Common
wealth of Kentucky:
Section 1. The U. S. Congress is hereby urged and
memorialized to stress the importance of the Tennessee Valley
Authority program and to make appropriations to continue and
expand this program.
Section 2. The Clerk of the House of the General As
sembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky shall transmit copies
of this resolution to all U. S. Senators and Congressmen from
Kentucky.
Approved March 22, 1954

Chapter 298 719
Julia Dodson to Dr. John Holifield, a physician and ex-soldier of
the Confederacy. He was educated in the common schools and
graduated in law from Cumberland University, in Tennessee,
where he was enrolled during the same period that Cordell Hull,
later Secretary of State of the United States, was a student. He
was admitted to the bar of this Commonwealth in 1896, and later
served terms as city attorney of Mayfield and county attorney of
Graves county.
The late Attorney General, J. W. Cammack, appointed him
an Assistant Attorney General in January, 1928, and he has since
served continuously in that position except for one term, 1932 to
1936, when he returned to Mayfield to rejoin his firm. He was
insistently recalled to the Attorney General's office in 1936 to
continue the railroad rate litigation which he had begun during
his first service in 1928. This involved the freight rate differen
tial between the North and the South which had so seriously
retarded and repressed the industrial growth of the South almost
from the period of the Northern triumph in the War for Southern
Independence.
The freight rate structures of the United States had devel
oped on a regional basis, with higher rates in the South and West
than in the North. For example, in the Supreme Court's decision
in New York v. United States, 331 U. S. 284, 67 S. Ct. 1207, a
comparative table is reproduced (at page 302 of the U. S. Re
porter) which shows a disadvantage to the Southern shipper of
37 to 50 percent in comparison with shipments of equal distance
in the North.
The attack on this and similar crippling differentials was
carried on by a number of Southern states and organizations,
with General Holifield always taking part as one of the leaders.
In numerous cases he represented the state before the Interstate
Commerce Commission, and in appeals from its decisions in the
Supreme Court of the United States, and elsewhere.
These cases include:
Commonwealth of Kentucky v. Anaphee <b W. R. Co., 213
I. C. C. 297, (original complaint 1928, decision 1935) involved
the rates from Kentucky to the large consuming areas in the
North. The Interstate Commerce Commission required the
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 740

The text mentions the "War for Southern Independence" and the "Northern triumph" as historical context for these economic disparities.

720 Acts of the General Assembly
railroads to reduce the rates from Kentucky to substantially the
same level as the rates in the North.
Intrastate Class and Commodity Rates in Kentucky, 223
I. C. C. 109, (decision, 1937) involved an order of the railroad
commission of Kentucky, which required the railroads to reduce
the intrastate rates to the level of the rates in the North. The
Interstate Commerce Commission sustained the order of the
railroad commission.
Alabama v. New York Central R. Co., 235 I. C. C. 255, (de
cision, 1939) known as the Southern Governors Case, involved
the rates on a number of important commodities moving from
the South to the North. The Interstate Commerce Commission
required a substantial reduction in the rates.
Class Rate Investigation, 1939, 262 I. C. C. 447; (decision,
1945) 281 I. C. C. 213, (decision, 1951) originated by the
Southern Governors, involved the class rates east of the Rocky
Mountains. The Interstate Commerce Commission found that
the interterritorial differences in rates were not justified and
required the railroads to establish uniform rates. The Supreme
Court sustained the order in New York v. United States, 331
U. S. 284.
Commonwealth of Kentucky v. United States, 325 U. S. 535,
involved an order of the Interstate Commerce Commission which
required an increase in the intrastate passenger fare. The court
set aside the order.
Lake Cargo Coal from Illinois, Ind., and Ky. to Chicago,
238, I. C. C. 633, (decision, 1940) involved, amongst others, a
differential of 35 cents per ton on coal West Kentucky over
Southern Illinois to Chicago for movement beyond by water.
The Interstate Commerce Commission reduced the differential
to 25 cents per ton.
Commonwealth of Kentucky v. Illinois C. R. Co., 259 I. C. C.
259, (decision, 1944) involved the differential of Western Ken
tucky over Southern Illinois of 35 cents per ton on coal to
Chicago proper. The Interstate Commerce Commission re
quired the railroads to reduce the differential to 25 cents per
ton. The order of the Commission was sustained by the court
in Chicago, B. ir Q. R. Co. v. United States, 60 Fed. Supp. 580.

classification.title: Acts of the General Assembly 1954
classification.summary: This excerpt from the 1954 Acts of the General Assembly of Kentucky discusses various legal cases and decisions related to railroad rates, primarily focusing on intrastate versus interstate rates and differentials between different regions. It references decisions from the Interstate Commerce Commission and the Supreme Court concerning rate reductions and adjustments for commodities like coal and general class rates. The cases cited include "Intrastate Class and Commodity Rates in Kentucky," "Alabama v. New York Central R. Co.," "Class Rate Investigation, 1939," "Commonwealth of Kentucky v. United States," "Lake Cargo Coal from Illinois, Ind., and Ky. to Chicago," and "Commonwealth of Kentucky v. Illinois C. R. Co."

entries.ocr_text:
Chapter 298 721
The latter two cases, especially, had an immediately bene
ficial effect on the Western Kentucky Coal Field.
Despite the fact that General Holifield was and is a Demo
crat, he was retained by Republican Attorney General Eldon
Dummit during the latter's term (1944-1948), and publicly com
mended by him "as deserving the credit" for the successful fight
to equalize rates.
General Holifield has served as First Assistant Attorney Gen
eral, and more recently, as Assistant Attorney General assigned
to the Kentucky Railroad Commission. He is considered as the
foremost authority on Kentucky's Constitution, and is also an
authority on Confederate history. He is best known to laymen
in Frankfort, however, as the teacher of a large men's Bible
class in the First Baptist Church of that city, and the author of
several religious monograms in the nature of legal briefs on re
ligious history or beliefs. These include "A Discussion of Some
of the Evidence of Christ's Resurrection" and "A Discussion of
Some of the Evidence of God's Existence and His Interest in
Humanity", both published in 1950.
General Holifield was married October 31, 1905 to Miss
Melinda May Drake, who died in 1938. They were childless.
In 1953 General Holifield was recognized and honored by the
University of Kentucky, which conferred upon him an LL.D.
degree.
AND WHEREAS, for these reasons, it is the desire of the General
Assembly to recognize and honor Marvin Bertrie Holifield,
and to make his achievements a matter of record,
NOW, THEREFORE,
Be it resolved by the Senate of the Commonwealth of Ken
tucky, the House of Representatives concurring:
Section 1. That the presiding officers of the Senate and
the House do express to Marvin Bertrie Holifield the apprecia
tion of the General Assembly for his labors on behalf of Ken
tucky and the South, congratulate him upon his anniversary, and
inscribe and present to him a copy of this resolution.
Section 2. That a copy of this resolution be sent to the
county court clerk of Graves county for inclusion in the records
of that county.
Adopted February 8, 1954
entries.citation: Acts of the General Assembly of the Commonwealth of Kentucky, 1954, p. 742
